      "id": "Anatomy 001",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.411Z",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "synonyms": [
        "shoulder raise",
        "arm lift"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "long_explanation": "The supraspinatus initiates the first 15 degrees of abduction, but the deltoid becomes the main mover from 15 to 90 degrees.",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_friction",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "muscle_trapezius"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Muscular System",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.067Z",
      "answers": [
        "Supraspinatus",
        "Deltoid",
        "Biceps brachii",
        "Trapezius"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "correct": 1,
      "variant": 1,
      "short_explanation": "The deltoid muscle abducts the shoulder beyond 15 degrees.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which muscle is primarily responsible for shoulder abduction from 15 to 90 degrees? [Variant 1]",
      "clinical_application": "Shoulder massage work may include friction to the deltoid insertion for ROM support.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_friction",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "muscle_trapezius"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy 002",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "subcategory": "Skeletal System",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "stats": {

        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "relevance_score": 4,
      "short_explanation": "The sacrum forms the posterior wall of the pelvic cavity.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "Palpation of the sacrum can guide assessment of pelvic alignment and SI joint dysfunction.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "exam_assessment",
        "joint_joint",
        "bone_sacrum",
        "bone_ilium",
        "bone_ischium",
        "bone_pubis"
      ],
      "synonyms": [
        "sacral bone",
        "pelvic base"
      ],
      "variant": 2,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "exam_assessment",
        "joint_joint",
        "bone_sacrum",
        "bone_ilium",
        "bone_ischium",
        "bone_pubis"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Ischium",
        "Pubis",
        "Sacrum",
        "Ilium"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.072Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.416Z",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which bone forms the posterior portion of the pelvis? [Variant 2]",
      "long_explanation": "The sacrum is a triangular bone formed by fused vertebrae that articulates with the ilium on both sides. It transmits weight from the spine to the pelvis.",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed."
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy 003",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_pain",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "muscle_gluteus_maximus",
        "muscle_iliopsoas",
        "muscle_rectus_femoris",
        "muscle_biceps_femoris",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Rectus femoris is part of the quadriceps and acts as a hip flexor.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists often stretch or release this muscle to address anterior pelvic tilt and lower back discomfort.",
      "question": "Which of the following muscles is a primary hip flexor? [Variant 3]",
      "relevance_score": 3,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "correct": 1,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "synonyms": [
        "rectus",
        "hip-lifting"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "Along with iliopsoas, rectus femoris helps lift the thigh toward the torso. It is biarticular, crossing both the hip and knee joints.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.072Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_pain",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "muscle_gluteus_maximus",
        "muscle_iliopsoas",
        "muscle_rectus_femoris",
        "muscle_biceps_femoris",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.416Z",
      "subcategory": "Muscular System",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "answers": [
        "Gluteus maximus",
        "Rectus femoris",
        "Biceps femoris",
        "Gastrocnemius"
      ],
      "variant": 3,
      "difficulty": "moderate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy 004",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.072Z",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "subcategory": "Nervous System",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "client_sensation",
        "muscle_orbicularis_oculi",
        "nerve_facial_nerve",
        "organ_tongue"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Trigeminal (CN V)",
        "Facial (CN VII)",
        "Accessory (CN XI)",
        "Vagus (CN X)"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Cranial nerve VII innervates muscles like the orbicularis oculi and zygomaticus. It also carries taste sensations from the anterior tongue.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "synonyms": [
        "VII",
        "facial nerve"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "source_reference": "Gray’s Anatomy, Clinical Edition",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "The facial nerve controls facial muscles for expression.",
      "variant": 4,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "client_sensation",
        "muscle_orbicularis_oculi",
        "nerve_facial_nerve",
        "organ_tongue"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "distractors": [],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which cranial nerve is primarily responsible for facial expression? [Variant 4]",
      "relevance_score": 2,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.416Z",
      "clinical_application": "In massage, therapists avoid overstimulation of the facial nerve in clients with facial nerve palsy or post-stroke symptoms.",
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy 005",
      "source_reference": "Anatomy Coloring Book; FSMTB Circulatory Review",
      "clinical_application": "Understanding blood flow paths helps therapists stay cautious around vulnerable vascular areas like the abdomen and carotid triangle.",

      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "What is the largest artery in the human body? [Variant 5]",
      "correct": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_artery",

      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "The aorta originates from the left ventricle and supplies oxygenated blood to all systemic arteries through its various branches.",

      "difficulty": "easy",

      "short_explanation": "The aorta is the body's largest artery and branches from the heart.",
      "variant": 5

    {
      "id": "Anatomy 006",
      "short_explanation": "The deltoid muscle abducts the shoulder beyond 15 degrees.",
      "question": "Which muscle is primarily responsible for shoulder abduction from 15 to 90 degrees? [Variant 6]",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.416Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "subcategory": "Muscular System",
      "clinical_application": "Shoulder massage work may include friction to the deltoid insertion for ROM support.",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Supraspinatus",
        "Deltoid",
        "Biceps brachii",
        "Trapezius"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.072Z",
      "variant": 6,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "synonyms": [
        "shoulder raise",
        "arm lift"
      ],
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_friction",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "muscle_trapezius"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "long_explanation": "The supraspinatus initiates the first 15 degrees of abduction, but the deltoid becomes the main mover from 15 to 90 degrees.",

      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists often stretch or release this muscle to address anterior pelvic tilt and lower back discomfort.",
      "short_explanation": "Rectus femoris is part of the quadriceps and acts as a hip flexor."
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy 014",
      "clinical_application": "In massage, therapists avoid overstimulation of the facial nerve in clients with facial nerve palsy or post-stroke symptoms.",
      "source_reference": "Gray’s Anatomy, Clinical Edition",
      "short_explanation": "The facial nerve controls facial muscles for expression.",
      "answers": [
        "Trigeminal (CN V)",
        "Facial (CN VII)",
        "Accessory (CN XI)",
        "Vagus (CN X)"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "long_explanation": "Cranial nerve VII innervates muscles like the orbicularis oculi and zygomaticus. It also carries taste sensations from the anterior tongue.",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "synonyms": [

        "facial nerve"
      ],

      "short_explanation": "The aorta is the body's largest artery and branches from the heart.",

      "question": "What is the largest artery in the human body? [Variant 15]",
      "long_explanation": "The aorta originates from the left ventricle and supplies oxygenated blood to all systemic arteries through its various branches.",

    {
      "id": "Anatomy 016",
      "relevance_score": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_friction",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "muscle_trapezius"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "The deltoid muscle abducts the shoulder beyond 15 degrees.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "answers": [
        "Supraspinatus",
        "Deltoid",
        "Biceps brachii",
        "Trapezius"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The supraspinatus initiates the first 15 degrees of abduction, but the deltoid becomes the main mover from 15 to 90 degrees.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "synonyms": [
        "shoulder raise",
        "arm lift"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.416Z",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "clinical_application": "Shoulder massage work may include friction to the deltoid insertion for ROM support.",
      "correct": 1,
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "subcategory": "Muscular System",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,

    {
      "id": "Anatomy 017",
      "variant": 17,
      "synonyms": [
        "sacral bone",
        "pelvic base"

      "answers": [

      ],
      "subcategory": "Skeletal System",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "clinical_application": "Palpation of the sacrum can guide assessment of pelvic alignment and SI joint dysfunction.",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.416Z",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "The sacrum is a triangular bone formed by fused vertebrae that articulates with the ilium on both sides. It transmits weight from the spine to the pelvis.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "exam_assessment",
        "joint_joint",

        "bone_pubis"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "question": "Which bone forms the posterior portion of the pelvis? [Variant 17]",
      "short_explanation": "The sacrum forms the posterior wall of the pelvic cavity.",

      "last_answered_at": null,

      "short_explanation": "The aorta is the body's largest artery and branches from the heart.",

      ],
      "answers": [
        "Supraspinatus",
        "Deltoid",
        "Biceps brachii",
        "Trapezius"
      ],
      "variant": 26,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which muscle is primarily responsible for shoulder abduction from 15 to 90 degrees? [Variant 26]",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.417Z",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "distractors": [],
      "relevance_score": 4,
      "subcategory": "Muscular System",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_friction",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "muscle_trapezius"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "long_explanation": "The supraspinatus initiates the first 15 degrees of abduction, but the deltoid becomes the main mover from 15 to 90 degrees.",
      "synonyms": [
        "shoulder raise",
        "arm lift"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Shoulder massage work may include friction to the deltoid insertion for ROM support.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "correct": 1,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "short_explanation": "The deltoid muscle abducts the shoulder beyond 15 degrees.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy 027",
      "variant": 27,
      "synonyms": [
        "sacral bone",
        "pelvic base"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Ischium",
        "Pubis",
        "Sacrum",
        "Ilium"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Skeletal System",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "clinical_application": "Palpation of the sacrum can guide assessment of pelvic alignment and SI joint dysfunction.",
      "relevance_score": 3,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.417Z",
      "long_explanation": "The sacrum is a triangular bone formed by fused vertebrae that articulates with the ilium on both sides. It transmits weight from the spine to the pelvis.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "tags": [

        "bone_ischium",
        "bone_pubis"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which bone forms the posterior portion of the pelvis? [Variant 27]",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "The sacrum forms the posterior wall of the pelvic cavity.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",

    {
      "id": "Anatomy 028",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.417Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "subcategory": "Muscular System",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question": "Which of the following muscles is a primary hip flexor? [Variant 28]",
      "long_explanation": "Along with iliopsoas, rectus femoris helps lift the thigh toward the torso. It is biarticular, crossing both the hip and knee joints.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists often stretch or release this muscle to address anterior pelvic tilt and lower back discomfort.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_pain",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "muscle_gluteus_maximus",
        "muscle_iliopsoas",
        "muscle_rectus_femoris",
        "muscle_biceps_femoris",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "relevance_score": 2,
      "synonyms": [
        "rectus",
        "hip-lifting"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "short_explanation": "Rectus femoris is part of the quadriceps and acts as a hip flexor.",
      "answers": [
        "Gluteus maximus",
        "Rectus femoris",
        "Biceps femoris",
        "Gastrocnemius"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "variant": 28,

      "id": "Anatomy 029",
      "correct": 1,
      "answers": [

        "Vagus (CN X)"
      ],
      "relevance_score": 5,

      "stats": {

      },
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which cranial nerve is primarily responsible for facial expression? [Variant 29]",
      "subcategory": "Nervous System",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "client_sensation",
        "muscle_orbicularis_oculi",
        "nerve_facial_nerve",
        "organ_tongue"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "synonyms": [
        "VII",
        "facial nerve"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.417Z",
      "variant": 29,
      "source_reference": "Gray’s Anatomy, Clinical Edition",
      "short_explanation": "The facial nerve controls facial muscles for expression.",
      "clinical_application": "In massage, therapists avoid overstimulation of the facial nerve in clients with facial nerve palsy or post-stroke symptoms.",
      "long_explanation": "Cranial nerve VII innervates muscles like the orbicularis oculi and zygomaticus. It also carries taste sensations from the anterior tongue.",

      "long_explanation": "The aorta originates from the left ventricle and supplies oxygenated blood to all systemic arteries through its various branches.",

      "short_explanation": "The aorta is the body's largest artery and branches from the heart.",

      "long_explanation": "The sacrum is a triangular bone formed by fused vertebrae that articulates with the ilium on both sides. It transmits weight from the spine to the pelvis.",
      "question": "Which bone forms the posterior portion of the pelvis? [Variant 37]",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "variant": 37
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy 038",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "synonyms": [

      },
      "answers": [
        "Gluteus maximus",

        "Biceps femoris",
        "Gastrocnemius"

      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "variant": 38,
      "relevance_score": 4,
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "short_explanation": "Rectus femoris is part of the quadriceps and acts as a hip flexor.",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists often stretch or release this muscle to address anterior pelvic tilt and lower back discomfort.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "subcategory": "Muscular System",
      "correct": 1,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "question": "Which of the following muscles is a primary hip flexor? [Variant 38]",
      "long_explanation": "Along with iliopsoas, rectus femoris helps lift the thigh toward the torso. It is biarticular, crossing both the hip and knee joints.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_muscles",

        "muscle_gastrocnemius"

    {
      "id": "Anatomy 039",
      "subcategory": "Nervous System",
      "long_explanation": "Cranial nerve VII innervates muscles like the orbicularis oculi and zygomaticus. It also carries taste sensations from the anterior tongue.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "client_sensation",
        "muscle_orbicularis_oculi",

      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "clinical_application": "In massage, therapists avoid overstimulation of the facial nerve in clients with facial nerve palsy or post-stroke symptoms.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.417Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which cranial nerve is primarily responsible for facial expression? [Variant 39]",
      "synonyms": [
        "VII",
        "facial nerve"
      ],
      "variant": 39,
      "source_reference": "Gray’s Anatomy, Clinical Edition",
      "relevance_score": 3,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Trigeminal (CN V)",
        "Facial (CN VII)",
        "Accessory (CN XI)",
        "Vagus (CN X)"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "The facial nerve controls facial muscles for expression.",
      "correct": 1,
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy 040",
      "clinical_application": "Understanding blood flow paths helps therapists stay cautious around vulnerable vascular areas like the abdomen and carotid triangle.",

      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "variant": 40,
      "short_explanation": "The aorta is the body's largest artery and branches from the heart.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_artery",
        "anatomy_blood_flow",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "artery_aorta",

        "organ_heart"

      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.417Z",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "long_explanation": "The aorta originates from the left ventricle and supplies oxygenated blood to all systemic arteries through its various branches.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "correct": 2,
      "synonyms": [
        "main artery",
        "cardiac output vessel"
      ],
      "distractors": [],

    {
      "id": "Anatomy 041",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "short_explanation": "The deltoid muscle abducts the shoulder beyond 15 degrees.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_friction",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "muscle_trapezius"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "subcategory": "Muscular System",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_friction",
        "anatomy_muscles",

        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "muscle_trapezius"
      ],
      "question": "Which muscle is primarily responsible for shoulder abduction from 15 to 90 degrees? [Variant 41]",
      "answers": [
        "Supraspinatus",
        "Deltoid",
        "Biceps brachii",
        "Trapezius"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Shoulder massage work may include friction to the deltoid insertion for ROM support.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/anatomy_refreshers",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.073Z",
      "correct": 1,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "synonyms": [
        "shoulder raise",
        "arm lift"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.417Z",
      "long_explanation": "The supraspinatus initiates the first 15 degrees of abduction, but the deltoid becomes the main mover from 15 to 90 degrees.",
      "variant": 41,
      "relevance_score": 5
    },

      ],
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "stats": {

        "correct": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "Cranial nerve VII innervates muscles like the orbicularis oculi and zygomaticus. It also carries taste sensations from the anterior tongue.",
      "source_reference": "Gray’s Anatomy, Clinical Edition",

      "long_explanation": "The aorta originates from the left ventricle and supplies oxygenated blood to all systemic arteries through its various branches.",

      "short_explanation": "The aorta is the body's largest artery and branches from the heart.",

    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 001",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "condition_pain",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "gate control theory",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "condition_pain",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "What is the main premise of the Gate Control Theory of pain?",
      "long_explanation": "The Gate Control Theory, developed by Melzack and Wall in the 1960s, proposes that pain is not a direct result of activation of pain receptors, but rather it is modulated by a gating mechanism in the spinal cord. Non-painful stimuli, like pressure or touch, can activate larger nerve fibers (A-beta), which in turn inhibit pain transmission from smaller pain fibers (A-delta and C fibers). This theory helps explain why rubbing an area after injury can decrease discomfort.",
      "answers": [
        "Pain signals are only processed in the brain",
        "Pain signals can be blocked at the spinal cord level by non-painful input",
        "Massage removes pain signals from nerves",
        "Pain only results from injury to bones"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 1,
      "short_explanation": "The Gate Control Theory suggests that non-painful input can block or reduce the perception of pain signals at the spinal cord level.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "applied_example": "Massage stimulates large sensory fibers that activate the gating mechanism in the spinal cord. This helps reduce pain perception during and after treatment, especially in chronic pain clients.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 002",
      "question": "Where in the nervous system does the 'gate' in Gate Control Theory exist?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "condition_pain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "unit": "gate control theory",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "condition_pain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "applied_example": "Understanding spinal gating helps massage therapists explain why gentle techniques like effleurage can reduce pain without directly treating the site of injury.",
      "answers": [
        "Cerebral cortex",
        "Brainstem",
        "Dorsal horn of the spinal cord",
        "Peripheral nerves"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "The dorsal horn of the spinal cord is where primary afferent neurons synapse and where interneurons can either amplify or suppress the transmission of sensory information. In Gate Control Theory, this is the critical site where non-painful stimuli can inhibit the progression of pain signals, effectively 'closing the gate' to pain.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "The gate in the Gate Control Theory is located in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord, where sensory signals are modulated.",
      "correct": 2,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 003",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 2,
      "question": "Which type of nerve fibers are responsible for closing the pain gate?",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "C fibers",
        "A-delta fibers",
        "A-beta fibers",
        "Sympathetic fibers"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "A-beta fibers, which carry non-painful stimuli like pressure and vibration, help close the pain gate at the spinal cord level.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "long_explanation": "A-beta fibers are large, myelinated nerve fibers that conduct touch, pressure, and vibration. When activated, they stimulate inhibitory interneurons in the dorsal horn, which can suppress transmission of pain signals from slower-conducting pain fibers like A-delta and C fibers. This mechanism forms the foundation of Gate Control Theory.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "technique_vibration",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "condition_pain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "unit": "gate control theory",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z",
      "applied_example": "Massage techniques that involve broad, rhythmic pressure activate A-beta fibers. This neurological stimulation can inhibit pain signals and produce relief in conditions like fibromyalgia or chronic tension.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "tags": [
        "technique_vibration",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "condition_pain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 004",
      "question": "What role does massage play in the Gate Control Theory?",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "condition_pain",
        "client_sensation",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 1,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "unit": "gate control theory",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Massage stimulates A-beta sensory fibers that can inhibit pain signals at the spinal cord level.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "condition_pain",
        "client_sensation",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "It activates pain fibers directly",
        "It stimulates fast-conducting A-beta fibers to inhibit pain",
        "It bypasses spinal nerves",
        "It increases pain sensitivity"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Massage activates fast-conducting A-beta fibers, which carry non-painful sensory input like touch and pressure. These fibers stimulate inhibitory interneurons in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord, blocking pain signals from slower-conducting fibers (A-delta and C fibers). This gating effect reduces the sensation of pain, which explains massage’s pain-relieving benefits.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 005",
      "answers": [
        "Light touch and vibration",
        "Sharp stabbing pain",
        "Pressure from a deep wound",
        "Sudden trauma"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_vibration",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "condition_pain",
        "client_sensation",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "Gentle sensations like vibration, stroking, or superficial pressure activate large-diameter nerve fibers (A-beta fibers). These signals compete with pain input at the spinal cord level, reducing the intensity of pain signals that reach the brain. This principle supports the use of gentle massage to alleviate discomfort.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_vibration",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "condition_pain",
        "client_sensation",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "short_explanation": "Light touch and vibration help close the pain gate by stimulating non-painful sensory fibers.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "question": "Which of the following sensations can help 'close the gate' on pain?",
      "correct": 0,
      "unit": "gate control theory"
    },
    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 006",
      "long_explanation": "Gate Control Theory offers a scientific explanation for the effectiveness of massage in managing pain. By understanding how sensory input modulates pain at the spinal cord level, massage therapists can apply specific techniques to activate inhibitory pathways. This helps clients with chronic pain, post-surgical discomfort, or tension-related issues.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "answers": [
        "It explains how massage decreases lymph flow",
        "It shows how certain strokes may worsen inflammation",
        "It provides a neurological basis for massage's pain-relieving effects",
        "It proves massage can realign spinal vertebrae"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Why is Gate Control Theory important in massage therapy?",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "theory_gate_control",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_stress",
        "client",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "concept_tension",
        "population_post_surgical",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "unit": "gate control theory",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "theory_gate_control",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_stress",
        "client",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "concept_tension",
        "population_post_surgical",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "It provides a neurological basis for how massage reduces pain."
    },
    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 007",
      "short_explanation": "Deep pressure can stimulate both inhibitory and pain pathways depending on intensity.",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "gate control theory",
      "correct": 1,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "condition_pain",
        "client"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z",
      "long_explanation": "While moderate pressure may activate A-beta fibers to close the gate, excessive or painful pressure may stimulate A-delta or C fibers, potentially opening the gate and increasing pain. This makes client feedback and pressure sensitivity critical in therapeutic massage to ensure the intended analgesic effect.",
      "question": "How does deep pressure affect the gate control mechanism?",
      "answers": [
        "It closes the gate faster than light pressure",
        "It may stimulate pain fibers and open the gate",
        "It deactivates nerve pathways",
        "It has no effect on the gate"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "technique_pressure",
        "condition_pain",
        "client"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 008",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "unit": "gate control theory",
      "long_explanation": "Effleurage (gliding strokes) and vibration target superficial sensory nerves that conduct signals faster than pain fibers. These techniques align with Gate Control Theory by engaging large-diameter fibers that modulate or block pain at the spinal cord. Selecting the appropriate technique enhances the effectiveness of massage in clinical settings.",
      "answers": [
        "Effleurage and vibration",
        "Tapotement and friction",
        "Trigger point therapy",
        "Myofascial release"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_effleurage",
        "technique_friction",
        "technique_tapotement",
        "technique_vibration",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "condition_pain",
        "modality_myofascial_release",
        "modality_trigger_point_therapy",
        "concept_targeted",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "Effleurage and vibration reduce pain by stimulating non-painful touch receptors.",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_effleurage",
        "technique_friction",
        "technique_tapotement",

      ],
      "question": "Which technique best aligns with Gate Control Theory to reduce pain?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 009",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "long_explanation": "When the gate is 'open', inhibitory control at the spinal cord is reduced, allowing pain signals from A-delta and C fibers to ascend to the brain without suppression. This leads to heightened pain perception. Stress, anxiety, or lack of sensory input can contribute to the gate staying open.",
      "short_explanation": "More pain signals reach the brain, increasing pain perception.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "What happens when the pain gate is 'open'?",
      "tags": [
        "theory_gate_control",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_stress",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "concept_anxiety",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "gate control theory",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Pain perception decreases",
        "Non-painful signals are enhanced",
        "Pain signals reach the brain more easily",
        "The spinal cord stops processing input"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "theory_gate_control",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_stress",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "concept_anxiety",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Gate Control Theory 010",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "condition_pain",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.419Z",
      "unit": "gate control theory",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "Which best describes the relationship between sensory input and pain modulation in Gate Control Theory?",
      "short_explanation": "Stimulating sensory nerves can inhibit or reduce pain signal transmission.",
      "correct": 1,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "answers": [
        "Sensory input has no effect on pain",
        "Stimulating sensory receptors can inhibit pain perception",
        "Sensory input always increases pain signals",
        "Pain only occurs with sensory loss"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "theory_gate_control",
        "condition_pain",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Gate Control Theory shows that non-painful stimuli can modulate pain by activating sensory pathways that suppress pain transmission at the spinal cord. This interaction between sensory input and pain modulation is foundational to why massage therapy, physical touch, or even heat and cold can reduce perceived pain intensity."
    },
    {
      "id": "Shoulder Insertion 001",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "long_explanation": "The deltoid tuberosity is a roughened ridge halfway down the lateral humerus. The insertion point is essential for translating the deltoid's contraction into abduction of the arm at the glenohumeral joint.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region",
      "synonyms": [
        "shoulder abductor",
        "deltoid muscle"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The deltoid inserts at the deltoid tuberosity, allowing it to lift the arm laterally.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "clinical_application": "Palpation near the deltoid insertion helps assess inflammation or hypertrophy in overhead athletes.",
      "image_reference": "shoulder_insertion_deltoid.png",
      "answers": [
        "Greater tubercle of the humerus",
        "Deltoid tuberosity of the humerus",
        "Coracoid process of the scapula",
        "Bicipital groove of the humerus"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "tags": [

      ],

      "question": "Where does the deltoid muscle insert?",

    {
      "id": "Shoulder Insertion 002",
      "keywords": [

      ],
      "short_explanation": "Pectoralis major inserts at the lateral lip of the bicipital groove, giving it leverage to adduct and medially rotate the arm.",
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region",
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/insertions/shoulder_insertion",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "answers": [
        "Lesser tubercle of the humerus",
        "Greater tubercle of the humerus",
        "Deltoid tuberosity",
        "Crest of the greater tubercle (lateral lip of intertubercular groove)"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "This broad muscle inserts on the humerus where it can efficiently pull the arm inward (adduction) and rotate it medially. Its wide sternal origin narrows into a powerful tendon at this insertion point.",
      "question": "Where does the pectoralis major insert?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.420Z",

        "bone_humerus"
      ],

      "clinical_application": "This area is often tender in bench press athletes or those with shoulder impingement syndrome.",

      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "image_reference": "shoulder_insertion_supraspinatus.png",
      "short_explanation": "The supraspinatus inserts at the greater tubercle, helping initiate shoulder abduction.",
      "long_explanation": "The greater tubercle provides a strong attachment for the supraspinatus to pull the arm away from the body. Its location on the lateral humerus makes it a key player in early-phase abduction.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.420Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "condition_tendinitis",
        "concept_insertion",
        "condition_injuries",
        "bone_landmark_acromion_process",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "correct": 3,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/insertions/shoulder_insertion",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Where does the supraspinatus insert?",
      "clinical_application": "Tears or tendinitis in this insertion site are common in rotator cuff injuries.",
      "tags": [
        "condition_tendinitis",
        "concept_insertion",
        "condition_injuries",
        "bone_landmark_acromion_process",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "distractors": [],
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Shoulder Insertion 004",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "keywords": [
        "ethics_boundary",
        "concept_insertion",
        "bone_landmark_acromion_process",
        "bone_landmark_olecranon_process",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "muscle_teres_major",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region",
      "clinical_application": "Overuse or tightness here may contribute to limited external rotation in overhead athletes.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "image_reference": "shoulder_insertion_teres_major.png",
      "synonyms": [
        "teres",
        "teres group"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "tags": [
        "ethics_boundary",
        "concept_insertion",
        "bone_landmark_acromion_process",
        "bone_landmark_olecranon_process",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "muscle_teres_major",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "correct": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Medial lip of the intertubercular sulcus of the humerus",
        "Acromion process",
        "Olecranon process",
        "Deltoid tuberosity"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Where does the teres major insert?",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/insertions/shoulder_insertion",
      "long_explanation": "This insertion point allows the teres major to function like the latissimus dorsi, pulling the humerus inward and rotating it toward the body. It's especially active in resisted arm movements.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "Teres major inserts at the medial lip of the bicipital groove, enabling adduction and internal rotation.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.420Z",
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Shoulder Insertion 005",
      "long_explanation": "Its flat tendon travels under the axilla to insert on the humerus, where it contributes powerfully to pulling motions. This location gives it leverage for actions like climbing or rowing.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "synonyms": [
        "lat",
        "posterior shoulder"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.420Z",
      "question": "Where does the latissimus dorsi insert?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.077Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "tags": [
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_insertion",
        "concept_tension",
        "bone_landmark_acromion_process",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "image_reference": "shoulder_insertion_latissimus_dorsi.png",
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "Latissimus dorsi inserts into the floor of the bicipital groove, enabling shoulder extension and medial rotation.",
      "answers": [
        "Greater tubercle of the humerus",
        "Lesser tubercle of the humerus",
        "Floor of the intertubercular sulcus of the humerus",
        "Acromion process"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "keywords": [
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_insertion",
        "concept_tension",
        "bone_landmark_acromion_process",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Tension at this insertion can cause tightness in swimmers and weightlifters performing pull-down movements.",
      "distractors": [],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/insertions/shoulder_insertion",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region"
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 001",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "applied_example": "For a client with shoulder hypermobility, a massage therapist may work on rotator cuff and scapular stabilizers to reduce strain on the joint and improve proprioceptive support without increasing laxity.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "short_explanation": "Ball-and-socket joints allow movement in all anatomical planes, making them the most mobile joint type.",
      "long_explanation": "Ball-and-socket joints, like those in the shoulder and hip, allow flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, rotation, and circumduction. Their rounded bone head fits into a concave socket, granting maximal range of motion at the cost of stability. These joints rely heavily on surrounding musculature for support.",
      "unit": "joint types",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Hinge joint",
        "Saddle joint",
        "Ball-and-socket joint",
        "Pivot joint"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_hinge_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "joint_saddle_joint"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "question": "What type of joint allows for the greatest range of motion?",
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_hinge_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "joint_saddle_joint"
      ],
      "correct": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 002",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "question": "Which joint type is found in the elbow and knee?",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "joint types",
      "long_explanation": "Hinge joints are uniaxial, meaning they allow movement in a single plane–like bending and straightening. Their structure enhances stability and strength during motion, such as lifting or walking.",
      "short_explanation": "The elbow and knee are hinge joints that permit movement in one direction: flexion and extension.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_hinge_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint"
      ],
      "applied_example": "Clients with limited knee extension may benefit from massage to the hamstrings and calf to release muscular restriction and improve range within the joint's natural axis.",
      "answers": [
        "Gliding joint",
        "Ball-and-socket joint",
        "Hinge joint",
        "Pivot joint"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_hinge_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 003",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "answers": [
        "Shoulder",
        "Thumb",
        "Neck (between atlas and axis)",
        "Hip"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Pivot joints allow one bone to rotate around another, such as between the first two cervical vertebrae.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "applied_example": "A massage therapist addressing neck stiffness might gently work on suboccipital muscles to restore rotation at the pivot joint while avoiding aggressive stretches that could strain cervical ligaments.",
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "joint types",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "Where in the body would you find a pivot joint?",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "bone_cervical_vertebrae"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The atlantoaxial joint between C1 and C2 vertebrae is a pivot joint allowing head rotation. These joints are specialized for twisting motions and are stabilized by surrounding ligaments and musculature.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "bone_cervical_vertebrae"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 004",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "short_explanation": "Gliding joints allow flat bones to slide past each other with limited movement.",
      "correct": 1,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_hinge_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "bone_carpals",
        "bone_tarsals"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "Which joint type allows bones to slide past one another?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "applied_example": "When treating wrist pain, massage focused on the forearm and intrinsic hand muscles may reduce tension affecting the glide between carpal bones.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "joint types",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_hinge_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "bone_carpals",
        "bone_tarsals"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Hinge joint",
        "Gliding joint",
        "Ball-and-socket joint",
        "Pivot joint"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "These joints, such as those in the carpals and tarsals, enable subtle translational movements that help distribute load and absorb shock. They are crucial for flexibility and smooth joint transitions."
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 005",
      "answers": [
        "Ball-and-socket joint",
        "Saddle joint",
        "Pivot joint",
        "Gliding joint"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Saddle joints, like the carpometacarpal joint of the thumb, allow flexion/extension and abduction/adduction. Their unique structure is essential for grasp and fine motor control but prone to overuse and arthritis.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "correct": 1,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "What type of joint is found at the base of the thumb?",
      "distractors": [],
      "applied_example": "Massage targeting the thenar eminence and forearm flexors may alleviate pain in clients with thumb arthritis or repetitive strain from texting or gripping tools.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "joint_saddle_joint"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "unit": "joint types",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "joint_saddle_joint"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The saddle joint at the base of the thumb allows movement in two planes, giving the thumb its unique mobility."
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 006",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "unit": "joint types",
      "short_explanation": "Pivot joints allow rotation around a single axis, such as turning the head or rotating the forearm.",
      "applied_example": "Clients with limited forearm pronation may benefit from massage of the supinator, pronator teres, and surrounding connective tissue to restore function in the pivot joint without joint mobilization.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_hinge_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "joint_condyloid_joint"
      ],
      "question": "Which joint type allows for rotation around a single axis?",
      "answers": [
        "Pivot joint",
        "Hinge joint",
        "Condyloid joint",
        "Suture"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "correct": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint",
        "joint_hinge_joint",
        "joint_pivot_joint",
        "joint_condyloid_joint"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Rotation occurs at pivot joints like the atlantoaxial joint or the proximal radioulnar joint in the forearm. These joints are stabilized by ligaments and muscular control to avoid excessive torque.",
      "category": "Anatomy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 007",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "long_explanation": "Synovial joints, which include most movable joints like knees and shoulders, have a capsule filled with synovial fluid to lubricate and nourish the joint. They are supported by cartilage, ligaments, and muscle.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "unit": "joint types",
      "question": "Synovial joints are characterized by:",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "applied_example": "When addressing chronic knee tightness, soft tissue work can improve circulation to periarticular structures and reduce tension that compresses the synovial cavity.",
      "short_explanation": "Synovial joints contain a fluid-filled cavity that allows smooth, controlled movement between bones.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Fibrous connections",
        "Cartilage fusion",
        "A fluid-filled joint cavity",
        "Bone fusion"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 008",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Knee",
        "Shoulder",
        "Elbow",
        "Wrist"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "question": "Which joint is an example of a ball-and-socket joint?",
      "applied_example": "Massage for shoulder dysfunction may target overused deltoid and trapezius muscles while addressing scapular stabilizers like the rhomboids and serratus anterior.",
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "The shoulder is a ball-and-socket joint, capable of motion in all directions.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "correct": 1,
      "unit": "joint types",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "long_explanation": "The glenohumeral joint of the shoulder provides the most range of motion in the body. It depends heavily on the rotator cuff and scapular muscles for stabilization during movement."
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 009",
      "long_explanation": "Sutures connect skull bones during early development and eventually ossify to create a protective structure. They allow brain growth in infants but form stable joints by adulthood.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Which of the following joints is immovable?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "joint_joint",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "unit": "joint types",
      "correct": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "joint_joint",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "answers": [
        "Suture",
        "Hinge",
        "Ball-and-socket",
        "Gliding"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "short_explanation": "Sutures are fibrous joints in the skull that are immovable once fused.",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "applied_example": "While sutures themselves don't move in adults, craniosacral therapists may use gentle touch around them to address tension in surrounding fascia and improve cerebrospinal fluid flow.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Joint Types 010",
      "correct": 1,
      "long_explanation": "Found in the wrist and knuckles, condyloid joints allow flexion/extension and abduction/adduction. Their biaxial movement supports complex tasks like writing, gripping, or shifting weight across joints.",
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.422Z",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "question": "Condyloid joints allow movement in:",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.078Z",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "One plane only",
        "Two planes (biaxial motion)",
        "All directions",
        "Rotation only"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "joint types",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "applied_example": "To support a client with wrist stiffness, massage of the forearm flexors/extensors and interosseous muscles may restore mobility in condyloid joints affected by repetitive use or inflammation.",
      "short_explanation": "Condyloid joints permit movement in two planes–like bending and side-to-side shifting.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "intermediate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ankle Dorsiflexor 001",
      "long_explanation": "It originates from the lateral tibia and inserts on the medial foot, allowing upward movement of the foot to clear the ground during walking.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_longus",
        "bone_tibia"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Gastrocnemius",
        "Peroneus longus",
        "Tibialis anterior",
        "Flexor digitorum longus"
      ],
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "question": "Which muscle is the primary dorsiflexor of the ankle?",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 2,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_longus",
        "bone_tibia"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_dorsiflexors",
      "clinical_application": "Weakness causes foot drop, increasing fall risk in older adults.",
      "synonyms": [
        "main dorsiflexor",
        "TA"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Tibialis anterior is the main muscle responsible for ankle dorsiflexion.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.423Z",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.079Z",
      "subcategory": "Ankle Dorsiflexors",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Ankle Dorsiflexor 002",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "subcategory": "Ankle Dorsiflexors",
      "answers": [
        "Extensor digitorum longus",
        "Tibialis posterior",
        "Extensor hallucis longus",
        "Peroneus brevis"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_dorsiflexors",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.079Z",
      "long_explanation": "It originates on the fibula and inserts on the distal phalanx of the hallux, supporting toe clearance during walking and balance during push-off.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "synonyms": [
        "EHL",
        "toe lifter"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 2,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "short_explanation": "EHL lifts the big toe and helps dorsiflex the ankle.",
      "question": "Which muscle dorsiflexes the foot and also extends the great toe?",
      "tags": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_balance",
        "concept_originates",

        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "muscle_extensor_hallucis_longus",
        "bone_fibula"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Often affected in L5 nerve root compression, leading to hallux extension weakness.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.423Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "technique_compression",

    {
      "id": "Ankle Dorsiflexor 003",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",

        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
