        "bone_fibula"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_dorsiflexors",
      "question": "Which anterior compartment muscle helps dorsiflex the ankle and extend toes 2–5?",
      "answers": [
        "Flexor digitorum longus",
        "Extensor digitorum longus",
        "Tibialis posterior",
        "Peroneus longus"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_longus",
        "bone_tibia",
        "bone_fibula"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.423Z",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "long_explanation": "EDL originates from the tibia and fibula and runs to the distal toes, playing a key role in swing phase of gait.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.079Z",
      "synonyms": [
        "EDL",
        "toe lifter"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "clinical_application": "EDL weakness may lead to dragging toes or increased fall risk.",
      "correct": 1,
      "subcategory": "Ankle Dorsiflexors",
      "short_explanation": "EDL extends the lesser toes and contributes to dorsiflexion.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Ankle Dorsiflexor 004",
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the ankle dorsiflexors?",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "nerve_tibial_nerve"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.423Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "The deep fibular (peroneal) nerve supplies the ankle dorsiflexors.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "nerve_tibial_nerve"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "subcategory": "Ankle Dorsiflexors",
      "clinical_application": "Entrapment can result in foot drop or toe drag during swing phase.",
      "answers": [
        "Tibial nerve",
        "Superficial fibular nerve",
        "Deep fibular (peroneal) nerve",
        "Saphenous nerve"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "This nerve innervates tibialis anterior, EDL, and EHL, which all contribute to foot dorsiflexion and toe extension.",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_dorsiflexors",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "synonyms": [
        "dorsiflexor innervation"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.079Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ankle Dorsiflexor 005",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.079Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Which muscle assists dorsiflexion and is sometimes considered part of the extensor group?",
      "synonyms": [
        "fibularis tertius"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_dorsiflexors",
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_longus",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_brevis"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_longus",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_brevis"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "subcategory": "Ankle Dorsiflexors",
      "long_explanation": "This small muscle originates from EDL fibers and inserts on the 5th metatarsal. Not everyone has it, but it contributes to ankle stabilization during gait.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.423Z",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "answers": [
        "Flexor hallucis longus",
        "Extensor digitorum brevis",
        "Peroneus tertius",
        "Tibialis posterior"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Weakness here may affect lateral ankle control during fast changes of direction.",
      "short_explanation": "Peroneus tertius aids in dorsiflexion and slight eversion."
    },
    {
      "id": "Ankle Plantarflexor 001",
      "long_explanation": "Originating from the femur and inserting on the calcaneus via the Achilles tendon, the gastrocnemius powerfully points the toes and aids in jumping or running.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_extensor_hallucis_longus",
        "bone_femur"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Tibialis anterior",
        "Gastrocnemius",
        "Extensor hallucis longus",
        "Tibialis posterior"
      ],
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "question": "Which muscle is the primary mover in ankle plantarflexion?",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 1,
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_extensor_hallucis_longus",
        "bone_femur"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_plantarflexors",
      "clinical_application": "Tightness or weakness may impair push-off and contribute to Achilles tendinopathy.",
      "synonyms": [
        "main plantarflexor",
        "calf"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The gastrocnemius is the main muscle responsible for plantarflexion of the ankle.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "subcategory": "Ankle Plantarflexors",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Ankle Plantarflexor 002",
      "question": "Which muscle assists plantarflexion and also crosses the knee joint?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "client",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius",
        "muscle_soleus",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_longus",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "answers": [
        "Soleus",
        "Flexor hallucis longus",
        "Gastrocnemius",
        "Tibialis posterior"
      ],
      "synonyms": [
        "calf muscle",
        "gastroc"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "long_explanation": "This gives it a dual role: plantarflexing the ankle and assisting in knee flexion. It is most effective in plantarflexion when the knee is extended.",
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "Gastrocnemius crosses both the knee and ankle joints.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "clinical_application": "Useful to assess in clients with hamstring strain or calf tightness affecting knee function.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "distractors": [],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "client",
        "movement_plantarflexion",

        "muscle_gastrocnemius",
        "muscle_soleus",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_longus",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Ankle Plantarflexors",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_plantarflexors"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ankle Plantarflexor 003",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",

      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "short_explanation": "Tibialis posterior supports the arch and plantarflexes the foot.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "bone_tarsals"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Ankle Plantarflexors",
      "question": "Which deep muscle contributes to plantarflexion and also inverts the foot?",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_plantarflexors",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "clinical_application": "Weakness contributes to flat feet and posterior tibial tendon dysfunction.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "synonyms": [
        "TP",
        "deep flexor"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "long_explanation": "It runs deep in the posterior compartment, wrapping medially to insert on tarsal bones. It’s crucial in maintaining the medial arch and powering inversion.",
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "Peroneus longus",
        "Tibialis posterior",
        "Extensor digitorum longus",
        "Gastrocnemius"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ankle Plantarflexor 004",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "muscle_soleus",
        "muscle_plantaris",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_longus"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "muscle_soleus",
        "muscle_plantaris",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_longus"
      ],
      "question": "Which of the following muscles does NOT contribute to plantarflexion?",
      "clinical_application": "Differentiating dorsiflexors vs plantarflexors helps assess gait and foot drop.",
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "answers": [
        "Soleus",
        "Flexor digitorum longus",
        "Tibialis anterior",
        "Plantaris"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_plantarflexors",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "subcategory": "Ankle Plantarflexors",
      "long_explanation": "Although located near the ankle, it acts in the opposite direction, lifting the foot rather than pointing it downward.",
      "short_explanation": "Tibialis anterior dorsiflexes the ankle, not plantarflexes it.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "synonyms": [
        "non-plantarflexor"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Ankle Plantarflexor 005",
      "correct": 1,
      "long_explanation": "Unlike gastrocnemius, soleus does not cross the knee and is more active in slow, sustained plantarflexion like standing or walking.",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "subcategory": "Ankle Plantarflexors",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "short_explanation": "Soleus is active during quiet standing to prevent the body from falling forward.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "synonyms": [
        "postural muscle",
        "soleus"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "clinical_application": "Tightness can cause chronic calf tension and gait issues in older adults.",
      "answers": [
        "Gastrocnemius",
        "Soleus",
        "Flexor hallucis longus",
        "Peroneus brevis"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/ankle_plantarflexors",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",

        "concept_tension",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius",
        "muscle_soleus",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_longus"
      ],
      "question": "Which muscle is considered a strong postural stabilizer in standing plantarflexion?",

        "anatomy_muscles",

        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "concept_tension",
        "muscle_gastrocnemius",

    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 001",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",

        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Triceps brachii is the main elbow extensor.",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "clinical_application": "Triceps strength is crucial in activities like pushing or weightlifting. Its assessment is important in post-surgical elbow rehab.",
      "long_explanation": "The triceps brachii, with three heads (long, lateral, and medial), is the primary muscle responsible for extending the elbow. The long head also assists with shoulder extension and adduction.",
      "answers": [
        "Anconeus",
        "Triceps brachii",
        "Deltoid",
        "Latissimus dorsi"
      ],
      "img": "triceps_brachii_elbow_extension.png",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "correct": 1,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "Which muscle is the primary extensor of the elbow joint?",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "population_post_surgical",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 147",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 002",
      "answers": [
        "Musculocutaneous nerve",
        "Radial nerve",
        "Median nerve",
        "Ulnar nerve"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_plexus_brachial",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "clinical_application": "Radial nerve injuries can lead to weakened elbow and wrist extension, sometimes observed as 'wrist drop.'",
      "long_explanation": "All three heads of the triceps are innervated by the radial nerve, which arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus (C6-C8). This nerve also innervates many forearm extensors.",
      "img": "radial_nerve_triceps.png",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "correct": 1,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy, 7th ed., Plate 417",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_plexus_brachial",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors",
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the triceps brachii?",
      "short_explanation": "The radial nerve innervates the triceps brachii.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 003",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "The long head of the triceps originates from the infraglenoid tubercle.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 147",
      "keywords": [
        "condition_pain",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "bone_landmark_olecranon_process",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "bone_scapula"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Olecranon process",
        "Lateral epicondyle",
        "Infraglenoid tubercle",
        "Coracoid process"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "question": "What is the origin of the long head of the triceps brachii?",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "correct": 2,
      "long_explanation": "The long head of the triceps brachii originates on the infraglenoid tubercle of the scapula, allowing it to assist with shoulder extension and adduction in addition to elbow extension.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "tags": [
        "condition_pain",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "bone_landmark_olecranon_process",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "bone_scapula"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "clinical_application": "Understanding the long head's origin is helpful when treating posterior shoulder pain or long head tendon strain.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "img": "triceps_long_head_origin.png",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 004",
      "img": "anconeus_muscle.png",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "question": "Which small muscle assists the triceps in elbow extension and stabilizes the elbow joint?",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "joint_joint",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 148",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 1,
      "short_explanation": "The anconeus assists triceps in elbow extension.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "joint_joint",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "clinical_application": "Palpation of the anconeus can reveal tenderness associated with lateral elbow stress or triceps overuse.",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "answers": [
        "Brachialis",
        "Anconeus",
        "Pronator teres",
        "Supinator"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The anconeus is a small triangular muscle located near the lateral epicondyle of the humerus. It helps with elbow extension and stabilizes the joint during rapid movements.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 005",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "long_explanation": "The medial head of the triceps is located beneath the long and lateral heads. It is always active during elbow extension, even with light resistance.",
      "short_explanation": "The medial head lies deep to the long and lateral heads.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "keywords": [
        "concept_targeted",
        "condition_injury",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "img": "triceps_medial_head.png",
      "answers": [
        "Long head",
        "Lateral head",
        "Medial head",
        "None of the above"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Targeting the medial head in rehab helps rebuild base strength in elbow extension post-injury.",
      "tags": [
        "concept_targeted",
        "condition_injury",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy, 7th ed., Plate 414",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors",
      "question": "Which head of the triceps brachii lies deep to the other two heads?",
      "correct": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 006",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_friction",
        "condition_tendinitis"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists may apply deep friction and cross-fiber massage to address triceps tendinitis.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_friction",
        "condition_tendinitis"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors",
      "source_reference": "Clinical Massage Therapy, Rattray & Ludwig, p. 503",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Triceps tendinitis usually affects the distal tendon at the olecranon. It results from overuse or improper technique in activities like push-ups or bench pressing.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "question": "What is a common overuse condition associated with the triceps tendon?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "img": "triceps_tendinitis.png",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "answers": [
        "Biceps tendinitis",
        "Triceps rupture",
        "Triceps tendinitis",
        "Olecranon bursitis"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "Triceps tendinitis is an overuse condition."
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 007",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "exam_assessment",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "bone_humerus",
        "organ_testes"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Radial nerve injury affects elbow extension.",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "answers": [
        "Elbow extension",
        "Forearm pronation",
        "Shoulder abduction",
        "Wrist flexion"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The radial nerve travels in the radial groove of the humerus. Injury here can lead to triceps weakness and loss of elbow extension, along with wrist drop from extensor paralysis.",
      "question": "Which motion is affected most if the radial nerve is damaged at the mid-humerus?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "correct": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "exam_assessment",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "bone_humerus",
        "organ_testes"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "clinical_application": "In trauma cases like humeral fractures, testing triceps reflex helps evaluate radial nerve integrity.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "img": "radial_nerve_injury.png",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy, 7th ed., Plate 416"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 008",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "question": "Which movement combination best activates the long head of the triceps?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 147",
      "answers": [
        "Shoulder flexion and elbow extension",
        "Shoulder extension and elbow extension",
        "Shoulder abduction and elbow flexion",
        "Shoulder rotation and elbow pronation"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "long_explanation": "Because the long head crosses both the shoulder and elbow joints, it is most active when both joints are extending simultaneously, such as during a swimming stroke or a behind-the-head press.",
      "correct": 1,
      "short_explanation": "The long head acts during shoulder and elbow extension.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "img": "triceps_activation.png",
      "clinical_application": "Including compound extension movements in therapy activates the long head effectively.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "distractors": [],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 009",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "short_explanation": "Triceps inserts at the olecranon process.",
      "clinical_application": "Injuries here can be palpated as tenderness at the posterior elbow and are involved in triceps tendinopathies.",
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Radial tuberosity",
        "Ulnar styloid",
        "Olecranon process",
        "Coronoid process"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "concept_insertion",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "bone_landmark_olecranon_process",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "joint_joint",
        "bone_ulna"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy, 7th ed., Plate 415",
      "img": "triceps_insertion.png",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "long_explanation": "All three heads of the triceps converge into a single tendon that inserts at the olecranon process of the ulna. This allows it to extend the elbow joint effectively.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "What is the insertion point of the triceps brachii muscle?",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "concept_insertion",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",

        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "joint_joint",
        "bone_ulna"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Extensors 010",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "population_athlete",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The lateral head of the triceps brachii is activated more during high-intensity or explosive elbow extension tasks such as throwing or heavy pressing.",
      "question": "Which head of the triceps is most active during high-force movements?",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 148",
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.080Z",
      "short_explanation": "The lateral head is recruited during powerful extension.",
      "answers": [
        "Long head",
        "Medial head",
        "Lateral head",
        "Anconeus"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "img": "triceps_lateral_head.png",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.424Z",
      "keywords": [
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "population_athlete",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "clinical_application": "Strengthening the lateral head can improve power and reduce injury risk in athletic clients.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_extensors"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 001",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Brachialis is the strongest elbow flexor regardless of forearm position.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 143",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "joint_joint",
        "bone_humerus",
        "bone_radius"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Biceps brachii",
        "Brachialis",
        "Brachioradialis",
        "Coracobrachialis"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z",
      "question": "Which muscle is the primary flexor of the elbow in all positions of forearm rotation?",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "correct": 1,
      "long_explanation": "The brachialis lies deep to the biceps brachii and attaches from the anterior humerus to the ulnar tuberosity. Unlike the biceps, it does not cross the shoulder joint or insert on the radius, allowing it to function strictly as a pure elbow flexor across all positions of supination or pronation.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "joint_joint",
        "bone_humerus",
        "bone_radius"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.425Z",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "clinical_application": "Understanding brachialis function is important in designing rehab protocols for elbow flexion after injuries such as distal humerus fractures.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "img": "brachialis_muscle.png",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 002",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "distractors": [],
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "The biceps brachii inserts on the radial tuberosity and is a powerful supinator of the forearm, especially when the elbow is flexed. This function is crucial in daily tasks like using a screwdriver.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.425Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "Biceps tears or tendinopathy can affect both flexion and supination strength, which massage therapists should assess when evaluating arm pain.",
      "answers": [
        "Shoulder abduction",
        "Forearm pronation",
        "Forearm supination",
        "Wrist flexion"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 142",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "correct": 2,
      "question": "What is the primary action of the biceps brachii besides elbow flexion?",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "condition_pain",
        "exam_assessment",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "condition_pain",
        "exam_assessment",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii"
      ],
      "img": "biceps_brachii_function.png",
      "short_explanation": "Biceps brachii also supinates the forearm.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 003",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Median nerve",
        "Radial nerve",
        "Ulnar nerve",
        "Musculocutaneous nerve"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the brachialis muscle?",
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy, 7th ed., Plate 413",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_plexus_brachial",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The musculocutaneous nerve innervates the brachialis.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.425Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_plexus_brachial",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "The musculocutaneous nerve (C5-C7) arises from the lateral cord of the brachial plexus and supplies the anterior arm muscles including biceps brachii, coracobrachialis, and brachialis. Brachialis may also receive a branch from the radial nerve.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "img": "nerve_brachialis.png",
      "clinical_application": "Injury to the musculocutaneous nerve can impair elbow flexion, highlighting its role in arm function assessments.",
      "correct": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 004",
      "short_explanation": "Brachioradialis flexes best with the forearm in a neutral position.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "img": "brachioradialis_flexion.png",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [

        "concept_originates",

        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "bone_radius"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.425Z",
      "answers": [
        "Supinated forearm",
        "Pronated forearm",
        "Neutral forearm",
        "Fully extended arm"
      ],
      "question": "The brachioradialis is most active during which type of elbow flexion?",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 144",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Originating from the lateral supracondylar ridge and inserting on the radius, brachioradialis acts primarily when the forearm is in mid-position, like when drinking from a cup with a thumbs-up grip.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_cupping",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "exam_assessment",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "bone_radius"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "clinical_application": "Palpation of the brachioradialis can help massage therapists assess lateral forearm tightness or radial nerve irritation.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 005",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question": "Which muscle helps flex the arm at the shoulder joint?",
      "short_explanation": "Coracobrachialis assists in shoulder flexion.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_pain",
        "concept_originates",

        "joint_joint",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "long_explanation": "Coracobrachialis originates on the coracoid process and inserts on the medial humerus. It helps flex and adduct the shoulder and stabilizes the humerus during arm movements.",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists often address coracobrachialis when treating anterior shoulder pain or impingement symptoms.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 140",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 1,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Brachialis",
        "Coracobrachialis",
        "Brachioradialis",
        "Triceps brachii"
      ],
      "img": "coracobrachialis_action.png",

    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 006",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "img": "biceps_brachii_dual_joint.png",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Which muscle of the arm crosses both the shoulder and elbow joints?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.425Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "tags": [

      ],
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy, 7th ed., Plate 412",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Biceps brachii crosses both the shoulder and elbow joints.",
      "answers": [
        "Brachialis",
        "Biceps brachii",
        "Brachioradialis",
        "Triceps brachii"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "long_explanation": "Biceps brachii originates at the scapula (short head from coracoid, long head from supraglenoid tubercle) and inserts on the radius, enabling it to flex the elbow and shoulder and supinate the forearm.",
      "clinical_application": "In shoulder dysfunctions, assessing biceps tightness or overuse can be crucial for full recovery.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "correct": 1,
      "keywords": [

    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 007",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.425Z",
      "clinical_application": "Massage techniques like friction therapy and stretching can help manage symptoms of biceps tendinitis.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "keywords": [
        "technique_friction",
        "condition_pain",
        "condition_tendinitis",
        "client_sensation",
        "modality_massage_techniques",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "bone_carpals"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Clinical Massage Therapy, Rattray & Ludwig, p. 486",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "short_explanation": "Biceps tendinitis is a common overuse condition.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "What is a common overuse condition affecting arm flexors?",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z",
      "tags": [
        "technique_friction",
        "condition_pain",
        "condition_tendinitis",
        "client_sensation",
        "modality_massage_techniques",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "bone_carpals"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Overuse of the biceps brachii, particularly the long head, can result in tendinitis, presenting as anterior shoulder or arm pain and sometimes a snapping sensation.",
      "answers": [
        "Carpal tunnel syndrome",
        "Golfer’s elbow",
        "Tennis elbow",
        "Biceps tendinitis"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "img": "biceps_tendinitis.png"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 008",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 154",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "muscle_triceps_brachii",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Addressing ECRL in therapy can improve grip endurance and reduce lateral forearm strain.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "long_explanation": "ECRL originates from the lateral humerus and acts to extend and abduct the wrist while assisting with elbow flexion. This muscle plays a role in complex movement patterns involving tool use or gripping.",
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which muscle is involved in both elbow flexion and wrist extension synergy patterns?",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Extensor carpi radialis longus synergizes elbow flexion and wrist extension.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.425Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z",
      "correct": 3,
      "img": "ecrl_synergy.png",
      "answers": [
        "Biceps brachii",
        "Triceps brachii",
        "Brachioradialis",
        "Extensor carpi radialis longus"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 009",
      "clinical_application": "Knowing this helps massage therapists isolate true elbow flexors during rehabilitation.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "Which arm flexor is not directly involved in supination or pronation?",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.425Z",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "bone_radius",
        "bone_ulna"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 143",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Brachialis does not affect forearm rotation.",
      "long_explanation": "Brachialis attaches to the ulna and is unaffected by radius rotation, making it a pure elbow flexor regardless of supination or pronation.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Biceps brachii",
        "Brachialis",
        "Brachioradialis",
        "Pronator teres"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "bone_radius",
        "bone_ulna"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "correct": 1,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "img": "brachialis_isolation.png"
    },
    {
      "id": "Arm Flexors 010",
      "img": "brachialis_test.png",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "Which test might isolate the brachialis muscle during clinical assessment?",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.425Z",
      "clinical_application": "Therapists can use this to assess brachialis function and detect muscular imbalances.",
      "source_reference": "Clinical Massage Therapy, Rattray & Ludwig, p. 496",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/arm_flexors",
      "short_explanation": "Elbow flexion in pronation isolates the brachialis.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "organ_testes"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 1,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "By flexing the elbow with the forearm pronated, the biceps brachii is biomechanically disadvantaged, allowing the therapist to more effectively evaluate brachialis strength.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "muscle_biceps_brachii",
        "organ_testes"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Flex elbow in supination",
        "Flex elbow in pronation",
        "Flex elbow in neutral grip",
        "Resisted wrist flexion"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.081Z",
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Foot Extensor 001",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.082Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Extensor hallucis longus extends the big toe at both joints.",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_fulling",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "muscle_extensor_hallucis_longus",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_longus",
        "muscle_abductor_hallucis",
        "bone_fibula"
      ],
      "synonyms": [
        "EHL",
        "toe lifter"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Extensor hallucis longus",
        "Flexor hallucis longus",
        "Extensor digitorum longus",
        "Abductor hallucis"
      ],
      "question": "Which muscle extends the great toe at both MTP and IP joints?",
      "long_explanation": "EHL originates on the fibula and inserts on the distal phalanx of the hallux, enabling full extension of the big toe during swing phase of gait.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.426Z",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "clinical_application": "Weakness here can lead to toe drag during walking, common in foot drop.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/foot_extensors",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "subcategory": "Foot Extensors",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "tags": [
        "technique_fulling",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "muscle_extensor_hallucis_longus",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_longus",
        "muscle_abductor_hallucis",
        "bone_fibula"
      ],
      "correct": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question_type": "multiple choice"
    },
    {
      "id": "Foot Extensor 002",
      "short_explanation": "Extensor digitorum longus extends toes 2–5.",
      "question": "What is the primary action of extensor digitorum longus?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.426Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "subcategory": "Foot Extensors",
      "clinical_application": "Tightness here may create dorsal foot pain or contribute to shin splints.",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Toe flexion",
        "Dorsiflexion of the foot only",
        "Extension of toes 2–5",
        "Plantarflexion of the ankle"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.082Z",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "synonyms": [
        "EDL",
        "long toe extensor"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "condition_pain",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "bone_tibia",
        "bone_fibula"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/foot_extensors",
      "long_explanation": "EDL originates from the tibia/fibula and extends down to insert on the distal phalanges of toes 2–5, helping lift the toes during swing phase.",
      "tags": [
        "condition_pain",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "bone_tibia",
        "bone_fibula"
      ],
      "correct": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "Foot Extensor 003",
      "subcategory": "Foot Extensors",
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.082Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "Weakness can cause foot slap or gait compensation.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.426Z",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "question": "Which muscle assists in both dorsiflexion and extension of the toes?",
      "long_explanation": "Its anatomical course allows it to both dorsiflex the ankle and extend the lateral toes, making it key in swing and heel strike phases.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "muscle_tibialis_posterior",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_longus"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "synonyms": [
        "toe and foot extensor"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "EDL helps lift the foot and extend toes together during gait.",
      "distractors": [],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/foot_extensors",
      "answers": [
        "Tibialis posterior",
        "Peroneus longus",
        "Extensor digitorum longus",
        "Flexor digitorum longus"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,

    {
      "id": "Foot Extensor 004",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "subcategory": "Foot Extensors",
      "short_explanation": "EHL inserts on the distal phalanx of the hallux.",
      "clinical_application": "Useful in gait correction therapies for big toe stiffness or neuropathy.",

      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.426Z",

      ],

      ],
      "long_explanation": "This allows full extension at both the MTP and IP joints, supporting toe clearance and balance during gait."

    {

      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "synonyms": [
        "EDB"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Extensor digitorum brevis is a dorsal foot muscle that extends toes 2–4.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/foot_extensors",
      "answers": [
        "Extensor digitorum brevis",
        "Abductor hallucis",

        "Peroneus brevis"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "long_explanation": "EDB originates on the dorsal calcaneus and inserts into the tendons of EDL, supporting toe extension.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which muscle is located on the dorsal surface of the foot and assists in toe extension?",
      "clinical_application": "May be mistaken for swelling or tumor in dorsal foot; palpation confirms its muscular nature.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.082Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3
    },
    {
      "id": "Foot Flexor 001",
      "long_explanation": "These include flexor digitorum brevis, flexor hallucis brevis, lumbricals, quadratus plantae, flexor digiti minimi brevis, and the dorsal and plantar interossei. Together, they help bend the toes and maintain arch integrity.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_balance",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_brevis",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_brevis",
        "muscle_flexor_digiti_minimi_brevis",
        "muscle_plantar_interossei"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "2",
        "4",
        "6",
        "8"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "question": "How many intrinsic foot muscles are involved in toe flexion?",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 3,
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed. – Foot Muscles",
      "keywords": [

        "concept_balance",
        "concept_assists",

      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/foot_flexors",
      "clinical_application": "These muscles are important in balance and toe-off during gait. Weakness may contribute to claw toe or arch collapse.",
      "synonyms": [
        "toe muscles",
        "toe flexors"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "There are 8 key intrinsic foot muscles that assist in toe flexion.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.426Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,

    },
    {

      },
      "subcategory": "Foot Flexors",
      "clinical_application": "Injuries here are common in ballet dancers and sprinters with overuse of toe-off power.",
      "short_explanation": "Flexor hallucis longus flexes the big toe at the distal joint.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "long_explanation": "This long muscle runs along the posterior leg and inserts on the distal phalanx of the hallux, allowing powerful flexion during push-off in gait.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Flexor digitorum longus",
        "Flexor hallucis brevis",
        "Flexor hallucis longus",
        "Abductor hallucis"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",

      "correct": 2,

    },
    {
      "id": "Foot Flexor 003",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",

      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.083Z",
      "clinical_application": "Useful in maintaining proper foot mechanics in pronators or those with collapsing arches.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/foot_flexors",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",

      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Which muscle assists in flexing the toes and correcting oblique pull of flexor digitorum longus?",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "short_explanation": "Quadratus plantae redirects pull of flexor digitorum longus to flex the toes straight.",
      "synonyms": [
        "QP",
        "plantar foot muscle"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Flexor hallucis brevis",
        "Quadratus plantae",
        "Lumbricals",
        "Extensor digitorum longus"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "subcategory": "Foot Flexors",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.426Z",
      "long_explanation": "It originates on the calcaneus and inserts onto the tendon of flexor digitorum longus, straightening the line of pull for more effective toe flexion.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Foot Flexor 004",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/foot_flexors",
      "subcategory": "Foot Flexors",
      "long_explanation": "Though located near the flexors in the leg, tibialis anterior extends the toes and dorsiflexes the foot. It does not contribute to toe curling or gripping.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "short_explanation": "Tibialis anterior is a dorsiflexor, not a toe flexor.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.083Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "correct": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Which of the following is NOT involved in toe flexion?",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "clinical_application": "Important to differentiate in gait assessments where dorsiflexion vs toe flexion can mimic similar movement.",
      "tags": [
        "exam_assessment",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_brevis",
        "muscle_plantar_interossei"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "synonyms": [
        "non-flexor muscles"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.426Z",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "exam_assessment",
        "muscle_tibialis_anterior",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_brevis",
        "muscle_plantar_interossei"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Flexor digitorum brevis",
        "Plantar interossei",
        "Tibialis anterior",
        "Lumbricals"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Foot Flexor 005",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "bone_landmark_medial_malleolus",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_longus",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_longus",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_brevis",
        "muscle_plantar_interossei"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which muscle flexes the lateral four toes at the distal interphalangeal joints?",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/foot_flexors",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "Flexor digitorum longus inserts on distal phalanges of toes 2–5.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.083Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_insertion",
        "bone_landmark_medial_malleolus",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_longus",
        "muscle_flexor_hallucis_longus",
        "muscle_flexor_digitorum_brevis",
        "muscle_plantar_interossei"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Foot Flexors",
      "clinical_application": "Overuse may contribute to plantar fasciitis or claw toe patterns.",
      "answers": [
        "Flexor digitorum brevis",
        "Flexor hallucis longus",
        "Flexor digitorum longus",
        "Plantar interossei"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "It passes behind the medial malleolus, then splits into four tendons to flex the DIP joints. It works with the quadratus plantae and lumbricals.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.426Z",
      "synonyms": [
        "FDL",
        "toe flexor"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice"
    },
    {
      "id": "Forearm Extensors 001",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/forearm_extensors",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "modality_soft_tissue",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "concept_targeted",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "Flexor carpi radialis",
        "Extensor carpi radialis longus",
        "Extensor digitorum",
        "Extensor carpi ulnaris"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "Extensor carpi radialis longus extends and abducts the wrist.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "modality_soft_tissue",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "concept_targeted",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "img": "extensor_carpi_radialis_longus.png",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "clinical_application": "Overuse can lead to lateral epicondylitis ('tennis elbow'), making it a key target in soft tissue therapy.",
      "correct": 1,
      "question": "Which muscle primarily extends and abducts the wrist?",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 158",
      "long_explanation": "This muscle originates from the lateral supracondylar ridge of the humerus and inserts on the second metacarpal. It acts to extend and radially deviate the wrist.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 3
    },
    {
      "id": "Forearm Extensors 002",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "correct": 1,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "modality_trigger_point_therapy",
        "concept_originates",

        "concept_insertion"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 159",
      "distractors": [],
      "img": "extensor_digitorum_action.png",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "Extensor digitorum extends the fingers.",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "modality_trigger_point_therapy",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question": "What is the main function of extensor digitorum?",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/forearm_extensors",
      "answers": [
        "Flex the fingers",
        "Extend the fingers",
        "Abduct the thumb",
        "Oppose the thumb"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Trigger point therapy in this muscle can relieve hand stiffness and improve finger range of motion.",
      "long_explanation": "Extensor digitorum originates from the lateral epicondyle and inserts into the extensor expansions of digits 2–5, enabling extension at the MCP and IP joints.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Forearm Extensors 003",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",

      ],
      "img": "extensor_carpi_radialis_brevis.png",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "ECRB is the most commonly involved muscle in lateral epicondylitis.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/forearm_extensors",
      "question": "Which forearm extensor is most lateral and often used for wrist stabilization?",
      "answers": [
        "Extensor carpi ulnaris",
        "Extensor digiti minimi",
        "Extensor carpi radialis brevis",
        "Extensor indicis"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "ECRB originates from the lateral epicondyle and inserts on the third metacarpal. It works with ECRL to stabilize the wrist during finger movements.",
      "short_explanation": "Extensor carpi radialis brevis stabilizes the wrist.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "source_reference": "Clinical Massage Therapy, Rattray & Ludwig, p. 492",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Forearm Extensors 004",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "img": "radial_nerve_forearm.png",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "correct": 1,
      "answers": [
        "Median nerve",
        "Radial nerve",
        "Ulnar nerve",
        "Musculocutaneous nerve"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/forearm_extensors",
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy, 7th ed., Plate 435",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the majority of forearm extensors?",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "clinical_application": "Compression or injury can result in wrist drop and impaired extension.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "The radial nerve and its branches supply all the extensor muscles in the posterior compartment of the forearm, enabling wrist and finger extension.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",

        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The radial nerve innervates forearm extensors."
    },
    {
      "id": "Forearm Extensors 005",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "clinical_application": "Ulnar-sided wrist pain may involve ECU tendon sheath inflammation, often managed with rest and massage.",
      "long_explanation": "Originating from the lateral epicondyle and ulna, ECU inserts on the 5th metacarpal and contributes to wrist extension and ulnar deviation.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",

    {
      "id": "Forearm Extensors 006",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Which extensor is specifically involved in extending the index finger?",
      "short_explanation": "Extensor indicis extends the index finger.",
      "img": "extensor_indicis.png",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/forearm_extensors",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Extensor indicis",
        "Extensor digiti minimi",
        "Extensor pollicis longus",
        "Extensor carpi radialis longus"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "clinical_application": "Manual therapy on extensor indicis helps relieve digital strain in repetitive motion disorders.",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 161",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "correct": 0,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "This muscle lies deep and medial to extensor digitorum and allows isolated extension of the index finger, important for pointing and precision tasks.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "category": "Anatomy"
    },
    {

      "long_explanation": "EPL allows full extension of the thumb, crucial for grasp and release movements, and forms part of the anatomical snuffbox.",
      "img": "extensor_pollicis_longus.png"
    },
    {
      "id": "Forearm Extensors 008",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "Flexing pinky",
        "Abducting wrist",
        "Extending pinky",
        "Opposing thumb"
      ],
      "question": "Which movement would most involve extensor digiti minimi?",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed., p. 160",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "EDM is a slender muscle that assists with isolated extension of the fifth digit and is often engaged during grip release or expressive hand gestures.",

        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted"

      "clinical_application": "Strain in EDM may be observed in musicians or typists due to repetitive finger extension.",

    {
      "id": "Forearm Extensors 009",
      "keywords": [

      "category": "Anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "EPB and APL form the lateral snuffbox wall.",
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question": "Which two muscles form the lateral boundary of the anatomical snuffbox?",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/forearm_extensors",
      "answers": [
        "EPL and EDM",
        "EPB and APL",
        "ED and ECU",
        "FCR and FDS"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "img": "anatomical_snuffbox.png",
      "long_explanation": "The anatomical snuffbox is bordered laterally by the tendons of extensor pollicis brevis and abductor pollicis longus, and medially by extensor pollicis longus. This area houses the radial artery.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "clinical_application": "Palpation here helps diagnose tendinopathies and assess radial artery pulse.",
      "correct": 1,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "stats": {

      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Forearm Extensors 010",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question": "Which extensor muscle is most commonly associated with tennis elbow?",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "answers": [
        "Extensor carpi ulnaris",
        "Extensor digitorum",
        "Extensor carpi radialis brevis",
        "Extensor pollicis brevis"
      ],

      },
      "subcategory": "Upper Limb",

        "concept_originated"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Lateral epicondylitis is commonly caused by repetitive strain of the ECRB muscle, which originates near the lateral epicondyle and stabilizes the wrist during gripping.",
      "clinical_application": "Soft tissue massage, stretching, and eccentric loading are effective treatments for ECRB-related pain.",
      "source_reference": "Clinical Massage Therapy, Rattray & Ludwig, p. 495",
      "img": "ecrb_tennis_elbow.png",
      "short_explanation": "ECRB is most involved in tennis elbow."
    },
