    {
      "id": "Hand Flexors 5064a75d",
      "long_explanation": "Pronator teres not only pronates the forearm but also assists in elbow flexion. It belongs to the superficial anterior forearm group.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Pronator teres is part of the flexor-pronator group.",

      "question": "Flexor-pronator muscle group includes which of the following?",

    {
      "id": "Hand Flexors 51718383",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "synonyms": [
        "forearm flexors",
        "anterior forearm muscles"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Palpation of this muscle is useful for identifying tendon orientation during massage or rehab settings.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/hand_flexors_mcqs",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "Though minor in function, this muscle helps tense the palm and assists wrist flexion. It’s often used in surgical tendon grafts.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th Ed.",
      "subcategory": "Muscle Groups",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "Palmaris longus tenses the palmar aponeurosis.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "correct": 0,
      "question": "Which muscle helps tense the palmar fascia and is considered functionally minor?",
      "answers": [
        "Palmaris longus",
        "Flexor digitorum superficialis",
        "Pronator teres",
        "Flexor pollicis brevis"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Hand Flexors 70087b43",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "question": "Which muscle is primarily responsible for flexing the wrist and is often targeted in forearm massage?",
      "answers": [
        "Flexor carpi radialis",
        "Extensor digitorum",
        "Brachioradialis",
        "Supinator"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The flexor carpi radialis originates from the medial epicondyle of the humerus and inserts into the base of the second and third metacarpals. It flexes and abducts the wrist. It's commonly tight in clients who type or grip repetitively.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "subcategory": "Muscle Groups",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "clinical_application": "Massage targeting the flexor carpi radialis can relieve tension in clients with repetitive wrist strain from office work or lifting.",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th Ed.",
      "short_explanation": "Flexor carpi radialis flexes the wrist and is commonly addressed in massage for wrist pain.",
      "synonyms": [
        "forearm flexors",
        "anterior forearm muscles"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "correct": 0,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_stress",

        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "concept_targeted",
        "concept_tension",
        "bone_humerus",
        "bone_metacarpals"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/hand_flexors_mcqs",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [

    {
      "id": "Hand Flexors 78b3000e",
      "subcategory": "Muscle Groups",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Its tendon is often used in tendon graft surgeries, and massage therapists should note asymmetry during palpation.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Palmaris longus",
        "Flexor carpi ulnaris",
        "Flexor digitorum profundus",
        "Flexor pollicis longus"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th Ed.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "short_explanation": "Palmaris longus is congenitally absent in some individuals.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/hand_flexors_mcqs",
      "long_explanation": "Palmaris longus is a superficial flexor muscle absent in 10–15% of people. It assists in wrist flexion but is not essential.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted"
      ],
      "question": "Which hand flexor is often absent in a portion of the population?",
      "correct": 0,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "synonyms": [
        "forearm flexors",
        "anterior forearm muscles"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Hand Flexors B2f795e5",
      "clinical_application": "Massage techniques focusing on forearm flexors help reduce RSI in office workers.",
      "synonyms": [
        "forearm flexors",
        "anterior forearm muscles"
      ],
      "question": "Which activity most likely overworks the flexor digitorum muscles?",
      "short_explanation": "Typing overuses finger flexors like flexor digitorum muscles.",
      "correct": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "modality_massage_techniques",
        "concept_focuses"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Muscle Groups",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "modality_massage_techniques",
        "concept_focuses"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Sustained finger flexion during typing can fatigue the flexor digitorum superficialis and profundus, leading to muscle imbalance or RSI.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th Ed.",
      "answers": [
        "Typing for extended hours",
        "Running",
        "Breathing exercises",
        "Leg curls"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/hand_flexors_mcqs"
    },
    {
      "id": "Hand Flexors F19501e1",
      "clinical_application": "Massage can reduce finger stiffness and improve hand function in clients with overuse injuries or musicians.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th Ed.",
      "subcategory": "Muscle Groups",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "long_explanation": "Flexor digitorum superficialis inserts into the middle phalanges of digits 2–5 and flexes the PIP joints. It also assists with wrist flexion.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "short_explanation": "It flexes the PIP joints of the fingers.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [

      ],
      "question": "The flexor digitorum superficialis primarily acts on which structure?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Hand Flexors F680ac8f",
      "stats": {

      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "short_explanation": "Overuse of flexor pollicis longus leads to thumb flexion pain.",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "answers": [
        "Thumb pain during flexion",
        "Elbow weakness",
        "Shoulder tightness",
        "Swelling in the upper arm"
      ],
      "correct": 0,
      "long_explanation": "Flexor pollicis longus flexes the thumb’s interphalangeal joint. Repetitive use can cause pain or trigger thumb issues.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/hand_flexors_mcqs",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th Ed.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "subcategory": "Muscle Groups",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "question": "What is a common clinical symptom of overuse of the flexor pollicis longus?",
      "clinical_application": "Massage can alleviate pain in massage therapists, gamers, or clients who text frequently.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "synonyms": [
        "forearm flexors",
        "anterior forearm muscles"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Hand Flexors Fa59b09d",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Ulnar nerve",
        "Median nerve",
        "Radial nerve",
        "Musculocutaneous nerve"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Flexor carpi ulnaris is unique among hand flexors because it is innervated by which nerve?",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th Ed.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Flexor carpi ulnaris is innervated by the ulnar nerve.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_pain",
        "client",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_pain",
        "client",
        "condition_discomfort",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Muscle Groups",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/hand_flexors_mcqs",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "It’s one of the few anterior forearm muscles not innervated by the median nerve. It aids in wrist flexion and ulnar deviation.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "clinical_application": "Massage may help clients with ulnar neuropathy or medial wrist discomfort.",
      "correct": 0,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "synonyms": [
        "forearm flexors",
        "anterior forearm muscles"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Hand Flexors Ff277ea7",
      "clinical_application": "Understanding innervation helps target massage for nerve impingement symptoms like carpal tunnel syndrome.",
      "subcategory": "Muscle Groups",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_targeted",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_axillary_nerve",
        "bone_carpals"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Median nerve",
        "Radial nerve",
        "Ulnar nerve",
        "Axillary nerve"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The median nerve innervates most forearm flexors.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",

        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",

        "nerve_axillary_nerve",
        "bone_carpals"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th Ed.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "synonyms": [
        "forearm flexors",
        "anterior forearm muscles"
      ],
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.084Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/movement groups/hand_flexors_mcqs",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "Which nerve innervates most of the anterior forearm flexor muscles?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.427Z",
      "long_explanation": "The median nerve supplies the majority of the hand flexors except for flexor carpi ulnaris and part of flexor digitorum profundus (ulnar nerve)."
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 001",
      "long_explanation": "Hyaline cartilage is found in the nose, trachea, larynx, ends of long bones, and the fetal skeleton. It provides smooth surfaces for joint movement and support with flexibility.",
      "answers": [
        "Fibrocartilage",
        "Elastic cartilage",
        "Hyaline cartilage",
        "Articular cartilage"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Principles of Anatomy and Physiology by Tortora",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "joint_joint",
        "organ_trachea",
        "organ_nose"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Hyaline cartilage is the most abundant type.",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 2,
      "img": "hyaline_cartilage.png",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "question": "Which type of cartilage is most abundant in the human body?",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "joint_joint",
        "organ_trachea",
        "organ_nose"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "synonyms": [
        "articular cartilage"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Degeneration of hyaline cartilage in joints is a key factor in osteoarthritis.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "category": "Connective Tissue",
      "difficulty": "easy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 002",
      "short_explanation": "Fibrocartilage is found in intervertebral discs.",
      "synonyms": [
        "IVD cartilage"
      ],
      "category": "Connective Tissue",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "Elastic cartilage",
        "Hyaline cartilage",
        "Fibrocartilage",
        "Costal cartilage"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Disc herniations often involve damage to fibrocartilage.",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_compression",
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_tension"
      ],
      "img": "fibrocartilage_disc.png",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "Fibrocartilage is the toughest cartilage, containing densely packed collagen fibers. It is found in intervertebral discs, pubic symphysis, and menisci, providing resistance to compression and tension.",
      "tags": [
        "technique_compression",
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_tension"
      ],
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "source_reference": "Clinically Oriented Anatomy by Moore",
      "question": "Which cartilage type is found in the intervertebral discs?",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology"
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 003",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "clinical_application": "Elastic cartilage maintains the shape of soft tissues and can be damaged in trauma (e.g. cauliflower ear).",
      "long_explanation": "Elastic cartilage contains elastin fibers in addition to collagen, allowing flexibility. It is found in the external ear, epiglottis, and Eustachian tube.",
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "correct": 1,
      "short_explanation": "Elastic cartilage is found in flexible structures like the ear.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "organ_ears"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "img": "elastic_cartilage_ear.png",
      "synonyms": [
        "auricular cartilage"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "category": "Connective Tissue",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "organ_ears"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Fibrocartilage",
        "Elastic cartilage",
        "Hyaline cartilage",
        "Articular cartilage"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question": "Which type of cartilage is found in the external ear and epiglottis?",
      "difficulty_rating": 3
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 004",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "synonyms": [
        "articular cartilage"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Hyaline cartilage has a glossy, smooth appearance.",
      "tags": [
        "technique_friction",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "answers": [

        "Hyaline cartilage",
        "Dense regular connective tissue"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "clinical_application": "The slick nature of hyaline cartilage is essential for joint articulation.",
      "question": "Which cartilage type appears glassy and translucent under a microscope?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "long_explanation": "Hyaline cartilage gets its name from the Greek 'hyalos' meaning glass. Its matrix is translucent, providing smooth, low-friction surfaces in joints.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "img": "hyaline_glassy_microscope.png",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "source_reference": "Ross Histology Textbook",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "technique_friction",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "category": "Connective Tissue"
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 005",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "long_explanation": "Due to dense collagen fiber content, fibrocartilage is highly resistant to stretching and compression, making it ideal for load-bearing areas like intervertebral discs and menisci.",
      "correct": 1,
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "synonyms": [
        "fibrous cartilage"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Fibrocartilage has the greatest tensile strength.",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "clinical_application": "Meniscal injuries in the knee involve fibrocartilage and are common in athletes.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "img": "fibrocartilage_strength.png",
      "source_reference": "Essentials of Musculoskeletal Care",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Which type of cartilage has the highest tensile strength?",
      "category": "Connective Tissue",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Hyaline cartilage",
        "Fibrocartilage",
        "Elastic cartilage",
        "Spongy bone"
      ],

      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",

      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "tags": [

        "anatomy_bones",

      ],
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 006",
      "img": "fetal_skeleton_hyaline.png",
      "synonyms": [
        "cartilage template"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_bones"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "category": "Connective Tissue",
      "long_explanation": "Hyaline cartilage provides a model for the fetal skeleton, which later undergoes endochondral ossification to become bone. It allows for early development with flexibility and minimal mass.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_bones"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "question": "Which type of cartilage forms the fetal skeleton before ossification?",
      "answers": [
        "Elastic cartilage",
        "Hyaline cartilage",
        "Fibrocartilage",
        "Compact bone"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "clinical_application": "Understanding cartilage templates is key in developmental disorders and orthopedic surgery in children.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "source_reference": "Langman’s Medical Embryology",
      "short_explanation": "Hyaline cartilage forms the early fetal skeleton.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 007",
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "Nasal septum",
        "Meniscus of the knee",
        "Auricle of the ear",
        "Costal cartilage"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "clinical_application": "Auricular trauma can lead to cartilage deformation, as seen in wrestlers with 'cauliflower ear.'",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "tags": [
        "bone_nasal",
        "organ_ears"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "Elastic cartilage gives the outer ear its shape and flexibility. It contains elastin fibers that make it more flexible than hyaline or fibrocartilage.",
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Connective Tissue",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Which of the following structures contains elastic cartilage?",
      "img": "auricle_elastic_cartilage.png",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "source_reference": "Netter’s Atlas of Human Anatomy",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "bone_nasal",
        "organ_ears"
      ],
      "synonyms": [
        "outer ear cartilage"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The auricle (outer ear) contains elastic cartilage."
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 008",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "tags": [
        "technique_compression",

      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "source_reference": "Clinically Oriented Anatomy by Moore",
      "category": "Connective Tissue",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Which type of cartilage is most resistant to compression?",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "Fibrocartilage resists compression due to its dense matrix.",
      "correct": 2,
      "img": "fibrocartilage_compression.png",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "long_explanation": "Fibrocartilage has tightly packed collagen fibers that give it exceptional resistance to compression and tension. It's ideal for shock-absorbing structures like the meniscus and pubic symphysis.",
      "clinical_application": "Injury or degeneration of fibrocartilage is common in joints subjected to high loads.",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "concept_tension",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Elastic cartilage",
        "Hyaline cartilage",
        "Fibrocartilage",
        "Loose connective tissue"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "synonyms": [
        "dense cartilage"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 009",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "synonyms": [
        "rib connector cartilage"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_bones",
        "bone_sternum",
        "bone_ribs"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "Costal cartilage is a type of hyaline cartilage.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_bones",
        "bone_sternum",
        "bone_ribs"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "Hyaline cartilage in costal regions provides both flexibility and support, allowing the ribcage to expand during respiration.",
      "question": "Costal cartilage, which connects ribs to the sternum, is made of what type of cartilage?",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "source_reference": "Tortora Anatomy & Physiology",
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "answers": [
        "Elastic cartilage",
        "Fibrocartilage",
        "Hyaline cartilage",
        "Spongy bone"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "category": "Connective Tissue",
      "img": "costal_hyaline_cartilage.png",
      "clinical_application": "Calcification of costal cartilage with age can reduce chest wall compliance."
    },
    {
      "id": "Cartilage 010",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "purpose_healing",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "source_reference": "Essentials of Musculoskeletal Care",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/cartilage",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "long_explanation": "Hyaline cartilage lacks blood vessels and has a limited capacity for repair. Injuries often result in fibrocartilage formation rather than true hyaline tissue.",
      "answers": [
        "Elastic cartilage",
        "Hyaline cartilage",
        "Fibrocartilage",
        "All regenerate equally"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "subcategory": "Cartilage Types",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.428Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.085Z",
      "category": "Connective Tissue",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "Hyaline cartilage has poor regenerative capacity.",
      "correct": 1,
      "img": "hyaline_cartilage_regeneration.png",
      "question": "Which cartilage type is least likely to regenerate after injury?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "purpose_healing",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "synonyms": [
        "joint repair"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 4,
      "difficulty": "hard",
      "clinical_application": "Joint cartilage injuries often require surgical interventions like microfracture or grafting due to poor natural healing.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 001",
      "correct": 2,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "Connective tissue forms the structural framework of the body and plays a role in support, insulation, protection, and transport. It connects muscles to bones, cushions organs, and forms tendons, ligaments, cartilage, and bone. It is characterized by an extracellular matrix made of fibers and ground substance.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "Connective tissue supports, binds, and protects other body structures.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "system_digestive"
      ],
      "question": "What is the primary function of connective tissue?",
      "answers": [
        "Conduct electrical impulses",
        "Line body cavities",
        "Support, bind, and protect other tissues",
        "Secrete digestive enzymes"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "system_digestive"
      ],
      "applied_example": "Massage therapists work directly with connective tissues like fascia and indirectly with ligaments and tendons to improve mobility, reduce adhesions, and enhance postural support.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "intermediate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 002",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "long_explanation": "Connective tissue comes in many forms including adipose, bone, blood, cartilage, and fibrous tissue. Adipose tissue stores energy, cushions organs, and helps maintain body temperature. Unlike epithelial or muscle tissues, it contains fewer cells and more extracellular material.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "Adipose tissue is a type of connective tissue specialized in fat storage and insulation.",
      "applied_example": "Massage improves circulation in adipose-rich areas, supporting tissue hydration and lymphatic flow, especially during relaxation or detoxifying bodywork sessions.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z",
      "answers": [
        "Epithelial tissue",
        "Muscle tissue",
        "Adipose tissue",
        "Neural tissue"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_bone",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_bone",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "question": "Which of the following is a type of connective tissue?",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "correct": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 003",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "DNA and proteins",
        "Plasma and organelles",
        "Fibers and ground substance",
        "Cholesterol and enzymes"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The extracellular matrix is made of fibers (collagen, elastin) and ground substance.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "The ECM in connective tissue consists of protein fibers like collagen (for strength), elastin (for flexibility), and reticular fibers (for support), suspended in a ground substance. This matrix provides structural integrity and transmits mechanical signals to cells.",
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "What is the extracellular matrix in connective tissue composed of?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_organelle",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "concept_integration",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "anatomy_dna"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_organelle",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "concept_integration",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "anatomy_dna"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "applied_example": "Massage techniques such as myofascial release aim to influence the ECM, improving glide between tissue layers and restoring healthy movement patterns.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "anatomy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 004",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "applied_example": "Massage over adipose-dense areas should be done with care to avoid excessive pressure while still stimulating circulation and relaxation.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "Adipose tissue is a loose connective tissue where fat-storing cells (adipocytes) are dominant. It acts as a thermal insulator, energy reservoir, and physical cushion for organs like the kidneys and eyes.",
      "correct": 1,
      "short_explanation": "Adipose tissue cushions organs and stores energy in the form of fat.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Which connective tissue acts as a fat storage site and cushions organs?",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Cartilage",
        "Adipose tissue",
        "Areolar tissue",
        "Dense regular tissue"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z",
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_kidneys",
        "organ_eyes"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_kidneys",
        "organ_eyes"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 005",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Ligaments",
        "Cartilage",
        "Tendons",
        "Adipose"
      ],
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "short_explanation": "Tendons are dense connective tissues that connect muscle to bone.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Tendons are composed primarily of parallel collagen fibers, providing high tensile strength. They transmit force from muscle contractions to bone, allowing movement. Unlike ligaments, they connect different types of tissues (muscle to bone).",
      "question": "Which connective tissue type connects muscle to bone?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_bone",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_bone",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "applied_example": "Massage therapists often work near tendon insertions to reduce muscle tension and prevent strain-related injuries such as tendinitis.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "anatomy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 006",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "applied_example": "Massage near ligaments should avoid overstretching or deep pressure. Work on adjacent muscles can support joint stability and improve proprioception.",
      "long_explanation": "Ligaments are dense regular connective tissue bands rich in collagen that stabilize and limit joint movement. They resist tensile stress and help prevent dislocation. Ligaments heal more slowly due to limited blood supply.",
      "short_explanation": "Ligaments connect bone to bone and stabilize joints.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_healing",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Muscle to bone",
        "Bone to bone",
        "Bone to cartilage",
        "Muscle to muscle"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z",
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "question": "Ligaments connect:",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_healing",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 007",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Which type of connective tissue is found in the dermis of the skin and provides strength in multiple directions?",
      "applied_example": "Deep tissue massage and myofascial techniques help mobilize this layer, improving skin flexibility and reducing fascial restrictions that may limit range of motion.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "correct": 0,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "Found in the dermis and organ capsules, dense irregular connective tissue contains interwoven collagen fibers that allow resistance to stress from multiple directions. It is critical for skin resilience and deep fascial integrity.",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Dense irregular connective tissue",
        "Loose connective tissue",
        "Cartilage",
        "Compact bone"
      ],
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Dense irregular connective tissue gives skin its multidirectional strength.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "organ_skin"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 008",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "condition_injury"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Fibroblasts are the main cell type in most connective tissues. They synthesize and maintain the extracellular matrix by producing collagen and elastin fibers. They play a key role in tissue repair and remodeling after injury.",
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Fibroblasts produce collagen, elastin, and other fibers in connective tissues.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "condition_injury"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "What cell type is primarily responsible for producing connective tissue fibers?",
      "answers": [
        "Osteocytes",
        "Chondrocytes",
        "Fibroblasts",
        "Erythrocytes"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "applied_example": "Massage may influence fibroblast activity through mechanical stimulation, aiding tissue regeneration and the breakdown of excess scar tissue over time."
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 009",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "correct": 2,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "Connective tissue fibers include collagen (strength), elastin (flexibility), and reticular fibers (support). Keratin, though fibrous, is an epithelial protein not part of connective tissue. It forms protective structures like the epidermis and fingernails.",
      "applied_example": "Massage therapists should know the difference between structural proteins. Keratinized areas (e.g., calluses) may require different pressure than soft connective tissue zones.",
      "question": "Which of the following is NOT a fiber found in connective tissue?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Keratin is not a connective tissue fiber; it is found in skin, hair, and nails.",
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "answers": [
        "Collagen",
        "Elastin",
        "Keratin",
        "Reticular"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue 010",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "anatomy_matrix"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Bone tissue is a mineralized connective tissue that supports and protects the body.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.086Z",
      "answers": [
        "Cartilage",
        "Bone tissue",
        "Adipose tissue",
        "Blood"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question": "Which connective tissue has a solid, mineralized matrix and supports the body structurally?",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "Bone is a specialized connective tissue composed of a solid matrix of collagen fibers and calcium phosphate minerals. It provides structure, produces blood cells, and stores minerals. It constantly remodels under the influence of stress and load.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "applied_example": "Massage does not target bone directly, but by influencing surrounding connective tissues and reducing muscle tension, it supports skeletal alignment and joint function.",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 1,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "anatomy_matrix"
      ],
      "unit": "connective tissue",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z"
    },
    {

    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 001",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "synonyms": [
        "muscle cell membrane"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_cytoplasm",
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "anatomy_plasma_membrane",
        "anatomy_component"
      ],
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "question": "What is the sarcolemma?",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_cytoplasm",
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_matrix",
        "anatomy_plasma_membrane",
        "anatomy_component"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Damage to the sarcolemma is a hallmark of muscular dystrophies, especially Duchenne’s.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 2,
      "answers": [
        "The contractile unit of muscle",
        "The cytoplasm of muscle fiber",
        "The cell membrane of a muscle fiber",
        "The protein component of muscle"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "long_explanation": "The sarcolemma is the specialized plasma membrane surrounding a muscle fiber. It plays a critical role in conducting action potentials and linking to the extracellular matrix.",
      "short_explanation": "The sarcolemma is the muscle cell membrane.",
      "source_reference": "Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology",
      "img": "sarcolemma_structure.png",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 002",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "answers": [
        "Myofibrils",
        "Sarcoplasmic reticulum",
        "T-tubules",
        "Z-discs"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "img": "t_tubules_sarcolemma.png",
      "synonyms": [
        "transverse tubules"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "T-tubules are invaginations of the sarcolemma.",
      "question": "What structure within the sarcolemma helps transmit action potentials into the muscle fiber?",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "Transverse tubules (T-tubules) are extensions of the sarcolemma that penetrate into the muscle cell, allowing depolarization to reach internal regions and initiate calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "clinical_application": "Defective T-tubules may contribute to cardiac arrhythmias and muscle weakness.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 003",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "synonyms": [
        "structural protein"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_matrix"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Actin",
        "Myosin",
        "Dystrophin",
        "Troponin"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Mutations in the dystrophin gene cause Duchenne muscular dystrophy, leading to sarcolemma breakdown and muscle degeneration.",
      "question": "Which of the following proteins is critical to maintaining sarcolemma integrity?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_matrix"
      ],
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "source_reference": "Molecular Biology of the Cell, Alberts",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "Dystrophin stabilizes the sarcolemma.",
      "long_explanation": "Dystrophin connects the internal cytoskeleton of muscle fibers to the sarcolemma and extracellular matrix. It prevents tearing during contraction.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma",
      "img": "dystrophin_sarcolemma.png",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 004",
      "source_reference": "Principles of Anatomy and Physiology by Tortora",
      "difficulty_rating": 4,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_organelle",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "anatomy_component"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "Calcium storage occurs in the sarcoplasmic reticulum, not sarcolemma.",
      "long_explanation": "While the sarcolemma conducts signals and interfaces with connective tissue and T-tubules, calcium is stored in the sarcoplasmic reticulum, an internal organelle.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "answers": [
        "Transmission of action potentials",
        "Attachment to connective tissue",
        "Calcium storage",
        "Initiation of muscle contraction"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "clinical_application": "Distinguishing sarcolemma from other muscle components is key in interpreting muscle biopsies.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "img": "sarcolemma_vs_sarcoplasmic_reticulum.png",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "synonyms": [
        "membrane role"
      ],
      "difficulty": "hard",
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "question": "Which function is NOT directly associated with the sarcolemma?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_organelle",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "anatomy_component"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 005",
      "synonyms": [
        "fiber wrapping"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "correct": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "question": "The sarcolemma is continuous with which extracellular structure?",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "The sarcolemma connects with the endomysium.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma",
      "img": "sarcolemma_endomysium.png",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "This connection is essential for distributing contraction force and maintaining muscle structure.",
      "source_reference": "Clinically Oriented Anatomy by Moore",
      "long_explanation": "The sarcolemma interfaces with the endomysium, a connective tissue layer that supports muscle fibers and transmits force during contraction.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "answers": [
        "Endomysium",
        "Epimysium",
        "Perimysium",
        "Myofibrils"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 006",
      "synonyms": [
        "Na+ channel"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Sodium channels initiate muscle action potentials.",
      "clinical_application": "Defects in sodium channels are linked to muscle channelopathies like periodic paralysis.",
      "question": "Which ion channel embedded in the sarcolemma is essential for action potential propagation?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.429Z",
      "source_reference": "Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology",
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "img": "sodium_channels_sarcolemma.png",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Voltage-gated sodium channels in the sarcolemma depolarize the membrane and initiate action potentials, which lead to muscle contraction.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "answers": [
        "Calcium channel",
        "Sodium channel",
        "Potassium pump",
        "Chloride channel"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 1
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 007",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "immunology_antibodies",
        "concept_targeted"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "question": "What is the main function of the motor end plate located on the sarcolemma?",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "immunology_antibodies",
        "concept_targeted"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "source_reference": "Clinically Oriented Anatomy by Moore",
      "answers": [
        "Act as a calcium reservoir",
        "Initiate contraction through myosin",
        "Receive neurotransmitters from neurons",
        "Anchor myofibrils to the membrane"
      ],
      "img": "motor_end_plate_sarcolemma.png",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 2,
      "long_explanation": "The motor end plate is a specialized region of the sarcolemma that receives acetylcholine from motor neurons, triggering depolarization and muscle contraction.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "short_explanation": "The motor end plate receives acetylcholine to trigger contraction.",
      "synonyms": [
        "ACh receptor site"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "clinical_application": "This site is targeted in myasthenia gravis, where antibodies block acetylcholine receptors.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "anatomy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 008",
      "synonyms": [
        "membrane pits"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Molecular Cell Biology by Lodish",

      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "clinical_application": "Defective caveolae proteins are linked to muscle dystrophies and lipodystrophy syndromes.",
      "question": "What is the role of caveolae in the sarcolemma?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "img": "caveolae_sarcolemma.png",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "answers": [
        "Produce ATP",
        "Aid in calcium release",
        "Facilitate signal transduction and membrane repair",
        "Store glycogen"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "short_explanation": "Caveolae are involved in signaling and membrane repair.",
      "long_explanation": "Caveolae are small invaginations in the sarcolemma that contain signaling molecules and play a key role in mechanosensing and membrane resealing after microinjury.",
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 009",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty": "hard",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "long_explanation": "Duchenne muscular dystrophy is a genetic disorder where lack of dystrophin weakens the sarcolemma, causing muscle fiber damage and degeneration.",
      "correct": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "This condition underscores the importance of sarcolemma stability in muscular health.",
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "question": "Which disease is primarily caused by mutations in the dystrophin gene affecting the sarcolemma?",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "img": "duchenne_dystrophy_sarcolemma.png",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "synonyms": [
        "DMD"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Duchenne muscular dystrophy results from dystrophin defects.",
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "Multiple sclerosis",
        "Myasthenia gravis",
        "Duchenne muscular dystrophy",
        "Polymyositis"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 4,
      "source_reference": "Muscle Biopsy: A Practical Approach"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcolemma 010",
      "question": "Which of the following best describes the electrical property of the sarcolemma?",
      "answers": [
        "It is impermeable to all ions",
        "It acts as a passive barrier only",
        "It maintains resting membrane potential and conducts impulses",
        "It stores neurotransmitters"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Alterations in membrane potential are seen in disorders like hyperkalemic periodic paralysis.",
      "source_reference": "Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology",
      "short_explanation": "The sarcolemma helps maintain membrane potential and conducts action potentials.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "long_explanation": "The sarcolemma has voltage-gated ion channels that generate and propagate action potentials. It maintains ion gradients essential for muscle excitability.",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "subcategory": "Sarcolemma",
      "synonyms": [
        "muscle electrical membrane"
      ],
      "img": "sarcolemma_membrane_potential.png",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_membrane_potential"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcolemma",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.087Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",

      "keywords": [
