      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 001",
      "long_explanation": "Sarcomeres are the repeating structural and functional units of a myofibril, responsible for muscle contraction. They are composed of thick (myosin) and thin (actin) filaments organized between two Z-lines.",
      "correct": 1,
      "distractors": [],
      "synonyms": [
        "muscle unit"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Myofibril",
        "Sarcomere",
        "Myosin",
        "Actin"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "clinical_application": "Understanding sarcomere mechanics is essential in diagnosing and treating muscular dystrophies.",
      "short_explanation": "The sarcomere is the basic contractile unit.",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "img": "sarcomere_structure.png",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "source_reference": "Essentials of Musculoskeletal Care",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "question": "What is the basic contractile unit of a skeletal muscle?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 002",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "long_explanation": "During contraction, the thin filaments slide over thick filaments, shortening the I band and H zone. The A band remains constant in length because thick filaments do not change length.",
      "answers": [
        "A band only",
        "I band and H zone",
        "Z disc",
        "M line"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "anatomy_zones"
      ],
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "img": "sarcomere_contraction.png",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "short_explanation": "The I band and H zone shorten during contraction.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "clinical_application": "Sarcomere sliding is the basis of the sliding filament theory, essential in rehabilitation therapies.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "correct": 1,
      "synonyms": [
        "sarcomere bands"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "anatomy_zones"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question": "Which bands of the sarcomere change length during muscle contraction?",
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 003",
      "source_reference": "Molecular Biology of the Cell, Alberts",
      "correct": 3,
      "clinical_application": "Mutations in myosin genes are associated with certain types of myopathies.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_protein"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "short_explanation": "Thick filaments are composed of myosin.",
      "long_explanation": "Myosin molecules form the thick filaments of the sarcomere and have heads that bind to actin to generate force during contraction. Their ATPase activity powers the sliding filament process.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "question": "What proteins make up the thick filaments in a sarcomere?",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "synonyms": [
        "motor protein"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_protein"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "img": "myosin_thick_filament.png",
      "answers": [
        "Actin",
        "Tropomyosin",
        "Troponin",
        "Myosin"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 004",
      "synonyms": [
        "Z line"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "What is the function of the Z disc in a sarcomere?",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "answers": [
        "Anchors thick filaments",
        "Separates sarcomeres",
        "Forms the M line",
        "Contains myosin heads"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "long_explanation": "Z discs are dense protein structures that anchor the actin filaments and mark the boundaries between sarcomeres. They play a critical role in transmitting force along the myofibril.",
      "clinical_application": "Z-disc integrity is important in muscle diseases such as desmin-related myopathy.",
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "concept_integration"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "source_reference": "Principles of Anatomy and Physiology by Tortora",
      "short_explanation": "The Z disc separates adjacent sarcomeres.",
      "img": "z_disc_sarcomere.png",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "correct": 1,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "concept_integration"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 005",
      "question": "Which protein helps restore sarcomere length after stretching and acts like a molecular spring?",
      "difficulty": "hard",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "Titin is a large elastic protein that extends from the Z disc to the M line, contributing to passive elasticity and helping sarcomeres return to resting length after contraction or stretch.",
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "synonyms": [
        "connectin"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Mutations in titin are linked to cardiomyopathies and muscle stiffness.",
      "correct": 1,
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 4,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_healing",
        "concept_restoration"
      ],
      "img": "titin_sarcomere_spring.png",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "source_reference": "Medical Physiology by Boron & Boulpaep",
      "short_explanation": "Titin acts as a spring to maintain sarcomere integrity.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Myosin",
        "Titin",
        "Actin",
        "Nebulin"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_healing",
        "concept_restoration"
      ],
      "category": "Muscle Physiology"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 006",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "Which band of the sarcomere contains only actin filaments?",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "anatomy_zones"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "img": "i_band_sarcomere.png",
      "distractors": [],
      "clinical_application": "Changes in I band length are useful markers in studies of muscle contraction and disease.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "anatomy_zones"
      ],
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "source_reference": "Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "A band",
        "H zone",
        "I band",
        "Z disc"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The I band appears lighter under a microscope and includes only thin filaments (actin). It shortens during muscle contraction as filaments slide past each other.",
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "synonyms": [
        "light band"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The I band contains only thin (actin) filaments."
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 007",
      "difficulty_rating": 4,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "long_explanation": "Located in the center of the A band and H zone, the M line consists of proteins that stabilize and anchor the thick filaments, ensuring proper alignment during contraction.",
      "short_explanation": "The M line anchors thick (myosin) filaments at the sarcomere center.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_zones"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "img": "m_line_sarcomere.png",
      "answers": [
        "Z line",
        "M line",
        "I band",
        "H zone"
      ],
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Disruption of M line proteins may result in myopathies and reduced contractile efficiency.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_zones"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "hard",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "source_reference": "Netter’s Atlas of Human Anatomy",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "synonyms": [
        "middle line"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "correct": 1,
      "question": "Which line in the sarcomere anchors thick filaments and defines the center of the A band?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 008",
      "img": "sarcomere_bands.png",
      "source_reference": "Principles of Anatomy and Physiology by Tortora",
      "clinical_application": "These changes can be visualized in histological studies of muscle pathology.",
      "correct": 3,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "science_histology",
        "anatomy_zones"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "science_histology",
        "anatomy_zones"
      ],
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "short_explanation": "The I band and H zone shorten; A band stays the same.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "answers": [
        "I band",
        "H zone",
        "Z disc",
        "Both I band and H zone"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "question": "Which structure shortens during muscle contraction but not the A band?",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "long_explanation": "As thin filaments slide toward the center of the sarcomere during contraction, both the I band and H zone decrease in length, while the A band (length of thick filaments) remains unchanged.",
      "synonyms": [
        "contractile bands"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 009",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which protein regulates actin-myosin binding by covering binding sites on actin?",
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Muscle Physiology",
      "long_explanation": "In resting muscle, tropomyosin lies along the actin filament, preventing myosin from binding. Upon calcium binding to troponin, tropomyosin shifts, allowing contraction to occur.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_muscles"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "img": "tropomyosin_actin_binding.png",
      "short_explanation": "Tropomyosin blocks myosin-binding sites on actin.",
      "source_reference": "Molecular Biology of the Cell, Alberts",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "synonyms": [
        "actin blocker"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Abnormal tropomyosin expression is implicated in some cardiomyopathies and skeletal muscle disorders.",
      "answers": [
        "Titin",
        "Nebulin",
        "Tropomyosin",
        "Calmodulin"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Sarcomere 010",
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "What is the role of calcium in sarcomere contraction?",
      "difficulty": "hard",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "img": "calcium_troponin_binding.png",
      "answers": [
        "Breaks down ATP",
        "Binds to myosin head",
        "Binds to troponin to uncover actin",
        "Inhibits acetylcholine release"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.430Z",
      "synonyms": [
        "calcium signaling"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Guyton and Hall Textbook of Medical Physiology",
      "long_explanation": "Calcium released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum binds to troponin, which causes a conformational change that shifts tropomyosin off actin’s binding sites, enabling myosin binding and contraction.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_targeted"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Sarcomeres",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "concept_targeted"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_rating": 4,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle fascia and connective tissue/sarcomeres",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Calcium binds to troponin, allowing actin-myosin interaction.",
      "clinical_application": "Calcium regulation is critical in muscle physiology and is targeted by drugs for conditions like malignant hyperthermia.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Muscle Physiology"
    },
    {
      "id": "Hand Intrinsic 001",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th Ed.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "category": "Muscle Innervation",
      "clinical_application": "Compression of the median nerve in carpal tunnel syndrome can lead to weakness and atrophy of the thenar eminence.",
      "img": "thenar_muscles_median.png",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "subcategory": "Intrinsic Hand Muscles",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the thenar muscles of the hand?",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve",
        "bone_carpals"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Ulnar nerve",
        "Median nerve",
        "Radial nerve",
        "Musculocutaneous nerve"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_musculocutaneous_nerve",
        "bone_carpals"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The median nerve innervates most of the thenar muscles.",
      "correct": 1,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.431Z",
      "long_explanation": "The thenar muscles, including abductor pollicis brevis, flexor pollicis brevis (superficial head), and opponens pollicis, are innervated by the recurrent branch of the median nerve. These muscles control thumb opposition and fine motor skills.",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "synonyms": [
        "thumb muscles",
        "thenar group"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle groups/intrinsic_hand_muscles",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "topic": "anatomy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Hand Intrinsic 002",
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Muscle Innervation",
      "answers": [
        "Radial nerve",
        "Median nerve",
        "Ulnar nerve",
        "Axillary nerve"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "tags": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "muscle_abductor_digiti_minimi",
        "muscle_flexor_digiti_minimi_brevis",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_axillary_nerve"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The ulnar nerve innervates the hypothenar muscles.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "muscle_abductor_digiti_minimi",
        "muscle_flexor_digiti_minimi_brevis",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve",
        "nerve_radial_nerve",
        "nerve_axillary_nerve"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.431Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Netter’s Atlas of Human Anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "subcategory": "Intrinsic Hand Muscles",
      "long_explanation": "The hypothenar muscles—abductor digiti minimi, flexor digiti minimi brevis, and opponens digiti minimi—are all innervated by the deep branch of the ulnar nerve. They contribute to movements of the little finger.",
      "img": "hypothenar_ulnar.png",
      "clinical_application": "Ulnar nerve compression, such as in Guyon's canal syndrome, may cause atrophy in the hypothenar eminence.",
      "correct": 2,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question": "Which nerve supplies the hypothenar muscles?",
      "synonyms": [
        "pinky muscles"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle groups/intrinsic_hand_muscles"
    },
    {
      "id": "Hand Intrinsic 003",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.088Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "img": "fpb_dual_innervation.png",
      "question": "Which intrinsic hand muscle is innervated by both the ulnar and median nerves?",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle groups/intrinsic_hand_muscles",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve"
      ],
      "synonyms": [
        "FPB"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 4,
      "long_explanation": "The flexor pollicis brevis has a superficial head (median nerve) and a deep head (ulnar nerve), making it one of the few hand muscles with dual innervation.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "source_reference": "Gray's Anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "Flexor pollicis brevis has dual innervation.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 1,
      "subcategory": "Intrinsic Hand Muscles",
      "clinical_application": "Understanding dual innervation is important in evaluating partial nerve injuries affecting thumb flexion.",
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Muscle Innervation",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.431Z",
      "answers": [
        "Adductor pollicis",
        "Flexor pollicis brevis",
        "Opponens pollicis",
        "Lumbricals 1 and 2"
      ],
      "difficulty": "hard",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_median_nerve",
        "nerve_ulnar_nerve"
      ]
    },

      "short_explanation": "The adductor pollicis is innervated by the ulnar nerve.",

      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle groups/intrinsic_hand_muscles",
      "long_explanation": "Despite being in the thumb, the adductor pollicis is not part of the thenar group and is supplied by the deep branch of the ulnar nerve.",
      "answers": [

      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle groups/intrinsic_hand_muscles",
      "stats": {

      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "short_explanation": "Lumbricals 1 and 2 are innervated by the median nerve.",
      "synonyms": [
        "intrinsic muscles"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Lumbricals 1 and 2, attached to the radial side of the index and middle fingers, are innervated by the median nerve. Lumbricals 3 and 4 are innervated by the ulnar nerve.",
      "source_reference": "Moore's Clinically Oriented Anatomy",
      "question": "Which intrinsic hand muscles are innervated by the median nerve?",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "clinical_application": "Loss of function in lumbricals can affect MCP flexion and IP extension.",
      "correct": 2,
      "subcategory": "Intrinsic Hand Muscles",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.431Z",
      "img": "lumbricals_median.png"
    },

      "difficulty": "medium",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which intrinsic hand muscles are responsible for finger abduction and are innervated by the ulnar nerve?",
      "last_answered_at": null,

      "subcategory": "Intrinsic Hand Muscles",

        "nerve_ulnar_nerve"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Loss of ulnar nerve function can impair finger abduction, affecting grip and dexterity.",
      "long_explanation": "The dorsal interossei muscles abduct the fingers away from the midline of the hand and are innervated by the deep branch of the ulnar nerve. There are four dorsal interossei.",

    {

      "img": "palmar_interossei_ulnar.png",
      "long_explanation": "The palmar interossei muscles are responsible for finger adduction and are innervated by the deep branch of the ulnar nerve. These muscles help stabilize the fingers during precision grip.",

      "long_explanation": "The hypothenar group includes the opponens digiti minimi, which allows for opposition of the little finger, and is innervated by the ulnar nerve.",

      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.431Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "long_explanation": "Unlike the superficial head (median nerve), the deep head of flexor pollicis brevis is innervated by the deep branch of the ulnar nerve. This highlights the complexity of thumb muscle innervation.",
      "clinical_application": "In combined nerve injuries, understanding dual innervation helps preserve surgical outcomes.",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question_type": "multiple_choice",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "subcategory": "Intrinsic Hand Muscles",

      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle groups/intrinsic_hand_muscles",

      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.089Z",

    {

      "keywords": [

        "nerve_radial_nerve"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "The radial nerve primarily supplies the extensor muscles in the forearm and does not innervate intrinsic hand muscles. Intrinsic muscles are innervated by the median and ulnar nerves.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Median nerve",
        "Radial nerve",
        "Ulnar nerve",
        "All of the above do"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.431Z",

      "clinical_application": "Radial nerve palsy causes wrist drop but typically spares intrinsic hand function.",

    {
      "id": "Dermatome 001",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "subcategory": "Spinal Nerve Roots – Sensory",
      "short_explanation": "The T2 dermatome covers the Axilla and upper chest.",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists and clinicians can use dermatome maps to identify referred pain, nerve root compression, or spinal dysfunctionsmaking the T2 dermatome essential in cases involving the Axilla and upper chest.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "condition_pain",
        "client_sensation",
        "anatomy_zones",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Netter's Atlas of Human Anatomy",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "correct": 2,
      "question": "Which spinal nerve root provides sensory innervation to the Axilla and upper chest?",
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "condition_pain",
        "client_sensation",
        "anatomy_zones",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "img": "/img/dermatomes/t2.png",
      "relevance_score": 88,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.432Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/dermatomes",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.089Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "synonyms": [
        "T2"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "C6",
        "C4",
        "T2",
        "L4"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Dermatomes represent areas of skin innervated by specific spinal nerve roots. The T2 root supplies sensation to the Axilla and upper chest, a critical zone for diagnosing radiculopathies and nerve root lesions."
    },

      "long_explanation": "Dermatomes represent areas of skin innervated by specific spinal nerve roots. The C6 root supplies sensation to the Lateral forearm, thumb, and index finger, a critical zone for diagnosing radiculopathies and nerve root lesions.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.432Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists and clinicians can use dermatome maps to identify referred pain, nerve root compression, or spinal dysfunctionsmaking the C6 dermatome essential in cases involving the Lateral forearm, thumb, and index finger.",

        "C2",
        "L3",
        "C6"

    {
      "id": "Femoral Innervation 001",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/femoral_nerve_innervations",
      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Pectineus (partial, also obturator), contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle.",
      "short_explanation": "The Pectineus (partial, also obturator) receives motor innervation from the femoral nerve.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscle",

      "relevance_score": 94,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the Pectineus (partial, also obturator)?",
      "synonyms": [
        "Pectineus (partial, also obturator)"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "clinical_application": "Injury to the femoral nerve may impair the function of the Pectineus (partial, also obturator), leading to weakness in leg extension or difficulty walking. Therapists must be cautious in anterior thigh treatments.",
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.436Z",
      "subcategory": "Femoral Nerve",
      "correct": 1,

      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Vastus Medialis, contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle.",

      "short_explanation": "The Vastus Medialis receives motor innervation from the femoral nerve.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.436Z",
      "clinical_application": "Injury to the femoral nerve may impair the function of the Vastus Medialis, leading to weakness in leg extension or difficulty walking. Therapists must be cautious in anterior thigh treatments.",

      "subcategory": "Femoral Nerve",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "short_explanation": "The Articularis Genu receives motor innervation from the femoral nerve.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "relevance_score": 91,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "correct": 3,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/femoral_nerve_innervations",
      "img": "/img/femoral_nerve/articularis_genu.png",
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the Articularis Genu?",
      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Articularis Genu, contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle.",
      "clinical_application": "Injury to the femoral nerve may impair the function of the Articularis Genu, leading to weakness in leg extension or difficulty walking. Therapists must be cautious in anterior thigh treatments.",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.436Z",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",

      "answers": [
        "Obturator Nerve",
        "Superior Gluteal Nerve",
        "Sciatic Nerve",
        "Femoral Nerve"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Pectineus (partial, also obturator), contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle.",

      },
      "short_explanation": "The Vastus Intermedius receives motor innervation from the femoral nerve.",
      "clinical_application": "Injury to the femoral nerve may impair the function of the Vastus Intermedius, leading to weakness in leg extension or difficulty walking. Therapists must be cautious in anterior thigh treatments.",

      "last_answered_at": null,

      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Vastus Intermedius, contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle."

      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Vastus Medialis, contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle.",

      "clinical_application": "Injury to the femoral nerve may impair the function of the Vastus Medialis, leading to weakness in leg extension or difficulty walking. Therapists must be cautious in anterior thigh treatments.",

    {
      "id": "Foot Innervation 001",
      "short_explanation": "The Extensor Digitorum Longus is innervated by the Deep Peroneal Nerve.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.439Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "img": "/img/foot_muscles/extensor_digitorum_longus.png",
      "long_explanation": "The Deep Peroneal Nerve provides motor innervation to the Extensor Digitorum Longus, allowing it to perform specific movements essential for balance and locomotion.",
      "question": "What nerve innervates the Extensor Digitorum Longus?",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "answers": [
        "Superficial Peroneal Nerve",
        "Tibial Nerve",
        "Lateral Plantar Nerve",
        "Deep Peroneal Nerve"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "concept_balance",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "nerve_tibial_nerve",
        "nerve_peroneal_nerve"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "clinical_application": "Dysfunction or compression of the Deep Peroneal Nerve may impair the Extensor Digitorum Longus, affecting foot posture and gait. This is relevant during foot massage and orthopedic assessments.",
      "correct": 3,
      "synonyms": [
        "Extensor Digitorum Longus"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "concept_balance",
        "muscle_extensor_digitorum_longus",
        "nerve_tibial_nerve",
        "nerve_peroneal_nerve"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "distractors": [],
      "relevance_score": 85,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.093Z",
      "subcategory": "Foot Muscles",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/foot_innervations"
    },

      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.093Z",
      "long_explanation": "The Lateral Plantar Nerve provides motor innervation to the Lumbricals (2nd-4th), allowing it to perform specific movements essential for balance and locomotion.",

      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/foot_innervations",

      "relevance_score": 98,
      "long_explanation": "The Deep Peroneal Nerve provides motor innervation to the Extensor Hallucis Brevis, allowing it to perform specific movements essential for balance and locomotion.",

      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "clinical_application": "Dysfunction or compression of the Deep Peroneal Nerve may impair the Extensor Hallucis Brevis, affecting foot posture and gait. This is relevant during foot massage and orthopedic assessments."
    },

        "correct": 0,

      },

      ],
      "short_explanation": "The Tensor Fasciae Latae is innervated by the Superior Gluteal Nerve.",
      "clinical_application": "Understanding Superior Gluteal Nerve innervation helps massage therapists evaluate gluteal dysfunction, correct gait disturbances, and improve functional mobility in clients with hip or back issues.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "long_explanation": "The Superior Gluteal Nerve arises from the sacral plexus and supplies the Tensor Fasciae Latae, a key structure in stabilizing the pelvis, supporting hip extension or abduction, and maintaining postural control during movement.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Which nerve provides innervation to the Tensor Fasciae Latae?",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.096Z",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 3,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.443Z",
      "synonyms": [
        "Tensor Fasciae Latae"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "img": "/img/gluteal_nerve/tensor_fasciae_latae.png",
      "answers": [
        "Obturator Nerve",
        "Inferior Gluteal Nerve",
        "Femoral Nerve",
        "Superior Gluteal Nerve"
      ]
    },

      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "clinical_application": "Understanding Superior Gluteal Nerve innervation helps massage therapists evaluate gluteal dysfunction, correct gait disturbances, and improve functional mobility in clients with hip or back issues.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "short_explanation": "The Gluteus Minimus is innervated by the Superior Gluteal Nerve.",
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the Gluteus Minimus?",

      "correct": 1,
      "answers": [

      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "The Superior Gluteal Nerve arises from the sacral plexus and supplies the Gluteus Minimus, a key structure in stabilizing the pelvis, supporting hip extension or abduction, and maintaining postural control during movement.",

    {
      "id": "Femoral Innervation 001",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Pectineus (partial, also obturator), contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle.",
      "subcategory": "Femoral Nerve",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "correct": 1,
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the Pectineus (partial, also obturator)?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "muscle_pectineus",
        "nerve_sciatic_nerve",
        "nerve_femoral_nerve",
        "nerve_obturator_nerve",
        "nerve_tibial_nerve"
      ],
      "synonyms": [
        "Pectineus (partial, also obturator)"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "relevance_score": 94,
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "clinical_application": "Injury to the femoral nerve may impair the function of the Pectineus (partial, also obturator), leading to weakness in leg extension or difficulty walking. Therapists must be cautious in anterior thigh treatments.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "answers": [
        "Tibial Nerve",
        "Femoral Nerve",
        "Obturator Nerve",
        "Sciatic Nerve"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.099Z",
      "short_explanation": "The Pectineus (partial, also obturator) receives motor innervation from the femoral nerve.",
      "img": "/img/femoral_nerve/pectineus_(partial,_also_obturator).png",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/lower_limb_nerve_innervations",

      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/lower_limb_nerve_innervations",

      "img": "/img/femoral_nerve/rectus_femoris.png",

        "nerve_tibial_nerve"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Rectus Femoris, contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "Injury to the femoral nerve may impair the function of the Rectus Femoris, leading to weakness in leg extension or difficulty walking. Therapists must be cautious in anterior thigh treatments.",

    {
      "id": "Femoral Innervation 024",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.445Z",
      "synonyms": [
        "Iliacus"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Obturator Nerve",
        "Tibial Nerve",
        "Superior Gluteal Nerve",
        "Femoral Nerve"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "distractors": [],
      "img": "/img/femoral_nerve/iliacus.png",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the Iliacus?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.099Z",
      "subcategory": "Femoral Nerve",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "muscle_iliacus",
        "nerve_femoral_nerve",
        "nerve_obturator_nerve",
        "nerve_tibial_nerve"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Injury to the femoral nerve may impair the function of the Iliacus, leading to weakness in leg extension or difficulty walking. Therapists must be cautious in anterior thigh treatments.",
      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Iliacus, contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "relevance_score": 89,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/lower_limb_nerve_innervations",
      "short_explanation": "The Iliacus receives motor innervation from the femoral nerve.",

      "id": "Femoral Innervation 034",

      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "The Articularis Genu receives motor innervation from the femoral nerve.",
      "subcategory": "Femoral Nerve",
      "img": "/img/femoral_nerve/articularis_genu.png",
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the Articularis Genu?",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "synonyms": [
        "Articularis Genu"
      ],
      "relevance_score": 97,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "nerve_sciatic_nerve",
        "nerve_femoral_nerve",
        "nerve_tibial_nerve"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Sciatic Nerve",
        "Femoral Nerve",
        "Superior Gluteal Nerve",
        "Tibial Nerve"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "long_explanation": "The femoral nerve originates from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) and innervates the Articularis Genu, contributing to hip flexion or knee extension depending on the specific muscle.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.099Z",

        "nerve_tibial_nerve"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/lower_limb_nerve_innervations",
      "clinical_application": "Injury to the femoral nerve may impair the function of the Articularis Genu, leading to weakness in leg extension or difficulty walking. Therapists must be cautious in anterior thigh treatments."

      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "short_explanation": "The Gracilis is innervated by the obturator nerve.",
      "subcategory": "Obturator Nerve",

      "topic": "anatomy",
      "img": "/img/obturator_nerve/gracilis.png",

      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.100Z",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "clinical_application": "Knowledge of obturator nerve pathways is crucial during treatments of groin or medial thigh pain, especially when targeting the Gracilis for therapeutic relief.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "The obturator nerve arises from the lumbar plexus and innervates medial thigh muscles like the Gracilis, supporting thigh adduction and pelvic stabilization."
    },

      "question": "Which nerve innervates the Adductor Magnus (adductor part)?",
      "long_explanation": "The obturator nerve arises from the lumbar plexus and innervates medial thigh muscles like the Adductor Magnus (adductor part), supporting thigh adduction and pelvic stabilization.",

      "long_explanation": "The obturator nerve arises from the lumbar plexus and innervates medial thigh muscles like the Pectineus (partial, mostly femoral), supporting thigh adduction and pelvic stabilization.",

    {
      "id": "Sciatic Innervation 001",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "exam_assessment",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "concept_targeted",
        "muscle_biceps_femoris",
        "nerve_sciatic_nerve",
        "nerve_femoral_nerve",
        "nerve_obturator_nerve"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The Biceps Femoris (short head) is innervated by the Common Fibular Nerve.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/lower_limb_nerve_innervations",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Which nerve innervates the Biceps Femoris (short head)?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.099Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "The Common Fibular Nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve system, provides motor innervation to the Biceps Femoris (short head), enabling actions essential for movement such as knee flexion, plantarflexion, or dorsiflexion. This understanding is critical for assessing lower limb nerve function.",
      "relevance_score": 98,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "exam_assessment",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "concept_targeted",
        "muscle_biceps_femoris",
        "nerve_sciatic_nerve",
        "nerve_femoral_nerve",
        "nerve_obturator_nerve"
      ],
      "synonyms": [
        "Biceps Femoris (short head)"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "answers": [
        "Femoral Nerve",
        "Common Fibular Nerve",
        "Obturator Nerve",
        "Inferior Gluteal Nerve"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "subcategory": "Sciatic Nerve",
      "correct": 1,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "clinical_application": "In massage therapy or rehabilitation, knowing that the Common Fibular Nerve innervates the Biceps Femoris (short head) helps target specific regions when addressing nerve impingements, gait dysfunctions, or posterior chain imbalances.",
      "img": "/img/sciatic_nerve/biceps_femoris_(short_head).png",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.446Z"
    },

      "clinical_application": "In massage therapy or rehabilitation, knowing that the Tibial Nerve innervates the Adductor Magnus (hamstring part) helps target specific regions when addressing nerve impingements, gait dysfunctions, or posterior chain imbalances.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",

        "concept_targeted",

      "synonyms": [

      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",

      "last_answered_at": null,
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "The Tibial Nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve system, provides motor innervation to the Adductor Magnus (hamstring part), enabling actions essential for movement such as knee flexion, plantarflexion, or dorsiflexion. This understanding is critical for assessing lower limb nerve function.",

      "clinical_application": "Knowledge of obturator nerve pathways is crucial during treatments of groin or medial thigh pain, especially when targeting the Gracilis for therapeutic relief.",

      "question": "Which nerve innervates the Gracilis?",
      "long_explanation": "The obturator nerve arises from the lumbar plexus and innervates medial thigh muscles like the Gracilis, supporting thigh adduction and pelvic stabilization.",

      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",

    {
      "id": "Obturator Innervation 050",
      "long_explanation": "The obturator nerve arises from the lumbar plexus and innervates medial thigh muscles like the Obturator Externus, supporting thigh adduction and pelvic stabilization.",
      "clinical_application": "Knowledge of obturator nerve pathways is crucial during treatments of groin or medial thigh pain, especially when targeting the Obturator Externus for therapeutic relief.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",

      "id": "Sciatic Innervation 001",
      "long_explanation": "The Common Fibular Nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve system, provides motor innervation to the Biceps Femoris (short head), enabling actions essential for movement such as knee flexion, plantarflexion, or dorsiflexion.       "question": "Which nerve innervates the Adductor Magnus (hamstring part)?",
      "long_explanation": "The Tibial Nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve system, provides motor innervation to the Adductor Magnus (hamstring part), enabling actions essential for movement such as knee flexion, plantarflexion, or dorsiflexion.       "clinical_application": "In massage therapy or rehabilitation, knowing that the Tibial Nerve innervates the Semitendinosus helps target specific regions when addressing nerve impingements, gait dysfunctions, or posterior chain imbalances.",

        "anatomy_nerve",

      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "long_explanation": "The Tibial Nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve system, provides motor innervation to the Semitendinosus, enabling actions essential for movement such as knee flexion, plantarflexion, or dorsiflexion. This understanding is critical for assessing lower limb nerve function.",
      "topic": "anatomy"

      "long_explanation": "The Deep Peroneal Nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve system, provides motor innervation to the Extensor Hallucis Brevis, enabling actions essential for movement such as knee flexion, plantarflexion, or dorsiflexion. This understanding is critical for assessing lower limb nerve function.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "In massage therapy or rehabilitation, knowing that the Deep Peroneal Nerve innervates the Extensor Hallucis Brevis helps target specific regions when addressing nerve impingements, gait dysfunctions, or posterior chain imbalances.",

        "nerve_tibial_nerve"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "In massage therapy or rehabilitation, knowing that the Tibial Nerve innervates the Tibialis Posterior helps target specific regions when addressing nerve impingements, gait dysfunctions, or posterior chain imbalances.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Obturator Nerve",
        "Femoral Nerve",
        "Inferior Gluteal Nerve",
        "Tibial Nerve"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Sciatic Nerve",
      "synonyms": [
        "Tibialis Posterior"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The Tibial Nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve system, provides motor innervation to the Tibialis Posterior, enabling actions essential for movement such as knee flexion, plantarflexion, or dorsiflexion. This understanding is critical for assessing lower limb nerve function.",

      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body by A. Biel",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/muscle innervations/sciatic_nerve_innervations",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "exam_assessment",
        "movement_plantarflexion",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "concept_targeted",
        "muscle_soleus",
        "nerve_sciatic_nerve",
        "nerve_obturator_nerve",
        "nerve_tibial_nerve"
      ],
      "img": "/img/sciatic_nerve/soleus.png",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "subcategory": "Sciatic Nerve",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "long_explanation": "The Tibial Nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve system, provides motor innervation to the Soleus, enabling actions essential for movement such as knee flexion, plantarflexion, or dorsiflexion. This understanding is critical for assessing lower limb nerve function.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "clinical_application": "In massage therapy or rehabilitation, knowing that the Tibial Nerve innervates the Soleus helps target specific regions when addressing nerve impingements, gait dysfunctions, or posterior chain imbalances.",
      "synonyms": [
        "Soleus"
      ]
    },
    {

    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 001",
      "short_explanation": "Thyroxine (T4) regulates metabolism and is produced by the thyroid gland.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "question": "Which hormone regulates metabolism and is produced by the thyroid gland?",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "long_explanation": "Thyroxine (T4) is a thyroid hormone responsible for regulating basal metabolic rate, energy production, and oxygen consumption throughout the body. It is secreted by the thyroid gland in response to TSH from the pituitary and helps maintain healthy growth, temperature, and weight balance. Hypothyroidism leads to fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance, while hyperthyroidism may cause anxiety, heat sensitivity, and weight loss.",
      "distractors": [],
      "applied_example": "A massage therapist working with a client who has hypothyroidism may focus on increasing circulation and muscle pliability, since clients may present with fatigue, sluggish tissue, or cold extremities.",
      "answers": [
        "Insulin",
        "Thyroxine (T4)",
        "Cortisol",
        "Testosterone"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_balance",
        "concept_anxiety",
        "organ_thyroid"
      ],
      "unit": "common hormones",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_balance",
        "concept_anxiety",
        "organ_thyroid"
      ],
      "correct": 1
    },
    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 002",
      "question": "Which hormone helps regulate blood sugar by promoting glucose uptake into cells?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_pancreas"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "unit": "common hormones",
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "Insulin helps lower blood sugar by promoting glucose uptake into cells.",
      "answers": [
        "Adrenaline",
        "Glucagon",
        "Insulin",
        "Growth Hormone"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "applied_example": "Clients with diabetes may have impaired circulation or peripheral neuropathy. Therapists should adjust pressure and check for numb areas, particularly in the feet and legs.",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_pancreas"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "Produced by the pancreas, insulin allows glucose from the bloodstream to enter muscle, fat, and liver cells for energy or storage. It plays a key role in blood sugar regulation. Lack of insulin (Type 1 diabetes) or insulin resistance (Type 2 diabetes) leads to elevated glucose levels, which can damage nerves, organs, and blood vessels."
    },
    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 003",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_stress",
        "system_immune",
        "concept_anxiety"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Cortisol is released by the adrenal cortex in response to stress and helps regulate metabolism and inflammation.",
      "distractors": [],
      "applied_example": "Massage can help reduce chronically elevated cortisol by activating the parasympathetic nervous system. This helps lower stress levels and improve recovery in overstressed clients.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "long_explanation": "Cortisol is a glucocorticoid hormone that helps the body respond to stress by increasing blood sugar, suppressing the immune system, and aiding metabolism. It's released as part of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. Chronic elevation due to prolonged stress can lead to fatigue, muscle breakdown, weight gain, and anxiety.",
      "answers": [
        "Epinephrine",
        "Cortisol",
        "Glucagon",
        "Aldosterone"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_stress",
        "system_immune",
        "concept_anxiety"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "common hormones",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "correct": 1,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "question": "Which hormone is released in response to stress and is produced by the adrenal cortex?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 004",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Cortisol",
        "Insulin",
        "Epinephrine (Adrenaline)",
        "Melatonin"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "short_explanation": "Epinephrine (adrenaline) triggers the fight-or-flight response by increasing heart rate, blood pressure, and alertness.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "question": "What hormone is responsible for the 'fight or flight' response?",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "purpose_stress",
        "organ_heart"
      ],
      "unit": "common hormones",
      "long_explanation": "Epinephrine is a fast-acting hormone released from the adrenal medulla in response to acute stress. It prepares the body for immediate action by dilating airways, increasing heart rate, and mobilizing energy stores. Its rapid effects contrast with cortisol's longer-term stress response.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "purpose_stress",
        "organ_heart"
      ],
      "applied_example": "Massage helps down-regulate the sympathetic nervous system. For anxious or high-adrenaline clients, calming techniques like effleurage and diaphragmatic breathing can counteract adrenaline-driven tension.",
      "correct": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 005",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_pancreas"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Glucagon raises blood glucose levels by stimulating the liver to release stored glucose.",
      "distractors": [],
      "applied_example": "If a client is diabetic and experiencing low blood sugar symptoms during a massage (lightheadedness, sweating), the therapist should stop the session and ensure the client eats or drinks something sweet.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "long_explanation": "Secreted by pancreatic alpha cells, glucagon increases blood sugar by signaling the liver to convert glycogen into glucose (glycogenolysis). It acts as a counterbalance to insulin, ensuring the body has enough energy during fasting or between meals.",
      "answers": [
        "Insulin",
        "Thyroxine",
        "Glucagon",
        "Cortisol"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_pancreas"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "common hormones",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "question": "Which hormone is released by the pancreas to increase blood glucose levels?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 006",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "unit": "common hormones",
      "applied_example": "Massage therapists should note hydration status, as clients with fluid balance issues (e.g., water retention or excessive thirst) may require session length or intensity adjustments.",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Aldosterone",
        "Cortisol",
        "Antidiuretic hormone (ADH)",
        "Calcitonin"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also called vasopressin, is released by the posterior pituitary. It acts on the kidneys to reduce urine output and conserve body water, helping maintain blood pressure and hydration. Dysfunction may lead to dehydration or water retention disorders like SIADH.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "question": "Which hormone promotes water reabsorption in the kidneys?",
      "tags": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_kidneys"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_kidneys"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "ADH promotes water retention by signaling the kidneys to reabsorb more water."
    },
    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 007",
      "tags": [
        "physiology_sleep",
        "biochemistry_levels"
      ],
      "unit": "common hormones",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "long_explanation": "Melatonin is produced by the pineal gland and helps regulate circadian rhythms. Its secretion is influenced by light exposure-levels rise in darkness and fall with light. It promotes drowsiness and helps signal the body that it's time to rest. Disruptions in melatonin can contribute to insomnia or jet lag.",
      "short_explanation": "Melatonin regulates the sleep-wake cycle and is released in response to darkness.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "applied_example": "Massage, especially in the evening, may help improve melatonin regulation by promoting relaxation and supporting better sleep for clients with insomnia or disrupted sleep schedules.",
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Which hormone regulates the sleep-wake cycle?",
      "correct": 2,
      "answers": [
        "Serotonin",
        "Cortisol",
        "Melatonin",
        "Dopamine"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "keywords": [
        "physiology_sleep",
        "biochemistry_levels"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "category": "Anatomy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 008",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Which hormone is involved in milk ejection during breastfeeding?",
      "long_explanation": "Oxytocin is released from the posterior pituitary and plays roles in labor, lactation, and emotional bonding. It stimulates uterine contractions during childbirth and milk letdown during breastfeeding. It's also linked to social bonding, affection, and reduced stress through parasympathetic activity.",
      "keywords": [
        "purpose_stress"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Prolactin",
        "Estrogen",
        "Oxytocin",
        "LH"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "applied_example": "Massage may increase oxytocin levels, especially through nurturing touch, creating a sense of emotional connection, comfort, and reduced stress - particularly beneficial in postpartum or anxious clients.",
      "short_explanation": "Oxytocin promotes emotional bonding, trust, and milk ejection during breastfeeding.",
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "tags": [
        "purpose_stress"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "common hormones"
    },
    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 009",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "What hormone stimulates growth and cell reproduction?",
      "applied_example": "Massage may support recovery by improving circulation and creating conditions that complement GH-driven tissue healing - ideal for athletes or clients post-injury.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "Insulin",
        "Growth Hormone",
        "Cortisol",
        "ADH"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "unit": "common hormones",
      "short_explanation": "Growth hormone stimulates cell growth, repair, and regeneration.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "long_explanation": "Growth hormone (GH) is secreted by the anterior pituitary and plays a key role in childhood growth, muscle building, fat metabolism, and tissue repair. In adults, it supports recovery and metabolic function. Deficiency can lead to fatigue and poor healing.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_healing",
        "concept_recovery"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_healing",
        "concept_recovery"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Common Hormones 010",
      "applied_example": "Massage for clients with low testosterone may focus on restoring circulation, reducing stress, and supporting muscle tone, especially if accompanied by fatigue or hormonal imbalance.",
      "question": "Which hormone is primarily responsible for the development of male secondary sex characteristics?",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_red_blood",
        "organ_adrenal_glands",
        "organ_testes"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_red_blood",
        "organ_adrenal_glands",
        "organ_testes"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "answers": [
        "Estrogen",
        "Progesterone",
        "Testosterone",
        "LH"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "Produced primarily in the testes (and in smaller amounts by adrenal glands), testosterone regulates traits like facial hair, voice deepening, muscle mass, and libido. It also supports bone density and red blood cell production. Low testosterone may cause fatigue, mood changes, and decreased muscle tone.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "Testosterone is responsible for male secondary sex characteristics and muscle development.",
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "common hormones"
    },
    {
      "id": "Neurotransmitters 001",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "unit": "neurotransmitters",
      "distractors": [],
      "applied_example": "In massage therapy, working with overactive or tense muscles may help support normal neuromuscular function and improve acetylcholine balance by reducing sympathetic nervous system dominance.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Dopamine",
        "Serotonin",
        "Acetylcholine",
        "Norepinephrine"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Acetylcholine is released at neuromuscular junctions, where it binds to receptors on skeletal muscle cells, triggering an action potential and resulting in contraction. This process is fundamental to voluntary movement and motor control. Dysfunction in acetylcholine signaling can result in paralysis or muscle fatigue, as seen in conditions like myasthenia gravis.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "question": "Which neurotransmitter is primarily responsible for muscle contraction?",
      "correct": 2,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "Acetylcholine is the neurotransmitter that initiates muscle contraction. It transmits signals from motor neurons to muscle fibers."
    },
    {
      "id": "Neurotransmitters 002",
      "tags": [
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_balance",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "physiology_sleep",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "concept_anxiety",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "unit": "neurotransmitters",
      "applied_example": "Massage may enhance serotonin production by reducing cortisol and activating the parasympathetic nervous system, promoting relaxation and emotional balance.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_balance",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "physiology_sleep",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "concept_anxiety",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "question": "Which neurotransmitter is associated with mood regulation, appetite, and sleep?",
      "answers": [
        "Dopamine",
        "GABA",
        "Serotonin",
        "Glutamate"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Serotonin (5-HT) is found in the brain and gastrointestinal tract. It influences mood, appetite, circadian rhythm, and even social behavior. Low serotonin levels are associated with depression and anxiety, while higher levels support feelings of well-being. Many antidepressants work by increasing serotonin levels in the brain.",
      "short_explanation": "Serotonin regulates mood, appetite, and sleep. It’s a key neurotransmitter in emotional and physiological balance."
    },
    {
      "id": "Neurotransmitters 003",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "condition_pain",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "physiology_sleep",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Dopamine is critical for motor control, motivation, and reward-seeking behavior.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "condition_pain",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "physiology_sleep",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "Dopamine acts within the brain's basal ganglia to regulate movement, and in the limbic system to influence mood and motivation. It’s essential for initiating movement and reinforcing pleasurable experiences. Deficits in dopamine are linked to Parkinson’s disease, while excess dopamine activity is associated with addiction.",
      "answers": [
        "Muscle contraction",
        "Pain inhibition",
        "Motor control, reward, and motivation",
        "Sleep regulation"
      ],
      "applied_example": "Therapeutic massage may improve dopamine levels indirectly by reducing stress, improving mood, and promoting a sense of physical reward or accomplishment.",
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Dopamine plays a significant role in which of the following?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "neurotransmitters",
      "difficulty": "intermediate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Neurotransmitters 004",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "'Glutamate' is the correct neurotransmitter for this function.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.452Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.104Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "GABA",
        "Serotonin",
        "Norepinephrine",
        "Glutamate"
      ],
      "question": "Which neurotransmitter is the main excitatory chemical in the brain?",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "unit": "neurotransmitters",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 3,
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "The correct answer, 'Glutamate', plays a critical role in this physiological or neurological function. Understanding its effects supports clinical reasoning and communication in massage settings.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "applied_example": "In practice, recognizing the effects of 'Glutamate' can help massage therapists understand client symptoms and adjust treatments to promote nervous system balance."
    },
