    {
      "id": "Shoulder Origin 001",
      "short_explanation": "The deltoid originates from the acromion, scapular spine, and clavicle — allowing it to move the arm in multiple directions.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.105Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "synonyms": [
        "deltoid",
        "shoulder abductor"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The deltoid has three parts originating from the lateral clavicle, acromion, and scapular spine. These origins support anterior, lateral, and posterior shoulder movements. A clear understanding of its origin helps identify shoulder abduction or dysfunction.",
      "correct": 0,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "clinical_application": "Palpating the origin helps identify strain in overhead athletes or clients with limited shoulder abduction.",
      "answers": [
        "Acromion and spine of the scapula, lateral third of clavicle",
        "Medial epicondyle of the humerus",
        "Coracoid process of the scapula",
        "Greater tubercle of the humerus"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "question": "Where does the deltoid muscle originate?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.453Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/origins/shoulder_origins",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "population_athlete",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "bone_clavicle",
        "bone_scapula",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "population_athlete",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "bone_clavicle",
        "bone_scapula",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "image_reference": "shoulder_origin_deltoid.png"
    },
    {
      "id": "Shoulder Origin 002",
      "answers": [
        "Coracoid process and supraglenoid tubercle",
        "Medial half of clavicle, sternum, and costal cartilages 1–6",
        "Acromion and spine of the scapula",
        "Inferior angle of the scapula"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Common site of overuse in weightlifters or clients with rounded shoulders. Palpation aids posture correction.",
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.453Z",
      "question": "What is the origin of the pectoralis major muscle?",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.105Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "correct": 1,
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/origins/shoulder_origins",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "muscle_pectoralis_major",
        "bone_clavicle",
        "bone_scapula",
        "bone_sternum",
        "bone_ribs"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "short_explanation": "Pectoralis major originates from the clavicle, sternum, and ribs, giving it broad leverage for arm movement.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_assists",
        "concept_assisted",
        "muscle_pectoralis_major",
        "bone_clavicle",
        "bone_scapula",
        "bone_sternum",
        "bone_ribs"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "With its origin across the medial clavicle, sternum, and upper costal cartilages, the pectoralis major facilitates powerful shoulder flexion and adduction. This large origin area allows it to assist in pushing, hugging, and lifting actions.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "image_reference": "shoulder_origin_pectoralis_major.png",
      "synonyms": [
        "pec major",
        "pectoralis"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Shoulder Origin 003",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "As one of the four rotator cuff muscles, the supraspinatus starts in the supraspinous fossa and passes under the acromion to insert on the humerus. Its origin above the scapular spine positions it to initiate abduction.",
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "condition_pain",
        "exam_assessment",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "bone_clavicle",
        "bone_scapula",
        "bone_sternum",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "answers": [
        "Infraspinous fossa of the scapula",
        "Lateral border of the scapula",
        "Supraspinous fossa of the scapula",
        "Clavicle and sternum"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/origins/shoulder_origins",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Common site of tendon impingement; important to evaluate in clients with painful arc or limited shoulder motion.",
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.105Z",
      "synonyms": [
        "SSP",
        "RTC muscle"
      ],
      "question": "The supraspinatus muscle originates from which structure?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "condition_pain",
        "exam_assessment",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_insertion",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "bone_clavicle",
        "bone_scapula",
        "bone_sternum",
        "bone_humerus"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.453Z",
      "short_explanation": "The supraspinatus originates in the supraspinous fossa of the scapula — above the spine.",
      "image_reference": "shoulder_origin_supraspinatus.png",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",

    },
    {
      "id": "Shoulder Origin 004",
      "synonyms": [
        "teres group",
        "posterior shoulder"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "clinical_application": "Often hypertonic in climbers or clients with strong pulling habits. Can restrict overhead reach.",
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/origins/shoulder_origins",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "muscle_pectoralis_minor",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "muscle_teres_major",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "muscle_trapezius",
        "bone_scapula"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "correct": 1,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.453Z",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "answers": [
        "Supraspinatus",
        "Teres major",
        "Pectoralis minor",
        "Trapezius"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.105Z",
      "short_explanation": "Teres major arises from the inferior part of the lateral scapular border.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Which of the following muscles originates on the lateral border of the scapula?",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "muscle_pectoralis_minor",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "muscle_teres_major",
        "muscle_supraspinatus",
        "muscle_trapezius",
        "bone_scapula"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "long_explanation": "The teres major helps adduct and medially rotate the arm. Its scapular origin allows it to function like a mini latissimus dorsi, especially during pulling actions.",
      "distractors": [],
      "image_reference": "shoulder_origin_teres_major.png"
    },
    {
      "id": "Shoulder Origin 005",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "mblex_category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "long_explanation": "Originating from the thoracolumbar fascia, lumbar vertebrae, iliac crest, and part of the scapula, the latissimus dorsi is a powerful extensor and internal rotator of the arm. Its wide origin enables strong pulling actions.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "purpose_stress",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_tension",
        "bone_landmark_acromion_process",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "bone_lumbar_vertebrae",
        "bone_clavicle",
        "bone_scapula"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.453Z",
      "synonyms": [
        "lat",
        "lats"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Anterior scapula and clavicle",
        "Inferior angle of scapula, spinous processes T7–L5, thoracolumbar fascia, and iliac crest",
        "Acromion process and clavicle",
        "Spinous processes C1–C4"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "category": "Anatomy & Physiology",
      "image_reference": "shoulder_origin_latissimus_dorsi.png",
      "subcategory": "Shoulder Region",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.105Z",
      "clinical_application": "Overactive in swimmers or clients with posture-related back tightness. Addressing origin tension helps reduce strain.",
      "short_explanation": "The latissimus dorsi has a broad origin across the mid and lower back.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "purpose_stress",
        "client",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "concept_tension",
        "bone_landmark_acromion_process",
        "muscle_latissimus_dorsi",
        "bone_lumbar_vertebrae",
        "bone_clavicle",
        "bone_scapula"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/anatomy/origins/shoulder_origins",
      "question": "Where does the latissimus dorsi originate?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Prefixes 001",
      "short_explanation": "The prefix 'cardi/o' refers to the heart.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "unit": "anatomy prefixes",
      "question": "What does the root 'cardi/o' refer to?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "applied_example": "When working with a client with heart conditions, massage therapists must be cautious with techniques that affect circulation, and use the prefix to understand clinical notes or referrals.",
      "long_explanation": "'Cardi/o' comes from the Greek word 'kardia,' meaning heart. It's commonly seen in medical terms like 'cardiology' (study of the heart) and 'cardiomegaly' (enlarged heart). Knowing this root helps identify terms related to cardiovascular function and pathology.",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_heart",
        "organ_lungs",
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_kidneys"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.454Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_heart",
        "organ_lungs",
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_kidneys"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "Lungs",
        "Heart",
        "Liver",
        "Kidney"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "category": "Anatomy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Prefixes 002",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Liver",
        "Blood",
        "Skin",
        "Stomach"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.454Z",
      "applied_example": "A client with hepatitis may have reduced liver function; understanding this prefix helps therapists recognize potential systemic considerations during treatment.",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "The prefix 'hepat/o' means liver.",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "'Hepat/o' comes from the Greek word 'hepar,' meaning liver. It's used in medical language like 'hepatitis' (inflammation of the liver) and 'hepatotoxic' (liver-damaging substances). Understanding this prefix supports awareness of hepatic function and conditions.",
      "unit": "anatomy prefixes",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "The root 'hepat/o' means:",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_stomach",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "tags": [
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_stomach",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "correct": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Prefixes 003",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "correct": 1,
      "question": "What does 'neur/o' refer to?",
      "unit": "anatomy prefixes",
      "long_explanation": "'Neur/o' is derived from the Greek 'neuron,' meaning nerve. It's seen in terms such as 'neurology' (study of nerves), 'neuropathy' (nerve damage), and 'neurotransmitter'. It is foundational in understanding how the body communicates via the nervous system.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Bone",
        "Nerve",
        "Muscle",
        "Kidney"
      ],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_kidneys"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_kidneys"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The prefix 'neur/o' refers to nerves or the nervous system.",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "applied_example": "Massage therapists working with clients who have nerve sensitivity or conditions like sciatica benefit from understanding neural pathways and terminology starting with 'neur/o'.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.454Z"
    },

    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 001",
      "question": "What does the suffix '-itis' mean?",
      "long_explanation": "'-itis' denotes inflammation, often associated with pain, heat, swelling, and reduced function. It appears in terms like 'arthritis' (joint inflammation) or 'tendonitis' (inflammation of tendons due to overuse). These conditions signal active tissue irritation that may limit range of motion or cause localized tenderness.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_pain",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "indication",
        "concept_localized",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "applied_example": "Clients with shoulder tendonitis may benefit from gentle work on surrounding musculature like the deltoid or rotator cuff, while avoiding direct pressure on the inflamed tendon itself.",
      "answers": [
        "Tumor",
        "Inflammation",
        "Condition",
        "Surgical removal"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-itis' indicates inflammation, such as in arthritis or tendonitis.",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_pain",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "indication",
        "concept_localized",
        "joint_joint"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 002",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "'-ectomy' comes from Greek meaning 'to cut out.' It appears in medical procedures such as 'cholecystectomy' (gallbladder removal) or 'mastectomy' (removal of breast tissue). Such surgeries often result in scar tissue, fascial adhesions, or postural compensation.",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question": "What is indicated by the suffix '-ectomy'?",
      "correct": 1,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "applied_example": "Post-surgical clients with an abdominal scar may benefit from myofascial release and gentle mobilization to restore tissue glide and reduce restriction caused by scar adhesions.",
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-ectomy' refers to surgical removal, as in appendectomy or mastectomy.",
      "answers": [
        "Pain",
        "Surgical removal",
        "Study of",
        "Disease"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "condition_pain",
        "indication",
        "organ_gallbladder"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "tags": [
        "condition_pain",
        "indication",
        "organ_gallbladder"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 003",
      "correct": 1,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "'-algia' stems from Greek for 'pain' and is used to describe discomfort in specific tissues or systems, like 'myalgia' (muscle pain) or 'cephalalgia' (headache). These terms indicate chronic or acute pain conditions that may not be associated with visible injury.",
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question": "The suffix '-algia' refers to:",
      "applied_example": "Clients with fibromyalgia, a condition marked by widespread 'myalgia,' may benefit from slow, light-pressure techniques that reduce pain perception without triggering flare-ups.",
      "answers": [
        "Swelling",
        "Pain",
        "Enlargement",
        "Hardening"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-algia' refers to pain, as seen in neuralgia or fibromyalgia.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_pain",
        "condition_fibromyalgia",
        "indication",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_discomfort"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_pain",
        "condition_fibromyalgia",
        "indication",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_discomfort"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 004",
      "correct": 0,
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "long_explanation": "'-logy' is used to denote scientific disciplines or specialties such as 'neurology' (study of the nervous system) and 'kinesiology' (study of movement). Understanding this suffix helps interpret medical documentation and client conditions.",
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "What does '-logy' mean in medical terminology?",
      "applied_example": "If a client is under the care of a rheumatologist, understanding '-logy' helps the massage therapist recognize the clinical specialty and adapt treatment for autoimmune or systemic issues.",
      "answers": [
        "Study of",
        "Measurement",
        "Cutting",
        "Infection"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-logy' means 'the study of', as in physiology or pathology.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "exam_terminology",
        "client",
        "ethics_documentation"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "exam_terminology",
        "client",
        "ethics_documentation"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 005",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "organ_heart",
        "organ_liver"
      ],
      "applied_example": "A client with known cardiomegaly should not receive circulatory massage without physician clearance, as increased venous return could strain cardiac output.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "'-megaly' signifies abnormal enlargement of an organ or tissue. Examples include 'cardiomegaly' (enlarged heart) and 'hepatomegaly' (enlarged liver). These conditions often signal systemic disease and can influence circulatory or mechanical dynamics during massage.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "Inflammation",
        "Hardening",
        "Enlargement",
        "Displacement"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "organ_heart",
        "organ_liver"
      ],
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "question": "The suffix '-megaly' refers to:",
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-megaly' means enlargement, as in splenomegaly or cardiomegaly.",
      "correct": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 006",
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_pain",
        "indication"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "The suffix '-oma' typically indicates:",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "long_explanation": "'-oma' denotes growths or tumors, which may be benign (like lipoma) or malignant (like lymphoma). This suffix helps therapists understand medical conditions involving tissue proliferation or neoplasms.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Pain",
        "Condition",
        "Tumor or mass",
        "Study"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z",
      "applied_example": "If a client has a lipoma under the skin, massage should avoid compressing that area directly to prevent irritation or discomfort.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-oma' indicates a tumor or mass, such as in lipoma or lymphoma.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_pain",
        "indication"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 007",
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "question": "What does the suffix '-pathy' mean?",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "long_explanation": "'-pathy' stems from Greek 'pathos' meaning disease or suffering. It appears in terms like 'neuropathy' (nerve disorder) and 'arthropathy' (joint disease). These conditions often involve degeneration, dysfunction, or hypersensitivity.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Study",
        "Surgical repair",
        "Disease",
        "Formation"
      ],
      "applied_example": "Clients with peripheral neuropathy may benefit from massage using gentle, warming techniques to improve circulation and reduce discomfort without overstimulation.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-pathy' refers to disease or dysfunction, as in neuropathy or myopathy."
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 008",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "The suffix '-plasty' means:",
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "correct": 0,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "long_explanation": "'-plasty' is used in reconstructive procedures. It's seen in terms like 'rhinoplasty' (nasal reshaping) or 'angioplasty' (blood vessel repair). These procedures often result in scar formation and changes in soft tissue integrity.",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Surgical repair",
        "Condition",
        "Pain",
        "Destruction"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_pain",
        "modality_soft_tissue",
        "bone_nasal"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_pain",
        "modality_soft_tissue",
        "bone_nasal"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-plasty' refers to surgical repair or reconstruction, like rhinoplasty or angioplasty.",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "applied_example": "Post-surgical clients with cosmetic or vascular procedures may benefit from gentle manual therapy to reduce swelling, promote tissue healing, and improve mobility around the affected area.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 009",
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "applied_example": "A client with anemia may fatigue easily during sessions. Lighter techniques with shorter durations may be more appropriate and better tolerated.",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "answers": [
        "Blood condition",
        "Hardening",
        "Tumor",
        "Surgical removal"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "'-emia' is found in terms related to blood composition or abnormalities. 'Anemia' refers to reduced red blood cells, while 'leukemia' is a cancer of white blood cells. These conditions may influence client fatigue, immune function, and tissue healing.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z",
      "correct": 0,
      "question": "What does the suffix '-emia' refer to?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_cancer",
        "purpose_healing",
        "indication",
        "client",
        "system_immune",
        "anatomy_red_blood"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_cancer",
        "purpose_healing",
        "indication",
        "client",
        "system_immune",
        "anatomy_red_blood"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-emia' indicates a blood condition, like anemia or leukemia."
    },
    {
      "id": "Anatomy Suffixes 010",
      "question": "The suffix '-stenosis' means:",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.455Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 2,
      "answers": [
        "Widening",
        "Swelling",
        "Narrowing",
        "Bleeding"
      ],
      "unit": "anatomy suffixes",
      "topic": "anatomy",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "applied_example": "Massage for clients with lumbar spinal stenosis should avoid excessive spinal extension and instead focus on reducing tension in hip flexors and paraspinals to reduce compression symptoms.",
      "short_explanation": "The suffix '-stenosis' refers to narrowing, often of vessels or passages, as in spinal stenosis.",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_artery",
        "condition_pain",
        "indication"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.106Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "category": "Anatomy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_artery",
        "condition_pain",
        "indication"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "'-stenosis' indicates pathological narrowing, such as of arteries ('aortic stenosis') or spinal canals ('spinal stenosis'). It often results in reduced flow or nerve compression, contributing to pain or reduced function."
    },

      "short_explanation": "Mature red blood cells lack a nucleus.",

    {
      "id": "Cell Specialization Fun Facts 005",
      "question": "Why is the axon of some neurons so long?",
      "long_explanation": "Neurons have extended axons to carry signals from the central nervous system to distant muscles or organs.",
      "unit": "cell specialization fun facts",
      "keywords": [

        "neural_signal"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapy can influence neural transmission and reduce nerve compression symptoms.",

      "answers": [

        "To transfer blood",

        "To store energy"

      "stats": {
