    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 001",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "system_immune",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "science_organization",
        "concept_balance",
        "biology_organisms",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "biochemistry_levels"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "A rapid immune response",
        "The maintenance of a stable internal environment",
        "The process of cellular reproduction",
        "The growth of tissues and organs"
      ],
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Homeostasis is the body's ability to maintain a stable internal environment.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "system_immune",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "science_organization",
        "concept_balance",
        "biology_organisms",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "biochemistry_levels"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "long_explanation": "Homeostasis refers to the regulation of internal conditions like temperature, pH, and glucose levels. The body uses feedback systems to detect changes and trigger corrective responses, keeping systems in balance despite external fluctuations.",
      "correct": 1,
      "question": "What is homeostasis?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 002",
      "question": "Which body system is primarily responsible for regulating homeostasis?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "concept_balance",
        "system_digestive",
        "organ_heart"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "The nervous system plays a key role in detecting changes and coordinating responses.",
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Digestive system",
        "Skeletal system",
        "Nervous system",
        "Lymphatic system"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "concept_balance",
        "system_digestive",
        "organ_heart"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The nervous system monitors internal and external environments, sending rapid electrical signals to adjust bodily functions. It works closely with the endocrine system to maintain balance in processes like temperature control and heart rate."
    },
    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 003",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_healing",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "concept_balance",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "concept_restoration",
        "organ_heart"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_healing",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "concept_balance",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "condition_injury",
        "condition_injuries",
        "concept_restoration",
        "organ_heart"
      ],
      "question": "Which of the following is an example of a negative feedback loop in homeostasis?",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "answers": [
        "Increased bleeding after injury",
        "Rising blood glucose triggering insulin release",
        "Muscle cramps from overuse",
        "Increased heart rate during exercise"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 1,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Negative feedback loops restore balance by reversing a change. For example, when blood sugar rises, insulin is released to lower it. Once glucose normalizes, insulin secretion decreases—demonstrating a self-regulating cycle.",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "short_explanation": "Insulin release after high blood glucose is a classic example of negative feedback."
    },
    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 004",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "correct": 1,
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Liver",
        "Pancreas",
        "Stomach",
        "Kidney"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_kidneys",
        "organ_stomach",
        "organ_pancreas"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "long_explanation": "The pancreas detects high blood sugar and releases insulin, which promotes glucose uptake by cells and storage in the liver. This helps return blood sugar to a stable level, a key aspect of metabolic homeostasis.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_liver",
        "organ_kidneys",
        "organ_stomach",
        "organ_pancreas"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "The pancreas releases insulin to regulate blood glucose levels.",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "question": "What organ helps regulate blood glucose levels through the secretion of insulin?",
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "difficulty": "intermediate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 005",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "When body temperature rises, sweat glands produce moisture that evaporates from the skin surface, releasing heat. This cooling mechanism is part of a negative feedback loop that prevents overheating.",
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "question": "Which of the following would be a homeostatic response to high body temperature?",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "short_explanation": "Sweating helps cool the body and lower internal temperature.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "correct": 1,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "answers": [
        "Vasoconstriction",
        "Sweating",
        "Shivering",
        "Pupil dilation"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 006",
      "question": "Which two systems work closely together to maintain homeostasis?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "concept_balance",
        "system_digestive"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Nervous and endocrine systems",
        "Digestive and reproductive systems",
        "Lymphatic and skeletal systems",
        "Integumentary and muscular systems"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 0,
      "short_explanation": "The nervous and endocrine systems coordinate responses to maintain balance.",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "concept_balance",
        "system_digestive"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The nervous system sends rapid signals while the endocrine system regulates longer-term changes via hormones. Together, they maintain vital parameters like blood pressure, metabolism, and hydration.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Cell Theory"
    },
    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 007",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_beating",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "purpose_healing",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "concept_restoration",
        "organ_heart"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 0,
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "tags": [
        "technique_beating",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "purpose_healing",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "concept_restoration",
        "organ_heart"
      ],
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "Baroreceptors detect low pressure and activate the sympathetic nervous system. This increases heart rate and narrows blood vessels, restoring circulation to vital organs.",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "question": "How does the body respond to low blood pressure to restore homeostasis?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "short_explanation": "The heart beats faster and vessels constrict to raise blood pressure.",
      "answers": [
        "Increasing heart rate and constricting blood vessels",
        "Slowing down metabolism",
        "Dilating capillaries",
        "Decreasing water retention"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 008",
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "answers": [
        "Adrenaline",
        "Melatonin",
        "Antidiuretic hormone (ADH)",
        "Insulin"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "keywords": [
        "concept_balance",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_kidneys"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "ADH (antidiuretic hormone) promotes water retention in the kidneys.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "concept_balance",
        "anatomy_kidneys",
        "organ_kidneys"
      ],
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "long_explanation": "ADH is secreted by the pituitary gland when dehydration is detected. It signals the kidneys to reabsorb more water, reducing urine output and helping maintain fluid balance.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "question": "Which hormone helps regulate water balance in the body?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 009",
      "question": "Which feedback mechanism enhances or intensifies a change in the body?",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "short_explanation": "Positive feedback amplifies changes instead of reversing them.",
      "answers": [
        "Negative feedback",
        "Homeostatic buffering",
        "Positive feedback",
        "Reversal loop"
      ],
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Unlike negative feedback, which stabilizes, positive feedback increases the original stimulus. It’s used in processes like blood clotting and childbirth where rapid escalation is beneficial.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "topic": "cell_theory"
    },
    {
      "id": "Homeostasis 010",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "tags": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [],
      "question": "Which of the following is an example of positive feedback?",
      "long_explanation": "During labor, oxytocin triggers contractions. These contractions stretch the cervix, which signals more oxytocin release—creating a loop until delivery is complete.",
      "correct": 2,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Blood sugar regulation",
        "Thermoregulation",
        "Uterine contractions during childbirth",
        "Sweating during exercise"
      ],
      "unit": "homeostasis",
      "short_explanation": "Uterine contractions intensify during childbirth due to positive feedback.",
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 001",
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "concept_balance",
        "system_digestive",
        "biochemistry_concentration"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "In an isotonic solution, the concentration of solutes is equal inside and outside the cell. Water moves in and out at the same rate, resulting in no net change in cell volume. This balance maintains cell stability.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "It swells and bursts",
        "It shrinks",
        "It maintains its shape with no net water movement",
        "It digests itself"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "concept_balance",
        "system_digestive",
        "biochemistry_concentration"
      ],
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "The cell maintains its shape with no net water movement.",
      "question": "What happens to a cell placed in an isotonic solution?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 002",
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z",
      "short_explanation": "Out of the cell, causing it to shrink.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "concept_balance",
        "anatomy_animal_cells"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "A hypertonic solution has more solutes outside the cell than inside. Water moves out to balance the gradient, causing the cell to lose volume and shrink — a process called crenation in animal cells.",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Into the cell",
        "Out of the cell",
        "Equally in and out",
        "Nowhere at all"
      ],
      "question": "In a hypertonic solution, water moves:",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "concept_balance",
        "anatomy_animal_cells"
      ],
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "correct": 1
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 003",
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "More solute than the cell",
        "The same solute concentration as the cell",
        "Less solute and more water than the cell",
        "No solutes"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "biochemistry_concentration",
        "anatomy_animal_cells"
      ],
      "question": "A hypotonic solution has:",
      "correct": 2,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "In a hypotonic solution, water flows into the cell due to the lower external solute concentration. This may cause the cell to swell and, in animal cells, potentially burst (lysis).",
      "short_explanation": "Less solute and more water than the cell.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "biochemistry_concentration",
        "anatomy_animal_cells"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 004",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "short_explanation": "It may swell and burst due to water influx.",
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "What effect does a hypotonic solution have on an animal cell?",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "The cell stays the same",
        "The cell shrinks",
        "The cell may swell and burst",
        "The cell forms a wall"
      ],
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Animal cells in hypotonic solutions absorb water, swelling due to osmotic pressure. Without a rigid cell wall, they can burst in a process known as hemolysis.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_animal_cells"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_animal_cells"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 005",
      "question": "Which solution type would cause plasmolysis in plant cells?",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "short_explanation": "A hypertonic solution causes plasmolysis.",
      "answers": [
        "Isotonic",
        "Hypotonic",
        "Hypertonic",
        "Acidic"
      ],
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "Plasmolysis occurs when water leaves a plant cell in a hypertonic solution, causing the membrane to pull away from the wall. This dehydrates the cell and disrupts function.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_plant_cells"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_plant_cells"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z",
      "topic": "cell_theory"
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 006",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Isotonic solutions like 0.9% saline match the body's osmotic balance. They restore hydration without disrupting cell size or electrolyte equilibrium, making them ideal for IV fluids.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "Hypertonic",
        "Hypotonic",
        "Isotonic",
        "Colloid"
      ],
      "question": "Which solution is best for intravenous rehydration?",
      "short_explanation": "An isotonic solution maintains fluid balance during IV therapy.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "purpose_healing",
        "concept_balance",
        "concept_restoration"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "purpose_healing",
        "concept_balance",
        "concept_restoration"
      ],
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic",
      "correct": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 007",
      "correct": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_red_blood"
      ],
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "When placed in a hypertonic solution, red blood cells lose water and shrink. This can impair oxygen transport and occurs in cases of dehydration or saline imbalance.",
      "question": "In a hypertonic environment, a red blood cell will:",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "answers": [
        "Expand and lyse",
        "Remain the same",
        "Shrink (crenate)",
        "Become isotonic"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_red_blood"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "Shrink due to water loss — a process called crenation.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 008",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "High water concentration to low water concentration.",
      "long_explanation": "Osmosis is the passive diffusion of water across a semipermeable membrane. Water moves from areas of high water (low solute) to low water (high solute) to balance concentrations.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "concept_balance",
        "biochemistry_concentration"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "concept_balance",
        "biochemistry_concentration"
      ],
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "answers": [
        "High solute to low solute",
        "Low water to high water",
        "High water concentration to low water concentration",
        "Equilibrium to gradient"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "question": "In osmosis, water moves from:",
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic"
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 009",
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "It has an equal solute concentration as the cell.",
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "answers": [
        "Higher solute than the cell",
        "Lower solute than the cell",
        "Equal solute concentration as the cell",
        "No water present"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "purpose_stress",
        "biochemistry_concentration"
      ],
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Which of the following best defines an isotonic solution?",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "long_explanation": "Isotonic solutions do not cause water to enter or exit cells excessively. They maintain equilibrium, preventing osmotic stress and are crucial in clinical hydration therapy.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "purpose_stress",
        "biochemistry_concentration"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Isotonic Hypotonic Hypertonic 010",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 0,
      "short_explanation": "Hemolysis — the bursting of red blood cells.",
      "long_explanation": "Excess water entering the cell in a hypotonic environment increases internal pressure. Without a cell wall, animal cells can burst — a condition known as hemolysis. This is dangerous and monitored during IV therapy.",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_red_blood",
        "anatomy_animal_cells"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.462Z",
      "tags": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_red_blood",
        "anatomy_animal_cells"
      ],
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "answers": [
        "Hemolysis",
        "Plasmolysis",
        "Crenation",
        "Osmotic dehydration"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z",
      "unit": "isotonic hypotonic hypertonic",
      "question": "What condition results from placing animal cells in a hypotonic solution for too long?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 001",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "short_explanation": "Histology is the study of the microscopic structure of tissues.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "histology",
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "question": "What does histology study?",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "answers": [
        "The structure of organs",
        "The body's electrical activity",
        "The microscopic structure of tissues",
        "Chemical properties of cells"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Histology focuses on examining tissues under a microscope to understand their structure and function. It plays a critical role in both biology and medicine by revealing how cells organize into tissues and how abnormalities may indicate disease.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "exam_assessment",
        "indication",
        "science_organization",
        "science_histology",
        "biology_organisms",
        "concept_focuses"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "exam_assessment",
        "indication",
        "science_organization",
        "science_histology",
        "biology_organisms",
        "concept_focuses"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 002",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "science_histology"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Because histology deals with structures too small to see with the naked eye, the microscope is essential. Light microscopes and electron microscopes allow scientists and clinicians to view cells and tissues in detail, aiding in education and diagnosis.",
      "unit": "histology",
      "question": "Which tool is essential for studying histology?",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "short_explanation": "The microscope is the primary tool used in histology.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "correct": 2,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z",
      "answers": [
        "CT scanner",
        "Telescope",
        "Microscope",
        "Stethoscope"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "science_histology"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 003",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "Epithelial",
        "Connective",
        "Muscular",
        "Skeletal"
      ],
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscle",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The four primary tissue types are epithelial, connective, muscular, and nervous. While skeletal tissue refers to bone or muscle, it is not a distinct category. For example, bone is a specialized form of connective tissue.",
      "correct": 3,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "histology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "Skeletal tissue is not one of the four main types; it is a subtype of connective tissue.",
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "question": "Which of the following is NOT one of the four basic tissue types?",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 004",
      "short_explanation": "Epithelial tissue covers surfaces and lines cavities.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "system_digestive",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "answers": [
        "Connective",
        "Muscle",
        "Epithelial",
        "Nervous"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question": "Which tissue type covers body surfaces and lines hollow organs?",
      "unit": "histology",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "system_digestive",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "long_explanation": "Epithelial tissue acts as a protective barrier, lining the skin, digestive tract, and other hollow organs. It also performs functions such as secretion, absorption, and filtration, depending on its location and structure.",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 005",
      "correct": 3,
      "unit": "histology",
      "short_explanation": "Nervous tissue transmits electrical signals throughout the body.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z",
      "answers": [
        "Muscle",
        "Connective",
        "Epithelial",
        "Nervous"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_nervous_system"
      ],
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "question": "Which tissue type is responsible for transmitting electrical signals?",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "long_explanation": "Nervous tissue is composed of neurons and supporting cells (neuroglia). Neurons transmit impulses that control body functions, reflexes, and thought processes, while neuroglia support and protect the neurons.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_nervous_system"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 006",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z",
      "answers": [
        "Secretes hormones",
        "Contracts to produce movement",
        "Supports and binds other tissues",
        "Initiates nerve impulses"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "histology",
      "long_explanation": "Connective tissue is the most diverse tissue type and includes bone, blood, adipose, and cartilage. It provides structural support, stores energy, transports nutrients, and defends against infection.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_bone",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_bone",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Connective tissue supports, binds, and protects other tissues.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "What is the function of connective tissue?",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "correct": 2,
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 007",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_dna",
        "science_histology"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "Which stain is commonly used to highlight nuclei in histological samples?",
      "short_explanation": "Hematoxylin stains cell nuclei a dark blue or purple color.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_dna",
        "science_histology"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "In histology, hematoxylin is often paired with eosin in the H&E stain. Hematoxylin binds to DNA and stains cell nuclei, which helps pathologists and researchers distinguish nuclear structure and diagnose abnormalities.",
      "unit": "histology",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "correct": 1,
      "answers": [
        "Gram stain",
        "Hematoxylin",
        "Eosin",
        "Safranin"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 008",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_blood_flow",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms"
      ],
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z",
      "unit": "histology",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_protein",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_blood_flow",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "long_explanation": "Muscle tissue contains contractile proteins that allow it to shorten and generate force. This enables body movement, blood flow, and internal organ function. It exists in skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle forms.",
      "short_explanation": "Muscle tissue contracts to produce movement.",
      "answers": [
        "Secretion",
        "Conduction",
        "Contraction",
        "Absorption"
      ],
      "question": "Muscle tissue is specialized for what function?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 009",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "long_explanation": "Although often not thought of as a tissue, blood is classified as connective tissue. Its liquid matrix (plasma) transports nutrients, gases, hormones, and waste products throughout the body and contains cells essential for immunity and clotting.",
      "short_explanation": "Blood is a connective tissue with a liquid matrix called plasma.",
      "unit": "histology",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z",
      "answers": [
        "Adipose",
        "Cartilage",
        "Bone",
        "Blood"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "system_immune",
        "anatomy_matrix"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_cells",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "system_immune",
        "anatomy_matrix"
      ],
      "question": "Which connective tissue has a liquid matrix?",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "correct": 3,
      "difficulty": "intermediate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Histology 010",
      "correct": 1,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [

      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_cancer",
        "science_histology",
        "biochemistry_levels",
        "organ_heart"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "short_explanation": "Histology helps pathologists diagnose tissue abnormalities or disease.",
      "category": "Cell Theory",
      "unit": "histology",
      "answers": [
        "Determining oxygen levels in the blood",
        "Diagnosing tissue abnormalities or disease",
        "Measuring heart rate",
        "Performing surgery"
      ],
      "question": "What is a pathologist most likely to use histology for?",
      "long_explanation": "Pathologists analyze tissue samples microscopically to detect structural changes linked to diseases such as cancer, infection, and autoimmune disorders. Histology provides crucial insights into cellular architecture and helps guide diagnosis and treatment.",
      "topic": "cell_theory",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.461Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.117Z"
    },

    {
      "id": "Massage History 006",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.463Z",
      "long_explanation": "Following Rome’s fall, Western Europe associated massage with superstition, and public health deteriorated.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "european_decline",
        "history_decline"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Why did massage decline in Europe after the Fall of Rome?",
      "topic": "history",
      "short_explanation": "Massage declined due to cultural shifts and hygiene loss.",
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "massage history",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Modern massage professionals can restore respect for touch as a clinical and healing art.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "european_decline",
        "history_decline"
      ],
      "category": "History",
      "correct": 0,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Association with magic and loss of hygiene",
        "Better surgical tools replaced it",
        "Religious support faded",
        "Laws banned it entirely"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.118Z"

      "long_explanation": "Records from the Huangdi Neijing, an ancient Chinese medical text, reference massage as part of therapeutic practice dating back to around 2700 BCE.",

        "Greeks",
        "Egyptians"
      ],

      "clinical_application": "This legacy continues today through acupressure and Tui Na techniques in modern massage practice.",

    {
      "id": "Massage History 002",
      "question": "Who is considered the father of Swedish massage?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.463Z",
      "answers": [
        "Avicenna",
        "Hippocrates",
        "Per Henrik Ling",
        "Johann Mezger"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Per Henrik Ling was a Swedish physician and fencing master who developed a system combining movement and touch known as medical gymnastics, which evolved into Swedish massage.",
      "topic": "history",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "short_explanation": "Ling developed Swedish massage based on gymnastics and physiology.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/history/history_of_massage",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.119Z",
      "stats": {

        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "clinical_application": "Swedish massage remains a foundation of Western massage therapy, emphasizing circulation and muscle relaxation.",
      "keywords": [

        "purpose_relaxation",
        "modality_massage_therapy"
      ],

    {

      "answers": [

        "concept_recovery"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists today still draw on these holistic concepts to support healing and rehabilitation.",
      "question": "Which Greek physician referred to massage as 'an exercise without motion'?",
      "long_explanation": "Hippocrates, the father of Western medicine, described massage as an 'exercise without motion' and included it in treatment for injury, stiffness, and recovery.",

      "difficulty_rating": 3,

      ],

        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "history",
      "short_explanation": "Hippocrates advocated for rubbing as a therapeutic tool.",

      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/history/history_of_massage",
      "short_explanation": "Mezger formalized massage using the French terms still used today.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "mblex_category": "History of Massage",
      "synonyms": [
        "Swedish strokes"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "In the 1800s, Johann Mezger adopted French terminology for massage strokes—effleurage (gliding), petrissage (kneading), friction, and tapotement (percussion)—standardizing Swedish massage techniques.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "history",
      "correct": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3
    },
    {
      "id": "Massage History 005",
      "short_explanation": "Shiatsu is a Japanese massage rooted in Chinese meridian theory.",
      "long_explanation": "Shiatsu uses finger pressure on meridian points to balance qi (energy flow), similar to acupressure. It blends traditional Chinese medicine with Japanese philosophy and touch.",
      "correct": 1,
      "subcategory": "Eastern Traditions",
      "question": "What is the name of the traditional Japanese massage that developed from Chinese medicine?",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,

      "clinical_application": "Abhyanga massage is used to reduce stress and align body–mind constitution in integrative health.",

      "category": "Massage Theory & History",
      "question": "Ayurvedic massage is associated with which traditional system of medicine?",

        "Ayurvedic bodywork"

    {
      "id": "Massage History 007",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "correct": 1,
      "synonyms": [
        "Dr. Kellogg"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "History of Massage",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "history",
      "short_explanation": "Harvey Kellogg promoted massage in his sanitarium and publications.",
      "subcategory": "Modern Contributions",
      "clinical_application": "His work helped normalize massage in Western wellness and rehabilitation settings.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "answers": [
        "Mary Shelley",
        "Harvey Kellogg",
        "Florence Nightingale",
        "John Upledger"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, 6th ed.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/history/history_of_massage",
      "question": "Who helped introduce massage therapy to American medicine in the early 20th century?",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.463Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "Kellogg was a physician who included massage, hydrotherapy, and exercise in his Battle Creek Sanitarium regimen and wrote 'The Art of Massage' in 1895.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage_therapy"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.119Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [

      ],

    },
    {
      "id": "Massage History 008",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "question": "Who founded the craniosacral therapy system in the 20th century?",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.463Z",
      "topic": "history",
      "correct": 0,
      "mblex_category": "History of Massage",
      "synonyms": [

      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Massage Theory & History",
      "tags": [

        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_healing",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_recovery"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "short_explanation": "Dr. John Upledger developed craniosacral therapy after researching cerebrospinal rhythms.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/history/history_of_massage",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.119Z",
      "subcategory": "Contemporary Movements",
      "long_explanation": "An osteopath and researcher, Upledger built on William Sutherland’s cranial osteopathy and created a light-touch therapy to influence CSF flow and nervous system regulation.",
      "source_reference": "Upledger Institute, FSMTB Content Outline",
      "clinical_application": "Craniosacral work is used today in chronic pain, trauma recovery, and nervous system regulation.",
      "answers": [
        "John Upledger",
        "Harvey Kellogg",
        "James Cyriax",
        "Moshe Feldenkrais"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_healing",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_recovery"
      ]
    },

        "Celsus",
        "Socrates"

      "difficulty": "easy",
      "long_explanation": "As the personal physician to Roman emperors and gladiators, Galen promoted massage as part of daily hygiene and recovery protocols.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [

      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.464Z",
      "question": "Who is considered the Father of Medicine and promoted the concept of \"anatripsis\" (rubbing up)?",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "His legacy underpins massage's medical credibility and supports its use in holistic patient care.",
      "keywords": [
        "hippocrates",
        "anatripsis"
      ],
      "topic": "history",
      "long_explanation": "As the Father of Medicine, Hippocrates emphasized balance and physical manipulation, introducing 'anatripsis' to describe massage's healing role.",
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.120Z",
      "category": "History",
      "answers": [
        "Claudius Galen",
        "Asclepius",
        "Hippocrates",
        "Avicenna"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Hippocrates promoted 'anatripsis,' the therapeutic use of rubbing.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "people",
      "last_answered_at": null
    },

      "short_explanation": "Johann Mezger formalized massage into a medical discipline using French terminology.",

      "long_explanation": "Mezger coined massage terms still used today and helped distinguish therapeutic massage from other bodywork.",

      "clinical_application": "Structural bodywork is essential for addressing chronic pain and postural dysfunction in massage clients.",

    {
      "id": "Famous People 001",
      "clinical_application": "Widely used today to help with PTSD, headaches, and nervous system regulation.",
      "category": "Massage Theory & History",
      "question": "Who is considered the founder of craniosacral therapy?",
      "interesting_fact": "Upledger served as clinical researcher at Michigan State University before founding the Upledger Institute.",
      "subcategory": "Famous Contributors",
      "mblex_category": "History of Massage",
      "long_explanation": "An osteopathic physician, Upledger advanced the field of light-touch therapy by exploring cranial rhythms and the flow of cerebrospinal fluid. He formalized craniosacral therapy for use in integrative and trauma care.",
      "answers": [
        "John Upledger",
        "Harvey Kellogg",
        "Per Henrik Ling",
        "Ida Rolf"

    {
      "id": "Famous People 002",
      "tags": [

      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "clinical_application": "Rolfing is used to improve posture, reduce chronic tension, and treat old injuries.",
      "answers": [
        "Ida Rolf",
        "Milton Trager",
        "Per Henrik Ling",
        "James Cyriax"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Rolf Institute, FSMTB Outline",
      "category": "Massage Theory & History",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/history/people/famous_people_massage_2",
      "subcategory": "Famous Contributors",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.120Z",
      "long_explanation": "Rolf was a biochemist who created a method of deep myofascial manipulation intended to balance the body in gravity. Her ten-session protocol remains foundational in somatic therapy.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "History of Massage",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [

        "concept_balance",
        "concept_integration",
        "modality_structural_integration",
        "condition_injuries",
        "concept_tension"
      ],
      "question": "Who created the Rolfing method of structural integration?",
      "short_explanation": "Ida Rolf developed structural integration to realign the body’s fascial system.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "correct": 0,
      "topic": "history",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "interesting_fact": "Ida Rolf earned a Ph.D. in biochemistry from Columbia University in 1920.",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "synonyms": [
        "Rolf",
        "SI"

    },
    {
      "id": "Famous People 003",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.464Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/history/people/famous_people_massage_2",
      "answers": [
        "Avicenna",
        "Johann Mezger",
        "Per Henrik Ling",
        "Harvey Kellogg"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Used as the foundation for general relaxation and circulatory massage protocols.",
      "interesting_fact": "Mezger's work helped legitimize massage as medical treatment in 19th-century Europe.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "synonyms": [

      "subcategory": "Famous Contributors",

      "difficulty": "easy",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Which physician helped standardize Swedish massage techniques using French terminology?",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Johann Mezger was a Dutch physician who helped standardize Swedish massage by adopting French medical terms for massage strokes. These remain part of massage curricula worldwide.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_swedish",
        "purpose_relaxation",
        "exam_terminology",
        "modality_massage_techniques"
      ],
      "category": "Massage Theory & History",
      "mblex_category": "History of Massage",
      "topic": "history",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, FSMTB Outline",
      "correct": 1,
      "short_explanation": "Mezger introduced the terminology for Swedish massage techniques.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.120Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Famous People 004",
      "distractors": [],
      "mblex_category": "History of Massage",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "topic": "history",
      "category": "Massage Theory & History",
      "interesting_fact": "Ling founded the Royal Gymnastic Central Institute in Stockholm in 1813.",
      "clinical_application": "Ling’s methods became the basis for Western sports massage and relaxation techniques.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.120Z",
      "answers": [

      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.464Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Who is recognized as the founder of Swedish massage?",
      "long_explanation": "Per Henrik Ling was a Swedish physiologist and fencing master who created 'medical gymnastics', which evolved into Swedish massage. He emphasized physical movement, anatomy, and physiology.",
      "short_explanation": "Ling developed Swedish massage from movement therapy and anatomy.",
      "synonyms": [
        "Per Ling",
        "Swedish system"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, FSMTB Outline",
      "correct": 1
    },

      "short_explanation": "Kellogg promoted massage as part of holistic treatment.",

      "long_explanation": "Dr. Kellogg integrated massage, diet, hydrotherapy, and exercise at his Battle Creek Sanitarium and authored 'The Art of Massage' in 1895.",

      "interesting_fact": "Kellogg was also instrumental in promoting vegetarianism and early health reform."
    },
    {
      "id": "Famous People 008",
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.120Z",
      "category": "Massage Theory & History",
      "interesting_fact": "Feldenkrais was a judo expert who rehabilitated his own knee injury using body awareness.",
      "mblex_category": "History of Massage",
      "long_explanation": "Originally trained in physics, Moshe Feldenkrais explored how gentle, mindful movement can change posture, coordination, and pain patterns by rewiring the nervous system.",

      "short_explanation": "Ida Rolf was one of the first women to found a named bodywork system.",
      "long_explanation": "Rolf’s deep fascial release work recognized the impact of gravity and structure on health. She taught that body alignment and posture influence function and wellness.",

      "question": "Which pioneer in trigger point therapy co-authored the seminal book on myofascial pain?",

      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {

      ],
      "interesting_fact": "Simons was an aerospace physician before turning to pain medicine.",
      "long_explanation": "Together, they mapped trigger point patterns and provided guidelines for palpation, referred pain, and treatment in massage and physical therapy.",

    {
      "id": "Ethics Boundaries 001",
      "category": "Licensing",
      "correct": 2,
      "unit": "boundaries questions",
      "short_explanation": "Informed consent is permission granted after explaining the treatment details.",
      "long_explanation": "It ensures the client understands and agrees to the treatment plan, including risks, benefits, and boundaries before starting.",
      "answers": [
        "Permission given automatically when the session begins",
        "Permission granted after the session ends",
        "Permission given after full disclosure of treatment details",
        "Verbal agreement during treatment"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "This builds trust, promotes autonomy, and protects both therapist and client legally and ethically.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "technique_fulling",
        "ethics_consent",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "ethics_informed_consent",
        "ethics_best_practices"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "technique_fulling",
        "ethics_consent",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "ethics_informed_consent",
        "ethics_best_practices"
      ],
      "question": "What is informed consent in massage therapy?",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "topic": "licensing",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ethics Boundaries 002",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z",
      "tags": [
        "assessment_soap",
        "ethics_consent",

      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "correct": 1,
      "answers": [
        "It allows the therapist to work freely",
        "It protects both client and therapist legally and ethically",
        "It avoids having to record SOAP notes",
        "It increases tips from clients"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "Why is informed consent important?",
      "short_explanation": "It protects both client and therapist by establishing mutual understanding.",
      "topic": "licensing",
      "unit": "boundaries questions",
      "clinical_application": "It reinforces transparency and professionalism—especially important in sensitive or clinical massage settings.",
      "long_explanation": "Informed consent helps maintain ethical standards, clear communication, and legal protection for both parties.",
      "category": "Licensing",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [

        "client",

        "ethics_protection"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Ethics Boundaries 003",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_deep_tissue",
        "anatomy_tissues",

        "ethics_informed_consent",
        "ethics_treatment_process"
      ],
      "question": "When should a massage therapist obtain informed consent?",
      "short_explanation": "Before every session or when changing the treatment plan.",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Consent is an ongoing process—not a one-time event—required for ethical care delivery.",
      "topic": "licensing",
      "correct": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Ongoing consent allows adjustments in pressure or areas treated without breaching client comfort.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "keywords": [

        "anatomy_tissues",
        "ethics_consent",

        "ethics_treatment_process"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "category": "Licensing",
      "unit": "boundaries questions",
      "answers": [
        "Only for deep tissue work",
        "Only during the first session",
        "Before starting each session or making changes to treatment",
        "Only if the client asks questions"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Ethics Boundaries 004",
      "short_explanation": "A physical boundary refers to respecting the client’s personal space and draping needs.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "Client’s right to personal space and draping",
        "Avoiding political conversations",
        "Listening carefully during sessions",
        "Sharing business cards with clients"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z",
      "unit": "boundaries questions",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "tags": [

      ],

        "boundary_physical"
      ],
      "topic": "licensing",
      "correct": 0,
      "category": "Licensing",
      "clinical_application": "Observing physical boundaries reduces risk of discomfort or misinterpretation during treatment.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Which of the following best describes a physical boundary?",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "It includes ensuring appropriate coverage and requesting permission before touching or adjusting the client’s body."
    },
    {
      "id": "Ethics Boundaries 005",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Licensing",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Clear emotional boundaries protect both the client’s safety and the therapist’s neutrality.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "organ_eyes",
        "boundary_emotional"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Therapists must maintain objectivity, avoid over-identifying, and prevent emotional entanglement with clients.",
      "short_explanation": "Emotional boundaries involve staying professional and limiting personal involvement.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "modality_massage_therapy",
        "organ_eyes",
        "boundary_emotional"
      ],
      "topic": "licensing",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "unit": "boundaries questions",
      "correct": 0,
      "question": "What is an emotional boundary in massage therapy?",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Limiting emotional involvement and maintaining professionalism",
        "Only working on muscles",
        "Using emotional music during sessions",
        "Avoiding eye contact with the client"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ethics Boundaries 006",
      "unit": "boundaries questions",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Respecting the client’s beliefs, thoughts, and opinions",
        "Avoiding any discussion during the session",
        "Focusing only on physical symptoms",
        "Performing techniques only with certification"
      ],
      "topic": "licensing",
      "short_explanation": "It’s the respect for a client’s thoughts, beliefs, and decisions.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z",
      "long_explanation": "Massage therapists should never impose personal beliefs or disregard client input during sessions.",
      "distractors": [],

        "correct": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Ethics Boundaries 007",
      "long_explanation": "Any suggestive remark or action undermines client safety and violates ethical standards.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z",
      "short_explanation": "Sexual boundary violations include inappropriate comments or behavior.",
      "keywords": [

        "ethics_consent",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "boundary_sexual"
      ],
      "answers": [

        "Making suggestive comments during a session",
        "Offering a water bottle after the session"

      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "client",
        "plan_plan",
        "ethics_clarity"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "long_explanation": "Misunderstandings can be harmful. Clear, repeated explanation ensures consent and confidence.",
      "clinical_application": "Checking for understanding improves safety, trust, and outcomes.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z",
      "unit": "boundaries questions"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ethics Boundaries 009",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "tags": [
        "ethics_consent",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "ethics_informed_consent",
        "ethics_maintenance"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "ethics_consent",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "ethics_informed_consent",
        "ethics_maintenance"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "long_explanation": "Boundaries are reinforced with clear dialogue, reliable behavior, and respect for client autonomy.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z",
      "answers": [
        "Avoiding informed consent",
        "Allowing dual relationships without discussion",
        "Clear communication and consistent professional behavior",
        "Treating clients like family"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Maintained through consistent communication and professionalism.",
      "clinical_application": "Maintaining boundaries reduces liability and enhances therapeutic effectiveness.",
      "category": "Licensing",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "Which of the following ensures maintenance of ethical boundaries?",
      "unit": "boundaries questions",
      "topic": "licensing",
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Ethics Boundaries 010",
      "category": "Licensing",
      "unit": "boundaries questions",
      "tags": [
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "boundary_emotional",
        "scope_of_practice"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "boundary_emotional",
        "scope_of_practice"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Therapists are not counselors and must avoid dual roles, maintaining a supportive but clear scope of practice.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.465Z",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "topic": "licensing",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "answers": [
        "Encourage deeper discussion to build trust",
        "Stay quiet and let the client vent",
        "Redirect the session and reinforce professional boundaries",
        "Give advice based on personal opinion"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Redirecting helps maintain emotional boundaries and prevents role confusion or ethical risk.",
      "short_explanation": "Redirect and reaffirm the professional focus of the session.",
      "question": "What should a therapist do if a client begins disclosing deeply personal issues?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Business 001",
      "mblex_category": "Ethics, Boundaries, Laws, Regulations",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/licensing/ethics/business_practices",
      "answers": [
        "6 months",
        "1 year",
        "At least 5 years or as required by state law",
        "Until the client stops scheduling sessions"
      ],
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "role_massage_therapist",
        "ethics_documentation",
        "documentation_client_records"
      ],
      "question": "How long should a massage therapist typically retain client records? [Variant 1]",
      "category": "Professional Practice",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "role_massage_therapist",
        "ethics_documentation",
        "documentation_client_records"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.467Z",
      "long_explanation": "Best practices recommend storing client records for a minimum of 5 years after the last session. Some states require longer. This supports ethical accountability and legal protection.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "source_reference": "FSMTB Business Guidelines",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Maintain a secure and consistent method of documenting and storing session notes.",
      "variant": 1,
      "short_explanation": "Records should be kept at least 5 years or longer if required by law.",
      "topic": "licensing",
      "synonyms": [
        "client chart",
        "massage records"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Client Records",
      "relevance_score": 5
    },
    {
      "id": "Business 002",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "client"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Scheduling",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Include your cancellation terms on intake forms and remind clients periodically.",
      "variant": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "client"
      ],
      "topic": "licensing",
      "correct": 2,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/licensing/ethics/business_practices",
      "question": "What is a best practice for managing client no-shows or late cancellations? [Variant 2]",
      "answers": [
        "Ignore the behavior and rebook anyway",
        "Allow one free no-show per month",
        "Have a written cancellation policy clients agree to in advance",
        "Double-book clients to compensate for missed appointments"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Written policies protect both the therapist and the client relationship.",
      "long_explanation": "Clear policies set expectations and reduce misunderstandings. Requiring clients to acknowledge your policy in writing supports professionalism and accountability.",
      "relevance_score": 4,
      "synonyms": [
        "rescheduling",
        "booking policy"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "category": "Professional Practice",
      "mblex_category": "Ethics, Boundaries, Laws, Regulations",
      "source_reference": "AMTA Practice Management Tips",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.467Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Business 003",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/licensing/ethics/business_practices",
      "correct": 2,
      "long_explanation": "Massage therapists must avoid misleading claims about outcomes. Ethical marketing builds trust and adheres to both legal and professional guidelines.",
      "mblex_category": "Ethics, Boundaries, Laws, Regulations",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "answers": [
        "Using testimonials that guarantee healing",
        "Offering exaggerated claims about massage cures",
        "Using clear, honest descriptions of services and credentials",
        "Selling unrelated wellness products during sessions"
      ],
      "topic": "licensing",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "clinical_application": "Describe techniques and expected benefits based on professional standards, not hype.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.467Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "variant": 3,
      "short_explanation": "Ethical marketing requires honesty and accuracy.",
      "source_reference": "FSMTB Business and Ethics Module",
      "subcategory": "Marketing",
      "question": "Which marketing approach aligns best with ethical massage business practices? [Variant 3]",
      "category": "Professional Practice",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "purpose_healing",
        "purpose_benefit",
        "ethics",
        "business_massage",
        "business_marketing"
      ],
      "keywords": [

        "business_massage",
        "business_marketing"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "relevance_score": 3,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "synonyms": [
        "ethical advertising",
        "clear messaging"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Business 004",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "client",
        "business_massage",
        "ethics_documentation",
        "concept_localized"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Legal Compliance",
      "correct": 2,
      "source_reference": "FSMTB and State Licensing Boards",
      "mblex_category": "Ethics, Boundaries, Laws, Regulations",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/licensing/ethics/business_practices",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "synonyms": [
        "business license",
        "malpractice insurance"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Legal operations require a license and business insurance.",
      "variant": 4,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "client",
        "business_massage",
        "ethics_documentation",
        "concept_localized"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Massage businesses must meet state and local requirements including licensure, zoning, and liability insurance. Operating without them may result in fines or license suspension.",
      "clinical_application": "Keep all documents up to date and posted as required in your workspace.",
      "topic": "licensing",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.467Z",
      "category": "Professional Practice",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "question": "Which of the following is required to legally operate a massage business? [Variant 4]",
      "answers": [
        "Free community services",
        "Monthly specials",
        "Business license and liability insurance",
        "A client membership program"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "relevance_score": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "Business 005",
      "category": "Professional Practice",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "clinical_application": "List all fees and billing policies on your website or intake forms.",
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "Clients deserve transparent and fair billing.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "topic": "licensing",
      "answers": [
        "Charging different rates without disclosing",
        "Padding session time to increase fees",
        "Providing clear invoices and posted rates",
        "Accepting barter instead of payment"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/licensing/ethics/business_practices",
      "question": "Which of the following is considered an ethical billing practice? [Variant 5]",
      "source_reference": "FSMTB Ethics and Business Practices",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.121Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "tags": [
        "ethics",
        "client",
        "business_billing"
      ],
      "synonyms": [
        "honest invoicing",
        "posted rates"
      ],
      "variant": 5,
      "keywords": [
        "ethics",
        "client",
        "business_billing"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Ethical billing includes publishing your rates, explaining fees clearly, and invoicing honestly. Discrepancies or surprises undermine professional credibility.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.467Z",
      "subcategory": "Financial Practices",
      "distractors": [],

    },

      "long_explanation": "Massage therapists must avoid misleading claims about outcomes. Ethical marketing builds trust and adheres to both legal and professional guidelines.",
