        "legal_scope",
        "client_communication"
      ],
      "variant": 10,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "topic": "massage_scope",
      "category": "Legal and Ethical Practice",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "No, that exceeds the scope of practice",
        "Yes, as long as it's non-prescription",
        "Only if requested by the client",
        "Yes, with written consent"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "TuinaVsAmma 001",
      "clinical_application": "Anmo techniques can help manage muscular tension and promote energy flow in clinical massage sessions.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "synonyms": [],
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "short_explanation": "Anmo involves rhythmic pressing and rubbing of soft tissues.",
      "correct": 0,
      "source_reference": "Chinese Massage Therapy Textbook, 3rd Ed.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "question": "What is Anmo? [Variant 1]",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/tuina_vs_amma",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "keywords": [
        "Anmo",
        "Tuina origin",
        "Chinese massage",
        "rhythmic pressure"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Anmo, a forerunner to Tui-na, is a traditional Chinese method that uses pressing, rubbing, and rhythmic hand techniques to influence energy and muscular function.",
      "topic": "massage_modalities",
      "tags": [
        "modality_chinese",
        "history_traditional",
        "soft_tissue_technique"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "An ancient Chinese practice involving rubbing and pressing the body",
        "A Japanese reflexology technique",
        "A Thai yoga massage form",
        "A type of Swedish effleurage"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "variant": 1,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "subcategory": "Tuina vs Amma",
      "relevance_score": 5
    },
    {
      "id": "TuinaVsAmma 002",
      "clinical_application": "Tui-na techniques are especially effective in orthopedic massage and sports injury rehab.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "long_explanation": "Tui-na focuses on specific manipulations like rolling, pushing, kneading, and pulling to treat musculoskeletal and energetic imbalances.",
      "source_reference": "Fundamentals of Traditional Chinese Medicine",
      "subcategory": "Tuina vs Amma",

      ],
      "short_explanation": "Tui-na involves dynamic pushing and pulling movements."

      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/tuina_vs_amma",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "short_explanation": "Amma has roots in traditional Japanese healing.",
      "question": "Which culture developed the massage form known as Amma? [Variant 3]",
      "tags": [
        "modality_amma",
        "modality_japanese",
        "energy_massage"
      ],
      "synonyms": [],
      "long_explanation": "Amma is a traditional Japanese bodywork system, based on Chinese Anmo, adapted and refined into an energetic healing art emphasizing meridians and tsubo points.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "source_reference": "Japanese Bodywork History, AMMA Institute"
    },
    {
      "id": "TuinaVsAmma 004",

      "clinical_application": "Therapists can apply Amma techniques for clients seeking energetic balancing.",

      "short_explanation": "Tsubo are vital energy points used in Eastern massage systems.",
      "question": "What are 'tsubo' in traditional Japanese massage? [Variant 5]",
      "source_reference": "Shiatsu Theory and Practice, 2nd Ed.",
      "clinical_application": "Targeting tsubo can help relieve stagnation, reduce pain, and support overall well-being.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "long_explanation": "Tsubo are acupoints that correspond to meridians; stimulating these points can affect internal organ function and systemic balance.",

      "difficulty": "moderate",

    {
      "id": "TuinaVsAmma 006",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/tuina_vs_amma",
      "question": "What is the purpose of stimulating tsubo points? [Variant 6]",
      "source_reference": "Eastern Energetics and Bodywork Manual",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "short_explanation": "Stimulating tsubo affects both qi and blood movement.",

      "correct": 0,
      "variant": 6,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",

        "vitality",

        "eastern medicine"

      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "long_explanation": "According to Eastern medicine, tsubo stimulation clears stagnation, supports organ function, and restores balance along meridians.",

        "To increase lymphatic fluid viscosity"

    {
      "id": "TuinaVsAmma 007",

      "answers": [

        "Long Swedish gliding strokes",
        "Use of cups or scraping tools"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Anmo sequences help improve range of motion and qi flow during therapeutic massage.",
      "question": "Which of the following best reflects the technique of Anmo? [Variant 7]",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "relevance_score": 5,

      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "The hallmark of Anmo is its use of rhythm and direction in pressing and rubbing to influence both physical and energetic systems.",

    {
      "id": "TuinaVsAmma 008",
      "difficulty": "moderate",

      "short_explanation": "Amma strongly emphasizes qi balance via tsubo.",

      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,

    {
      "id": "AshiatsuIntro 001",
      "category": "Massage Modalities",
      "topic": "ashiatsu",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "subcategory": "Ashiatsu Introduction",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "clinical_application": "Ideal for athletes and clients with dense muscle tissue who need sustained deep tissue work.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "source_reference": "Ashiatsu Barefoot Massage Institute Manual",
      "synonyms": [],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "long_explanation": "Ashiatsu, which means 'foot pressure' in Japanese, involves therapists applying massage using their bare feet. This allows for a deep yet gentle distribution of pressure over large areas.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "answers": [
        "Barefoot massage techniques with deep, broad pressure",
        "Using elbows for myofascial release",
        "Hot stone therapy",
        "Trigger point compression with thumbs"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "variant": 1,
      "question": "What is Ashiatsu massage primarily known for? [Variant 1]",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ashiatsu",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "short_explanation": "Ashiatsu involves using the therapist’s feet for broad, deep pressure.",

        "broad pressure"

      "question": "Which body part is primarily used by the therapist during Ashiatsu? [Variant 2]",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "short_explanation": "Ashiatsu is performed using the therapist’s feet.",
      "long_explanation": "Therapists walk or glide on clients using overhead bars for control, distributing pressure through their feet to access deep muscles safely.",

      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "category": "Massage Modalities",
      "short_explanation": "Ideal for dense or athletic muscle types.",
      "long_explanation": "The broad pressure of Ashiatsu penetrates deep muscle layers, making it suitable for athletes, chronic tension, or large muscle groups like the back and thighs.",
      "synonyms": [],
      "answers": [
        "Athletes and individuals needing deep tissue work",
        "Children under 10",
        "Clients with osteoporosis",
        "Post-op patients"
      ],
      "correct": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "question": "Ashiatsu is most beneficial for which type of client? [Variant 3]",
      "clinical_application": "Effective in improving ROM, reducing scar tissue, and addressing deep-seated knots."
    },
    {
      "id": "AshiatsuIntro 004",
      "clinical_application": "Enhances therapist ergonomics and protects the spine and wrists.",

      "short_explanation": "Overhead bars help guide and stabilize the therapist.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ashiatsu",
      "topic": "ashiatsu",
      "answers": [

        "To allow inversion therapy"

      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "variant": 4,
      "question": "Why are overhead bars often used in Ashiatsu massage? [Variant 4]",

    {

      "long_explanation": "The name reflects the core technique of the modality—applying pressure with feet instead of hands.",

      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "question": "Compared to traditional deep tissue massage, Ashiatsu provides: [Variant 6]",
      "long_explanation": "Feet can cover more surface area than hands, allowing deeper pressure with less discomfort. This results in greater muscle penetration with less pain response.",

        "pressure_distribution",

    {
      "id": "AshiatsuIntro 010",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "topic": "ashiatsu",
      "clinical_application": "Used in clients with tight paraspinals, sciatica, or poor posture.",
      "question": "Ashiatsu is particularly effective for which of the following outcomes? [Variant 10]",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "source_reference": "Massage Therapy for Orthopedic Conditions, Ch. 7",
      "synonyms": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T14:45:36.840188Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "subcategory": "Ashiatsu Introduction",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ashiatsu",
      "long_explanation": "Broad, gravity-aided foot pressure allows tractioning of the spine and myofascial release, promoting flexibility and pain relief.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "variant": 10,
      "category": "Massage Modalities",
      "short_explanation": "Ashiatsu decompresses the spine and targets deep muscles.",

      "keywords": [
        "spine",

        "deep tissue",
        "ashiatsu outcome"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Elongating the spine and relieving deep muscular tension",
        "Increasing skin exfoliation",
        "Lymphatic drainage of the limbs",
        "Correcting vertebral misalignments"
      ],

        "spinal_elongation",
        "myofascial_release",
        "deep_relaxation"
      ],

    {
      "id": "Ayurveda 001",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "clinical_application": "Understanding the origin of the term reinforces Ayurveda's foundation as a wellness system, not just a treatment method.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ayurveda_intro",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "The Sanskrit word 'Ayurveda' combines 'ayur' (life) and 'veda' (knowledge or science), representing a holistic system that emphasizes the balance of body, mind, and spirit.",
      "question": "What does the word 'Ayurveda' mean?",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "tags": [
        "purpose_healing",
        "concept_balance",
        "modality_ayurveda"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "purpose_healing",
        "concept_balance",
        "modality_ayurveda"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "answers": [
        "Ancient science",
        "Healing hands",
        "Knowledge of life",
        "Body balance"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.128Z",
      "short_explanation": "Ayurveda means 'knowledge of life.'",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "source_reference": "Ayurveda Foundations Manual",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "modalities"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ayurveda 002",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "Ayurveda is a traditional Indian healing system developed through centuries of empirical observation and philosophical insight into natural balance and human health.",
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "China",
        "Egypt",
        "India",
        "Greece"
      ],
      "question": "Ayurveda originated in which country?",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.128Z",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ayurveda_intro",
      "short_explanation": "Ayurveda originated in India over 3,000 years ago.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_ayurveda",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "modality_ayurveda",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "clinical_application": "Practitioners should recognize its deep cultural and historical roots in Indian medicine.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "source_reference": "Ayurveda Foundations Manual"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ayurveda 003",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "question": "What are the three doshas in Ayurveda?",
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Ayurveda Foundations Manual",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "modality_ayurveda"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ayurveda_intro",
      "answers": [
        "Kapha, Pitta, Vayu",
        "Earth, Wind, Fire",
        "Kapha, Vata, Pitta",
        "Vata, Soma, Agni"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Assessing doshic constitution can inform customized massage plans, oil selection, and treatment pressure.",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "short_explanation": "Kapha, Vata, and Pitta are the three doshas.",
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.128Z",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "long_explanation": "In Ayurvedic theory, these doshas are the bioenergetic forces that govern physiological activity in the body, and imbalance among them is believed to lead to illness.",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_ayurveda"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Ayurveda 004",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "tags": [
        "modality_ayurveda"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "question": "Which dosha is associated with movement and air?",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "topic": "modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "answers": [
        "Pitta",
        "Vata",
        "Kapha",
        "Prana"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ayurveda_intro",
      "clinical_application": "Massage for Vata-dominant clients should be grounding, rhythmic, and warm.",
      "long_explanation": "Vata is one of the three doshas and controls mobility, nervous system function, and circulation. It’s linked to creativity and flexibility, but when imbalanced, it may lead to anxiety and dryness.",
      "keywords": [
        "ayurveda",
        "doshas",
        "vata",
        "pitta",
        "kapha"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Vata governs movement and is associated with air and space.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Ayurveda Foundations Manual",
      "correct": 1,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.128Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ayurveda 005",
      "correct": 1,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Pitta governs digestion and metabolism.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ayurveda_intro",
      "source_reference": "Ayurveda Foundations Manual",
      "long_explanation": "Pitta is related to the fire and water elements and is responsible for all metabolic and transformational processes in the body, including digestion, hormones, and body temperature.",
      "answers": [
        "Vata",
        "Pitta",
        "Kapha",
        "Ojas"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Massage for Pitta clients should be cooling and calming, avoiding excessive heat or stimulation.",
      "keywords": [
        "ayurveda",
        "doshas",
        "vata",
        "pitta",
        "kapha"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.128Z",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "tags": [
        "modality_ayurveda"
      ],
      "category": "Modalities",
      "question": "Which dosha governs metabolism and digestion?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ayurveda 006",
      "keywords": [
        "ayurveda",
        "doshas",
        "vata",
        "pitta",
        "kapha"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "answers": [
        "Fire",
        "Air",
        "Earth and Water",
        "Ether"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "Massage can stimulate Kapha through vigorous, warming techniques and invigorating oils.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "short_explanation": "Kapha is associated with earth and water.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_ayurveda"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ayurveda_intro",
      "source_reference": "Ayurveda Foundations Manual",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Which element is primarily associated with Kapha dosha?",
      "correct": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Kapha governs structure and stability. Its qualities are heavy, slow, and moist, providing cohesion to the body. Imbalances can lead to lethargy or congestion.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.128Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "difficulty": "moderate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Ayurveda 007",
      "answers": [
        "Agni",
        "Prana",
        "Dosha",
        "Dhatu"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/ayurveda_intro",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "short_explanation": "Prana is considered the vital life energy in Ayurveda.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "source_reference": "Ayurveda Foundations Manual",
      "correct": 1,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [

      },
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.128Z",
      "question": "In Ayurveda, what is the term for life energy or vitality?",
      "long_explanation": "Prana refers to the life force that animates all living beings and flows through breath, food, and thought. It is closely linked with Vata and the nervous system.",
      "clinical_application": "Massage can help circulate and unblock Prana through breath-focused and intentional touch.",
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_ayurveda"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Ayurveda 008",
      "keywords": [
        "system_immune",
        "system_digestive",
        "physiology_sleep",
        "modality_ayurveda"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Ayurveda Foundations Manual",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "question": "What is the purpose of Panchakarma in Ayurveda?",
      "long_explanation": "Panchakarma is a therapeutic process involving oil massages, herbal treatments, enemas, and fasting that eliminates accumulated toxins (ama) from the body.",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "Panchakarma aims to detoxify and cleanse the body.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 1,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "clinical_application": "Massage is an essential preparatory and supportive therapy in Panchakarma protocols.",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "answers": [
        "Boost immunity",
        "Cleanse the body of toxins",
        "Promote deep sleep",
        "Stimulate digestion"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.128Z",

      "stats": {

        "attempts": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Head massage with Brahmi oil is commonly used to reduce stress and support nervous system balance.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.128Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "long_explanation": "Brahmi (Bacopa monnieri) enhances cognitive function, memory, and mental clarity, often used in Ayurvedic formulations and massage oils.",

      "last_answered_at": null,

    },
    {
      "id": "Ayurveda 010",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "What is the Ayurvedic term for digestive fire?",
      "short_explanation": "Agni is the digestive fire in Ayurvedic physiology.",
      "source_reference": "Ayurveda Foundations Manual",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",

      "answers": [

        "Ama"

      "clinical_application": "Therapeutic massage can support Agni by improving circulation, reducing stress, and aiding detoxification.",

      },
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,

      "long_explanation": "Agni represents the transformative energy that governs digestion, absorption, and assimilation. Weak agni leads to toxin buildup (ama) and poor health.",

    {
      "id": "ChavuttiThirumal 001",

      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/chavutti_thirumal",
      "long_explanation": "Rooted in the martial art of Kalaripayattu, Chavutti Thirumal is a barefoot massage technique developed in Kerala, India. It emphasizes energy alignment, flexibility, and musculoskeletal therapy.",

      "relevance_score": 5,
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Massage Techniques",

      "synonyms": [],
      "clinical_application": "Useful for clients seeking flexibility, deep pressure, and energetic alignment through ancient techniques.",
      "answers": [

      ],

      "question": "Which body part is used by the therapist in Chavutti Thirumal? [Variant 2]",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "Chavutti is a barefoot massage technique.",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "subcategory": "Chavutti Thirumal & Barefoot Massage",
      "long_explanation": "The therapist uses their foot—often a single foot—for long strokes along the client's body while holding a rope overhead for balance and control.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/chavutti_thirumal"
    },
    {
      "id": "ChavuttiThirumal 003",

      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "source_reference": "Kerala Ayurvedic Institute Curriculum",
      "long_explanation": "Often used in Ayurvedic rejuvenation programs (like Panchakarma), Chavutti Thirumal supports detoxification, circulation, and alignment of bodily systems.",

    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 001",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "concept_focuses",
        "concept_targeted",
        "concept_tension",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "question": "What is the primary goal of connective tissue massage (CTM)?",
      "long_explanation": "The primary goal of connective tissue massage is to influence visceral and systemic health by stimulating specific dermatomal regions. Rather than focusing on deep muscle, CTM works through skin traction and tension, affecting the sympathetic nervous system and related organ functions through shared spinal segments.",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "CTM targets skin and superficial fascia to affect the autonomic nervous system via dermatomes.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "applied_example": "For clients with digestive complaints, a therapist may perform CTM along dermatomes associated with the mid-thoracic spine to reflexively influence gut motility and relaxation.",
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "answers": [
        "To apply deep pressure to muscle bellies",
        "To stimulate the lymphatic system only",
        "To affect autonomic nervous system responses through dermatomes",
        "To stretch the tendons directly"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z",
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",

      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 002",
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "question": "Connective tissue massage was originally developed by:",
      "correct": 1,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "Elizabeth Dicke developed CTM in Germany in the 1920s following personal vascular illness.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "answers": [
        "Ida Rolf",
        "Elizabeth Dicke",
        "Per Henrik Ling",
        "Janet Travell"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_rolfing",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "founder_elizabeth_dicke",
        "founder_ida_rolf",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "category": "Modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_rolfing",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "founder_elizabeth_dicke",
        "founder_ida_rolf",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "concept_originates",
        "concept_originated",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Elizabeth Dicke is credited with developing CTM after self-applying traction to her skin to relieve chronic circulatory issues. She discovered reflexive changes in organ function and systemic relief, leading to a structured method now widely practiced in Europe.",
      "applied_example": "Understanding the history of CTM helps therapists appreciate its focus on neuromuscular and visceral reflex arcs rather than mechanical depth.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 003",
      "applied_example": "Massage therapists using CTM focus on shallow traction across the skin rather than deep strokes, aiming to release fascial adhesions and influence nervous system tone.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "correct": 2,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "concept_targeted",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "CTM primarily targets the subcutaneous fascia, just beneath the skin.",
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z",
      "question": "Which layer of the body does connective tissue massage primarily target?",
      "answers": [
        "Epidermis",
        "Adipose tissue",
        "Subcutaneous fascia",
        "Muscle fibers"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "The subcutaneous fascia is a layer of connective tissue located between the skin and underlying muscles. CTM uses specific pulls and stretches to engage this layer and affect autonomic responses. The goal is not muscular manipulation, but rather fascial mobilization with reflex effects.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "concept_targeted",
        "organ_skin"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 004",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z",
      "applied_example": "In CTM, a therapist might use pulling strokes along the lower back to influence pelvic or bladder reflexes in clients with urinary tension or low back dysfunction.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "question": "What is a hallmark technique of connective tissue massage?",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "technique_rocking",
        "technique_tapping",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "anatomy_meridians",
        "concept_tension",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "technique_rocking",
        "technique_tapping",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "anatomy_meridians",
        "concept_tension",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "answers": [
        "Gentle rocking and jostling",
        "Slow, superficial gliding strokes",
        "Short, pulling strokes that create tension in the skin",
        "Rhythmic tapping over meridians"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "CTM uses short, pulling strokes that create tension in the skin and fascia.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "long_explanation": "A hallmark of CTM is the use of deliberate, shallow pulls on the skin in specific directions aligned with dermatomes. This technique produces a sensory input that reflexively alters organ tone, vascular tone, or sympathetic activity."
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 005",
      "question": "Connective tissue massage is commonly used in Europe to address:",
      "answers": [
        "Cardiac arrhythmias",
        "Visceral dysfunctions and organ referral patterns",
        "Broken bones",
        "Acute skin infections"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "CTM is widely used in Europe for visceral and autonomic dysfunctions.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "correct": 1,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "anatomy_zones",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "In European practice, CTM is used to influence internal organ function, especially in cases of irritable bowel, menstrual cramps, or stress-related hypertension. By working dermatomally, therapists stimulate reflex zones that correspond to internal organ innervation.",
      "applied_example": "Clients with tension linked to digestive discomfort may benefit from CTM along thoracic and lumbar dermatomes related to the small and large intestines.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "purpose_stress",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "anatomy_zones",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 006",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "anatomy_zones",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "answers": [
        "Stimulation of lymph nodes",
        "Direct contact with nerves under the skin",
        "Reflexive stimulation through shared spinal segments",
        "Increased muscle strength"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z",
      "question": "Which of the following best describes the mechanism behind CTM’s effect on internal organs?",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "long_explanation": "CTM relies on the principle that skin and organs sharing the same spinal segments can reflexively affect one another. By stimulating dermatomal skin zones, therapists may influence underlying visceral function without direct internal contact.",
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "anatomy_zones",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "applied_example": "CTM over the T4-T6 dermatomes might be used to regulate tension linked to respiratory or cardiac dysfunction, offering an indirect approach to systemic balance.",
      "short_explanation": "CTM affects internal organs via spinal reflexes in shared dermatomal segments.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 007",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "applied_example": "If a client experiences pelvic organ tension, a therapist may apply CTM to sacral dermatomes S2-S4 to reflexively affect those organs.",
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "correct": 2,
      "question": "In connective tissue massage, lines of pull are often determined by:",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "answers": [
        "Trigger points",
        "Postural patterns",
        "Dermatomes",
        "Joint alignment"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Lines of pull in CTM are aligned with dermatomes-skin areas innervated by a single spinal nerve. These patterns are mapped and selected based on the client's symptoms and targeted autonomic or visceral regions.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "client",
        "anatomy_zones",
        "concept_targeted",
        "joint_joint",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "category": "Modalities",
      "short_explanation": "CTM uses dermatome patterns to determine treatment zones.",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_joint",

        "client",

        "concept_targeted",
        "joint_joint",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate"
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 008",
      "applied_example": "A therapist should inform clients about these sensations ahead of time to reduce anxiety and encourage breathing through the discomfort as adhesions are gently addressed.",
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "client_sensation",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "short_explanation": "Clients often report a burning or stinging sensation during CTM as fascial adhesions are released.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_ctm",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "client_sensation",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "question": "What sensation is commonly reported by clients during connective tissue massage?",
      "answers": [
        "Numbness",
        "Tickling",
        "Burning or stinging as adhesions release",
        "Cold chills"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "long_explanation": "The pulling techniques used in CTM can produce burning, stinging, or prickling sensations-especially in areas with dense or adhered fascia. These sensations are expected and reflect the neurological stimulation of the skin and underlying fascia.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 009",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "applied_example": "Therapists using CTM for asthma may target dermatomes related to lung function, such as T2-T5, to help regulate respiratory tone and reduce stress-induced flare-ups.",
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 3,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "High blood pressure",
        "Asthma",
        "Liver disease",
        "All of the above"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "CTM may benefit high blood pressure, asthma, and liver function via autonomic modulation.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "tags": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_benefit",
        "concept_balance",
        "concept_tension",
        "organ_liver"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_benefit",
        "concept_balance",
        "concept_tension",
        "organ_liver"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z",
      "question": "Which condition may benefit from CTM's effects on autonomic balance?",
      "long_explanation": "By influencing the sympathetic and parasympathetic balance, CTM can support conditions linked to dysautonomia. It's been used in Europe to complement medical treatment of conditions like hypertension, bronchial asthma, and liver dysfunction-especially when emotional tension plays a role.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "category": "Modalities"
    },
    {
      "id": "Connective Tissue Massage 010",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 1,
      "applied_example": "Therapists trained in both CTM and deep tissue may choose CTM for clients with visceral or stress-linked tension, especially when deep work would be contraindicated or overstimulating.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.130Z",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_deep_tissue",
        "modality_ctm",
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "modality_oils",
        "concept_focuses",
        "concept_targeted",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "CTM focuses on superficial skin-fascial pulls, unlike deep tissue massage which targets muscles.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.477Z",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "unit": "connective tissue massage",
      "long_explanation": "CTM distinguishes itself from deep tissue massage by working primarily on the skin and subcutaneous fascia, using traction techniques to stimulate reflex arcs. It does not aim to release muscle knots or use compressive force, but instead accesses autonomic pathways through the skin.",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "question": "Connective tissue massage differs from deep tissue massage in that:",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_deep_tissue",
        "modality_ctm",

        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "modality_oils",
        "concept_focuses",
        "concept_targeted",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "category": "Modalities",
      "answers": [
        "It only uses the elbows for pressure",
        "It focuses on superficial skin-fascial pulls rather than deep muscle work",
        "It uses oil for better glide",
        "It requires physician supervision"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 001",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "question": "What is the primary function of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)?",
      "answers": [
        "Digesting nutrients",
        "Transporting oxygen",
        "Protecting and nourishing the brain and spinal cord",
        "Regulating heart rate"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "purpose_stress",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "system_digestive",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_tension",
        "organ_heart",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "Cerebrospinal fluid surrounds the brain and spinal cord, acting as a shock absorber while also delivering nutrients and removing waste. It plays a vital role in central nervous system homeostasis.",
      "short_explanation": "CSF cushions and nourishes the brain and spinal cord.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "purpose_stress",
        "physiology_homeostasis",
        "system_digestive",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_tension",
        "organ_heart",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "In craniosacral therapy, optimizing CSF flow is key to improving neurological health and relieving tension."
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 002",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "short_explanation": "The choroid plexus produces most of the body's CSF.",
      "clinical_application": "Knowledge of CSF production is essential when treating conditions related to cranial pressure or fluid imbalance.",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Medulla oblongata",
        "Cerebral cortex",
        "Choroid plexus",
        "Cerebellum"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "CSF is mainly produced by the choroid plexus within the brain's ventricles. These structures filter blood plasma and secrete CSF into the ventricular system.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "tags": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Where is cerebrospinal fluid primarily produced?",
      "correct": 2,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "organ_brain"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 003",
      "short_explanation": "CSF is reabsorbed through the arachnoid villi into venous circulation.",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question": "Which structure reabsorbs cerebrospinal fluid into the bloodstream?",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "concept_balance"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "answers": [
        "Corpus callosum",
        "Arachnoid villi",
        "Basal ganglia",
        "Pineal gland"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "keywords": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "concept_balance"
      ],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "clinical_application": "Restricted CSF reabsorption can lead to intracranial pressure issues—important for craniosacral practitioners to monitor.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "long_explanation": "Arachnoid villi are small projections into the superior sagittal sinus where CSF reenters the bloodstream. This reabsorption maintains fluid balance in the central nervous system.",
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 004",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "clinical_application": "Alterations in this rhythm can indicate dysfunction and help guide therapeutic intervention.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "question": "What is the normal rhythm of cerebrospinal fluid in craniosacral therapy?",
      "answers": [
        "20–25 cycles per minute",
        "2–4 cycles per minute",
        "6–12 cycles per minute",
        "12–20 cycles per minute"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "exam_assessment",
        "indication",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "correct": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "tags": [
        "exam_assessment",
        "indication",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "long_explanation": "The craniosacral rhythm is a subtle pulsation distinct from the cardiovascular or respiratory rhythms. It reflects the ebb and flow of CSF and is palpable by trained hands.",
      "short_explanation": "CST practitioners assess the CSF rhythm at 6–12 cycles/minute."
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 005",
      "answers": [
        "Produces cerebrospinal fluid",
        "Anchors muscles to bone",
        "Contains and guides CSF flow",
        "Transmits sensory nerve impulses"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "question": "What role does the dura mater play in cerebrospinal fluid dynamics?",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "long_explanation": "The dura mater forms part of the meninges that surround and protect the brain and spinal cord. It helps maintain the pressure system for CSF and is a focus in CST for releasing restrictions.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "short_explanation": "The dura mater contains and helps direct the flow of CSF.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "purpose_stress",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "concept_focus",
        "concept_tension",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "clinical_application": "Freeing dural restrictions can normalize CSF flow and reduce chronic tension.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "purpose_stress",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "concept_focus",
        "concept_tension",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 006",
      "correct": 1,
      "short_explanation": "CSF motion is a key indicator of cranial system function.",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "tags": [
        "modality_lymphatic_drainage",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "indication",
        "concept_balance",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "question": "Why is cerebrospinal fluid important in craniosacral therapy?",
      "clinical_application": "Practitioners assess CSF rhythm to guide gentle corrections throughout the cranial-sacral axis.",
      "long_explanation": "CST is based on the principle that CSF has a measurable rhythm that reflects the body’s internal balance. Restrictions in its motion can relate to dysfunction or trauma.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_lymphatic_drainage",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "exam_assessment",
        "indication",
        "concept_balance",
        "biochemistry_glucose",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy"
      ],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "It provides glucose to muscles",
        "Its movement reflects cranial health and vitality",
        "It increases lymphatic drainage",
        "It strengthens skeletal muscles"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 007",
      "answers": [
        "Improved fluid dynamics",
        "Healthy tissue tone",
        "Possible system restriction or blockage",
        "Vibrant metabolic function"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "purpose_stress",
        "exam_assessment",
        "indication",
        "concept_localized",
        "concept_tension"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "correct": 2,
      "long_explanation": "When CSF rhythm is weak or asymmetrical, it can indicate fascial tension, trauma, or fluid imbalance. This serves as a cue to explore related structures or diaphragms.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Used diagnostically to localize restrictions and assess progress after treatment.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "purpose_stress",
        "exam_assessment",
        "indication",
        "concept_localized",
        "concept_tension"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "A diminished CSF rhythm may suggest a blockage or fascial restriction.",
      "question": "What could a diminished CSF rhythm indicate in CST assessment?",
      "topic": "modalities"
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 008",
      "correct": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Often produces deep relaxation and is used to signal therapeutic integration in a session.",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "purpose_relaxation",
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_integration",
        "concept_tension"
      ],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "A still point is a therapeutic pause in the CSF rhythm.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "question": "Which term describes the momentary pause in CSF rhythm often induced in CST?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "long_explanation": "The still point is a brief suspension of craniosacral motion that allows the body to reset and release tension. It can occur naturally or be gently facilitated by the therapist.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "purpose_relaxation",
        "purpose_stress",
        "concept_integration",
        "concept_tension"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Respiratory hold",
        "Cardiac stop",
        "Still point",
        "Fluid stasis"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 009",
      "short_explanation": "Hydrocephalus is caused by excess CSF buildup in the brain.",
      "correct": 1,
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "tags": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "organ_brain"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "category": "Modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "Hydrocephalus occurs when CSF accumulates due to impaired drainage or overproduction, leading to pressure on brain tissue. It's a condition that underscores the importance of proper CSF regulation.",
      "answers": [
        "Hypertension",
        "Hydrocephalus",
        "Diabetes",
        "Asthma"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "clinical_application": "Though medical management is required, CST may support postural decompression and promote comfort.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Which condition is most closely linked with abnormal CSF pressure or flow?"
    },
    {
      "id": "Csf 010",
      "clinical_application": "Helps reduce headaches, nervous system tension, and improve fluid dynamics holistically.",
      "question": "How does craniosacral therapy influence cerebrospinal fluid flow?",
      "short_explanation": "CST influences CSF flow through gentle touch and dural work.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/cerebrospinal_fluid",
      "long_explanation": "Practitioners use light palpation to release restrictions in cranial bones and connective tissues that house CSF pathways. This restores balance and fluid movement throughout the central nervous system.",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 1,
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_membrane",

      "keywords": [

        "modality_massage_techniques",
        "concept_restoration",
        "concept_tension",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Through forceful joint traction",
        "Via gentle manipulation of cranial bones and dural membranes",
        "By stimulating muscle contraction",
        "Through lymphatic massage techniques"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 001",
      "answers": [
        "Increasing blood pressure",
        "Releasing fascial restrictions in the craniosacral system",
        "Strengthening skeletal muscles",
        "Enhancing lymphatic drainage"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Craniosacral therapy (CST) is a gentle, hands-on modality that targets the membranes and cerebrospinal fluid surrounding the brain and spinal cord. Its primary goal is to identify and release restrictions in the craniosacral system to improve central nervous system function and overall well-being.",
      "short_explanation": "Craniosacral therapy aims to release restrictions in the craniosacral system to enhance the body’s natural healing.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "tags": [
        "modality_lymphatic_drainage",

      ],
      "keywords": [
        "modality_lymphatic_drainage",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_nervous_system",

        "concept_assists",

      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "question": "What is the primary goal of craniosacral therapy?",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "clinical_application": "Practitioners may use CST techniques to assist clients dealing with migraines, chronic pain, and nervous system dysregulation.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 1
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 002",
      "long_explanation": "Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) plays a crucial role in protecting and nourishing the brain and spinal cord. CST practitioners aim to detect and normalize the rhythm of CSF, which they believe affects the health of the entire body.",
      "short_explanation": "CST focuses on the movement of cerebrospinal fluid around the brain and spinal cord.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "purpose_stress",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_focuses",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Synovial fluid",
        "Lymph",
        "Cerebrospinal fluid",
        "Blood plasma"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Modalities",
      "keywords": [
        "purpose_stress",
        "anatomy_brain",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_focuses",
        "organ_brain",
        "organ_spinal_cord"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "clinical_application": "Subtle palpation of CSF flow helps guide therapeutic decisions, especially in neurological or stress-related complaints.",
      "question": "What fluid does craniosacral therapy focus on?",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 003",
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Craniosacral therapy was developed by which osteopathic physician?",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "long_explanation": "In the 1970s and 1980s, Dr. John Upledger expanded on early cranial concepts introduced by William Sutherland to create craniosacral therapy. He integrated osteopathic principles with light-touch techniques to access the craniosacral system.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "correct": 0,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_rolfing",
        "founder_ida_rolf",
        "concept_integration",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Upledger’s approach is widely taught in massage and physical therapy programs for its gentle effectiveness.",
      "answers": [
        "John Upledger",
        "Andrew Taylor Still",
        "Janet Travell",
        "Ida Rolf"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "modality_rolfing",
        "founder_ida_rolf",
        "concept_integration",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Dr. John Upledger is credited with developing and popularizing craniosacral therapy.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 004",
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "Still point induction is a CST technique that encourages relaxation by pausing the craniosacral rhythm.",
      "answers": [
        "Muscle stripping",
        "Myofascial unwinding",
        "Still point induction",
        "Deep tissue compression"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Useful for clients with chronic stress, tension headaches, or anxiety-related symptoms.",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "A still point occurs when the rhythm of cerebrospinal fluid temporarily stops or slows, allowing the body to reset and reorganize. Inducing this state through gentle contact (often at the occiput) is believed to promote deep healing and parasympathetic activation.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Which of the following is a technique used in craniosacral therapy?",
      "tags": [
        "modality_deep_tissue",
        "modality_myofascial",
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_relaxation",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_healing",
        "client",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_tension",
        "concept_anxiety"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "modality_deep_tissue",
        "modality_myofascial",
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_relaxation",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_healing",
        "client",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_tension",
        "concept_anxiety"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 005",
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "question": "What type of touch is characteristic of craniosacral therapy?",
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pounding",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_fibromyalgia",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Modalities",
      "answers": [
        "5–10 pounds of pressure",
        "Firm gliding strokes",
        "Feather-light contact, under 5 grams of pressure",
        "Percussion techniques"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "technique_pounding",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_fibromyalgia",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "short_explanation": "CST uses very light touch—often less than the weight of a nickel.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "long_explanation": "Craniosacral therapy relies on subtle palpation skills to detect minute movements and rhythms. The light touch is meant to interact with the craniosacral rhythm without engaging the muscle or skeletal systems directly.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Ideal for sensitive clients, including those with fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, or trauma.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 006",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "exam_assessment",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_focus",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "bone_temporal",
        "bone_sacrum",
        "bone_ilium"
      ],
      "question": "Which structure is not directly assessed during craniosacral therapy?",
      "answers": [
        "Sacrum",
        "Temporal bones",
        "Ilium",
        "Deltoid muscle"
      ],
      "correct": 3,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "The deltoid is not part of the craniosacral system.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "anatomy_spine",
        "exam_assessment",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_focus",
        "muscle_deltoid",
        "bone_temporal",
        "bone_sacrum",
        "bone_ilium"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "clinical_application": "Focus remains on the cranial sutures, spinal alignment, and sacral movement.",
      "distractors": [],
      "long_explanation": "CST practitioners primarily work with bones of the cranium, spine, sacrum, and associated fascia—especially those influencing the dural membrane system. While adjacent structures may respond to treatment, skeletal muscles like the deltoid are not directly manipulated.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 007",
      "correct": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Deviations in the rhythm’s quality, symmetry, or amplitude may suggest areas of restriction.",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "exam_assessment",
        "bone_sacrum"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "short_explanation": "The craniosacral rhythm is a subtle pulsation of CSF flow.",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "question": "What is the typical rhythm practitioners assess during CST?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "long_explanation": "The craniosacral rhythm, also called the primary respiratory mechanism, is typically palpated at a rate of 6–12 cycles per minute. Skilled therapists feel for this motion at the skull, sacrum, or feet.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "exam_assessment",
        "bone_sacrum"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Cardiac rhythm",
        "Respiratory rhythm",
        "Craniosacral rhythm",
        "Circadian rhythm"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 008",
      "correct": 1,
      "clinical_application": "Used for pelvic alignment, calming the nervous system, and supporting recovery after labor.",
      "distractors": [],
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_relaxation",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_healing",
        "purpose_benefit",
        "client",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_recovery",
        "concept_tension",
        "concept_calm",
        "modality_modality"
      ],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "short_explanation": "CST can be beneficial for postpartum recovery due to its gentle approach.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "question": "Which client population may particularly benefit from craniosacral therapy?",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "long_explanation": "Postpartum individuals may experience tension, pain, or nervous system dysregulation due to childbirth. CST offers a non-invasive option to relieve cranial and sacral imbalances, especially when other modalities may feel too intense.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_nervous_system",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_relaxation",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_healing",
        "purpose_benefit",
        "client",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "concept_recovery",
        "concept_tension",
        "concept_calm",
        "modality_modality"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Competitive bodybuilders",
        "Postpartum individuals",
        "Healthy teenagers",
        "Long-distance runners only"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z"
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 009",
      "answers": [
        "Joint range of motion",
        "Myotome function",
        "Fascial tension and rhythmic motion",
        "Muscle contraction strength"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "CST therapists feel for fascial restrictions and rhythmic craniosacral motion.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "purpose_stress",
        "exam_assessment",
        "concept_focus",
        "concept_tension",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.131Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "correct": 2,
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "May help identify areas of chronic tension that are not apparent through traditional physical assessments.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "tags": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_muscle_contraction",
        "purpose_stress",
        "exam_assessment",
        "concept_focus",
        "concept_tension",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "question": "What does a craniosacral therapist typically assess with their hands?",
      "long_explanation": "With gentle palpation, practitioners assess the motion of the craniosacral system, fascial ease or restriction, and overall energetic flow. Unlike orthopedic assessment, the focus is on subtle rhythmic patterns rather than gross movement."
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral 010",
      "long_explanation": "While many practitioners and clients report benefits, craniosacral therapy remains controversial in the scientific community due to limited high-quality research. The subtle nature of its techniques makes clinical trials challenging, but anecdotal and clinical reports support its use for certain populations.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_benefit",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "client",
        "concept_integration",
