      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.478Z",
      "clinical_application": "Practitioners should explain benefits and limitations transparently and integrate CST with other evidence-based care when appropriate.",
      "correct": 1,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "answers": [
        "It causes muscle soreness",
        "It lacks strong scientific validation",
        "It is too aggressive",
        "It requires expensive equipment"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "CST is often criticized for lacking robust scientific evidence.",
      "question": "Which criticism is most commonly directed at craniosacral therapy?",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/craniosacral_intro",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Modalities"
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 001",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "clinical_application": "Releasing restrictions in these diaphragms helps improve cerebrospinal fluid flow and postural alignment.",
      "long_explanation": "The five primary diaphragms addressed in craniosacral therapy are the pelvic diaphragm, respiratory diaphragm, thoracic inlet, hyoid diaphragm, and tentorium cerebelli. Each one plays a key role in maintaining structural and fluidic continuity.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.132Z",
      "answers": [
        "Two",
        "Three",
        "Four",
        "Five"
      ],
      "tags": [
        "concept_balance",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "bone_hyoid"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "concept_balance",
        "modality_craniosacral_therapy",
        "bone_hyoid"
      ],
      "question": "How many primary diaphragms are commonly addressed in craniosacral therapy?",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "short_explanation": "CST often works with five diaphragms to promote full-body fascial balance.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/diaphragms",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "correct": 3,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": []
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 002",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/diaphragms",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "The tentorium cerebelli is the cranial diaphragm at the base of the skull.",
      "answers": [
        "Thoracic inlet",
        "Tentorium cerebelli",
        "Respiratory diaphragm",
        "Hyoid diaphragm"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "purpose_stress",
        "client",
        "concept_tension",
        "bone_hyoid"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "The tentorium cerebelli is a membrane that separates the cerebrum from the cerebellum. In CST, it is treated to influence cranial rhythm and relieve intracranial tension.",
      "clinical_application": "Useful for clients with migraines, TMJ issues, or head trauma.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.132Z",
      "correct": 1,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question": "Which diaphragm is located at the base of the skull?",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_membrane",
        "purpose_stress",
        "client",
        "concept_tension",
        "bone_hyoid"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",
      "category": "Modalities"
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 003",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "purpose_stress",
        "client",
        "concept_tension",
        "bone_hyoid",
        "organ_tongue"
      ],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "The hyoid diaphragm supports the tongue, voice, and swallowing.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "question": "Which diaphragm supports vocal and swallowing function in CST?",
      "clinical_application": "May relieve tension in clients with speech issues, jaw clenching, or upper neck strain.",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "purpose_stress",
        "client",
        "concept_tension",
        "bone_hyoid",
        "organ_tongue"
      ],
      "category": "Modalities",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.132Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/diaphragms",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 2,
      "long_explanation": "The hyoid diaphragm includes muscles and fascia surrounding the hyoid bone and plays a role in speech, swallowing, and upper cervical tension. It links the cranial and thoracic regions.",
      "answers": [
        "Pelvic diaphragm",
        "Tentorium cerebelli",
        "Hyoid diaphragm",
        "Respiratory diaphragm"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 004",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "keywords": [

      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.132Z",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "At the level of the hyoid bone",
        "At the base of the neck and top of the thorax",
        "Below the lungs",
        "Above the sacrum"
      ],
      "question": "What is the anatomical location of the thoracic inlet diaphragm?",
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "technique_compression",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_bones",
        "purpose_stress",
        "client",

        "bone_clavicle",
        "organ_lungs"
      ],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/diaphragms",
      "short_explanation": "The thoracic inlet is found where the neck meets the upper chest.",
      "correct": 1,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "This region includes the clavicle, first rib, and upper thoracic fascia. In CST, restrictions here can impact breathing, lymphatic flow, and upper body tension.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",
      "clinical_application": "Often released in clients with shoulder tension or postural compression."
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 005",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Releasing this diaphragm can benefit clients with anxiety, shallow breathing, or core restrictions.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_benefit",
        "client",
        "concept_anxiety",
        "bone_hyoid"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/diaphragms",
      "correct": 1,
      "tags": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_benefit",
        "client",
        "concept_anxiety",
        "bone_hyoid"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "long_explanation": "This diaphragm separates the thoracic and abdominal cavities. Its movement helps influence craniosacral rhythm and supports global fascial mobility through breath.",
      "short_explanation": "The respiratory diaphragm is the key muscle of breathing and fascial rhythm.",
      "answers": [
        "Pelvic diaphragm",
        "Respiratory diaphragm",
        "Tentorium cerebelli",
        "Hyoid diaphragm"
      ],
      "question": "Which diaphragm plays a major role in both respiration and fascial movement?",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.132Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "topic": "modalities"
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 006",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.132Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "The pelvic diaphragm supports bowel, bladder, and core function.",
      "long_explanation": "This diaphragm consists of the levator ani and coccygeus muscles. It’s vital for pelvic organ support, postural alignment, and links to the respiratory diaphragm above.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "correct": 1,
      "answers": [
        "Respiratory diaphragm",
        "Pelvic diaphragm",
        "Tentorium cerebelli",
        "Thoracic inlet"
      ],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_benefit",
        "science_organization",
        "biology_organisms",
        "organ_bladder"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "May benefit postpartum clients, or those with low back pain, pelvic instability, or pelvic trauma.",
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "Which diaphragm is closely associated with pelvic floor function?",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/diaphragms",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",

    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 007",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_craniosacral",
        "exam_terminology",
        "concept_focuses",
        "bone_hyoid"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "question": "Which of the following is *not* considered one of the five craniosacral diaphragms?",
      "long_explanation": "While the abdominal cavity is important, CST focuses on specific fascial partitions: pelvic, respiratory, thoracic inlet, hyoid, and tentorium cerebelli. The term ‘abdominal diaphragm’ is not part of this list.",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "The abdominal diaphragm is not one of the primary five in CST.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "correct": 2,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",
      "clinical_application": "Clarity in terminology helps prioritize manual therapy techniques correctly.",
      "answers": [
        "Tentorium cerebelli",
        "Thoracic inlet",
        "Abdominal diaphragm",
        "Hyoid diaphragm"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.132Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/diaphragms",
      "category": "Modalities",

        "bone_hyoid"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 008",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.132Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",
      "stats": {

        "concept_balance",

      ],
      "clinical_application": "Ensures a structured release process and reduces compensatory strain.",
      "long_explanation": "CST uses a bottom-to-top or top-to-bottom approach to restore balance through fascial chains and fluid rhythm. Sequencing respects the body's interconnected mechanics.",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "answers": [
        "To align chakras",
        "To follow respiratory order",
        "To ensure fascial continuity and fluid flow",
        "To release muscle knots"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "short_explanation": "Diaphragms are released in sequence to support bodywide balance.",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "question": "Why are craniosacral diaphragms treated sequentially?",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "modality_craniosacral",

        "purpose_healing",
        "concept_balance",

      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 009",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "By gently tractioning the sacrum, therapists can detect fascial restriction patterns and influence pelvic diaphragm mobility. It's often combined with listening techniques.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.132Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "clinical_application": "Helpful in sacroiliac issues, pelvic tension, or birth recovery.",

        "technique_compression",
        "purpose_stress",

        "bone_sacrum"
      ],
      "question": "Which technique may be used to assess or release the pelvic diaphragm?",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",
      "short_explanation": "Sacral traction can help assess and release the pelvic diaphragm.",

      ],

    {
      "id": "Craniosacral Diaphragm 010",

      "stats": {

        "correct": 0

      "answers": [
        "To improve range of motion in limbs",
        "To calm the vagus nerve directly",
        "To balance core fascial planes and fluidic movement",
        "To reduce skin adhesions"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/craniosacral/diaphragms",

        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Diaphragm releases in CST address tension and asymmetry in major fascial planes. This supports rhythmic movement, internal organ function, and cerebrospinal fluid flow.",
      "question": "What is the purpose of the ‘diaphragm release’ protocol in CST?",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_nerve",
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_organs",
        "purpose_relaxation",

        "organ_skin"

      "category": "Modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "The protocol helps normalize pressure and movement across body diaphragms.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.479Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Frequently used with clients suffering from headaches, trauma, or breathing dysfunction.",
      "topic": "modalities"
    },
    {
      "id": "DeepTissueMassage 001",
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "answers": [
        "Releasing chronic muscle tension",
        "Improving lymph drainage",
        "Skin exfoliation",
        "Relaxing energy centers"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/deep_tissue",
      "keywords": [
        "deep pressure",
        "adhesions",
        "myofascial",
        "massage therapy"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "subcategory": "Deep Tissue",
      "short_explanation": "Deep tissue massage is designed to release persistent muscular holding.",
      "question": "What is the primary goal of deep tissue massage? [Variant 1]",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body, Massage Edition",
      "clinical_application": "Ideal for athletes, chronic pain sufferers, and clients with postural imbalances.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "It targets deeper layers of muscle and fascia to break up adhesions and relieve long-standing tension, particularly from overuse or injury.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "correct": 0,
      "topic": "deep_tissue",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "tags": [
        "modality_deep_tissue",
        "muscle_tension",
        "therapeutic_pressure"
      ],
      "variant": 1,
      "synonyms": [],
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities"
    },
    {
      "id": "DeepTissueMassage 002",
      "short_explanation": "The focus is beyond superficial layers, targeting fascia and deeper musculature.",
      "variant": 2,
      "tags": [
        "connective_tissue",
        "deep_layers",
        "muscle_fascia"
      ],
      "topic": "deep_tissue",
      "source_reference": "Clinical Massage Therapy Manual",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "synonyms": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "question": "Which layer of tissue is the focus in deep tissue massage? [Variant 2]",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/deep_tissue",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "clinical_application": "Effective for myofascial release, pain relief, and ROM improvement.",
      "long_explanation": "Deep tissue techniques aim to reach connective structures and reduce chronic restrictions by working through superficial tissue first.",
      "answers": [
        "Deep muscles and connective tissue",
        "Epidermis",
        "Adipose layer",
        "Subcutaneous skin only"
      ],
      "correct": 0,
      "subcategory": "Deep Tissue",
      "keywords": [
        "fascia",
        "deep muscle",
        "massage",
        "pressure depth"
      ],
      "distractors": [],

    },
    {
      "id": "DeepTissueMassage 003",
      "correct": 0,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "subcategory": "Deep Tissue",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "topic": "deep_tissue",
      "variant": 3,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "short_explanation": "These techniques reach deeper layers by progressing slowly.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question": "Which technique is most commonly associated with deep tissue work? [Variant 3]",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "clinical_application": "Used on the erector spinae, piriformis, and quadratus lumborum.",
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "source_reference": "Massage Techniques and Applications, 6th Ed.",
      "synonyms": [],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/deep_tissue",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "The slow pace allows tissues to respond, reducing guarding and improving depth without causing injury.",

      ],

      },

      "long_explanation": "Clients with compensatory patterns, overuse injuries, or scoliosis often benefit from structural work via deep tissue techniques.",

    {
      "id": "DeepTissueMassage 005",
      "synonyms": [],
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "keywords": [
        "soreness",
        "aftercare",
        "hydration",
        "muscle healing"
      ],
      "question": "A common sensation after a deep tissue massage may be: [Variant 5]",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/deep_tissue",
      "subcategory": "Deep Tissue",
      "long_explanation": "Similar to post-exercise soreness, this is a normal response as adhesions are released and circulation improves.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "answers": [
        "Muscle soreness",
        "Euphoria",
        "Tingling in hands",
        "Allergic reaction"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Massage Aftercare Recommendations Guide",
      "short_explanation": "Some soreness may occur as deeper tissues are worked.",

      "topic": "deep_tissue",

      },
      "clinical_application": "Clients should hydrate post-session and avoid strenuous activity immediately after.",

    {
      "id": "DeepTissueMassage 006",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "deep_tissue",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "subcategory": "Deep Tissue",
      "variant": 6,
      "long_explanation": "Deep tissue techniques can disrupt fragile tissue, increasing inflammation or risking re-injury if applied too soon.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "tags": [
        "contraindication",
        "injury",
        "clinical_intake"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "synonyms": [],
      "short_explanation": "Healing tissue can be aggravated by deep pressure.",
      "clinical_application": "Always perform a detailed intake and defer deep work on acute or healing sites.",
      "source_reference": "Pathology for Massage Therapists, 2nd Ed.",
      "keywords": [
        "post-surgery",
        "healing",
        "acute",
        "contraindicated"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "question": "What is a potential contraindication for deep tissue massage? [Variant 6]",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/deep_tissue",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Recent surgery or injury",
        "Old scar tissue",
        "Calluses",
        "Mild edema"
      ],
      "correct": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "DeepTissueMassage 007",
      "clinical_application": "Often used at the beginning and end of sessions to transition between depths.",
      "tags": [
        "technique_effleurage",
        "warming_strokes",
        "massage_preparation"
      ],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/deep_tissue",
      "distractors": [],
      "question": "What massage stroke is commonly used to warm tissues before deeper pressure? [Variant 7]",
      "correct": 0,
      "variant": 7,
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "long_explanation": "Gentle, gliding strokes increase circulation and relax the superficial layers, reducing resistance before deeper techniques are introduced.",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "keywords": [

        "warm up",
        "transition",
        "circulation"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "subcategory": "Deep Tissue",
      "topic": "deep_tissue",
      "synonyms": [],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Effleurage",
        "Tapotement",
        "Petrissage",
        "Vibration"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Swedish Massage Foundations Manual",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "short_explanation": "Effleurage prepares the tissue for deeper work."
    },
    {
      "id": "DeepTissueMassage 008",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "deep_tissue",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "synonyms": [],
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "variant": 8,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "long_explanation": "Elbows and thumbs are commonly used to apply sustained pressure to trigger points and break down adhesions in connective tissue.",
      "subcategory": "Deep Tissue",
      "tags": [
        "tools_elbows",
        "pinpoint_pressure",
        "trigger_points"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Helpful for treating levator scapulae, gluteal muscles, or plantar fascia.",

        "precision",
        "release"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Thumbs or elbows",
        "Palms",
        "Fingernails",
        "Knuckles only"
      ],

      "short_explanation": "These tools provide precision and depth.",

    {

      "variant": 9,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "tags": [
        "expected_outcomes",
        "massage_results",

      ],
      "question": "Which outcome is NOT commonly expected from deep tissue massage? [Variant 9]",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "keywords": [
        "hydration",
        "myth",
        "posture",

      ],
      "synonyms": [],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Long-term dehydration",
        "Increased ROM",
        "Pain relief",
        "Postural improvement"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Deep tissue affects muscles, not hydration levels long term.",
      "subcategory": "Deep Tissue",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/deep_tissue",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "source_reference": "Massage Effects and Physiology Overview",
      "long_explanation": "While hydration is important post-session, deep tissue work does not result in chronic fluid loss. Other outcomes like improved posture or ROM are expected.",
      "clinical_application": "Encourage water intake post-treatment, but educate clients on realistic outcomes.",
      "topic": "deep_tissue",
      "category": "Massage Techniques"
    },
    {
      "id": "DeepTissueMassage 010",
      "variant": 10,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "deep_tissue",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "source_reference": "Myofascial and Trigger Point Bodywork Manual",
      "subcategory": "Deep Tissue",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/deep_tissue",
      "distractors": [],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "question": "Deep tissue massage is ideal for addressing: [Variant 10]",
      "keywords": [
        "knots",
        "restrictions",
        "adhesions",
        "pain relief"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "short_explanation": "It targets restrictions in muscle and fascia.",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "synonyms": [],
      "answers": [
        "Trigger points and adhesions",
        "Lymphatic drainage only",
        "Mild relaxation",
        "Skin exfoliation"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Trigger points (hyperirritable spots) and adhesions (stuck tissue) limit range and cause referred pain, both of which are treated with slow, deep work.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:00:54.223773Z",
      "clinical_application": "Used extensively in sports massage and injury rehab.",
      "tags": [
        "trigger_points",
        "adhesions",
        "myofascial_release"
      ],
      "correct": 0,
      "category": "Massage Techniques"
    },
    {
      "id": "ChakraMCQ 001",
      "clinical_application": "Chakra-balancing massage can incorporate intention, polarity, or crystal work to support energy flow.",
      "variant": 1,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "answers": [
        "They regulate the flow of energy through the body",
        "They support blood flow",
        "They produce lymph fluid",
        "They control muscular contractions"
      ],
      "question": "What is the primary function of chakras in holistic health systems? [Variant 1]",
      "short_explanation": "Chakras act as energetic centers that influence physical and emotional wellbeing.",
      "category": "Energy Modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "subcategory": "Chakra System",
      "synonyms": [],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "Chakras are conceptualized as spinning wheels or vortices of energy along the spine that impact vitality, mood, and organ function. Balanced chakras support holistic health.",
      "source_reference": "Eastern Body, Western Mind – Anodea Judith",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/chakras",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",

        "chakra",

        "flow"
      ],
      "topic": "chakras",

      "correct": 0,
      "tags": [

      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "ChakraMCQ 002",
      "correct": 0,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "answers": [
        "Seven",
        "Five",
        "Twelve",
        "Three"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "The classical chakra system recognizes seven main energy centers.",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/chakras",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "These seven chakras align from the base of the spine to the crown of the head and represent different aspects of consciousness and physiological processes.",
      "variant": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Practitioners may target these regions with breathwork, massage, or energetic techniques.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "source_reference": "Chakra Healing Guide, 3rd Ed.",
      "subcategory": "Chakra System",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "distractors": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "synonyms": [],
      "tags": [
        "chakra_count",
        "energy_centers",
        "eastern_theory"
      ],
      "category": "Energy Modalities",
      "question": "How many primary chakras are recognized in most Eastern systems? [Variant 2]",
      "keywords": [
        "seven",
        "energy centers",
        "chakra line"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "topic": "chakras"
    },
    {
      "id": "ChakraMCQ 003",

      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "long_explanation": "Associated with safety and grounding, the root chakra governs survival instincts, physical security, and stability.",

      "answers": [

    {
      "id": "Somatic 001",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.136Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "source_reference": "Somatic Psychology: Body-Mind Integration",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "question": "What is the primary focus of somatic therapy?",
      "tags": [
        "modality_somatic",
        "trauma_release",
        "body_awareness"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Somatic therapy emphasizes releasing trauma stored in the body.",
      "unit": "somatic therapy",
      "keywords": [
        "trauma",
        "body",
        "somatic therapy",
        "release"
      ],
      "category": "Modalities",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists incorporating somatic principles can observe shifts in breathing, muscle tension, and posture to support trauma-informed care.",
      "answers": [
        "Cognitive restructuring",
        "Analyzing dreams",
        "Releasing trauma stored in the body",
        "Increasing medication adherence"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Somatic therapy helps clients process trauma through body-centered awareness, allowing unresolved stress patterns to emerge and be released through breath, movement, or sensation tracking."
    },
    {
      "id": "Somatic 002",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "Rather than reliving trauma, Somatic Experiencing guides clients to notice internal sensations and safely release stored energy from the autonomic nervous system.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.136Z",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists can integrate this by pacing pressure and observing shifts in client tone or breath.",
      "unit": "somatic therapy",
      "question": "Which of the following is a core principle of somatic experiencing (SE)?",
      "keywords": [
        "survival energy",
        "trauma",
        "somatic experiencing",
        "release"
      ],
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "source_reference": "Peter Levine, Somatic Experiencing Textbook",
      "short_explanation": "Somatic Experiencing focuses on discharging survival energy.",
      "tags": [
        "modality_SE",
        "nervous_system_discharge",
        "trauma_integration"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "answers": [
        "Prolonged exposure to trauma triggers",
        "Reliving traumatic events",
        "Discharging excess survival energy",
        "Challenging automatic thoughts"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "Somatic 003",
      "tags": [
        "modality_somatic",

      "short_explanation": "Interoception enhances internal body awareness.",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "long_explanation": "Interoception involves noticing internal sensations like heartbeat, breath, or gut tension. It's a cornerstone of somatic approaches that foster emotional regulation.",
      "question": "What role does interoception play in somatic therapies?",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "source_reference": "Neuroscience of Interoception and Touch, 2nd Ed.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.136Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "Regulating sleep patterns",
        "Improving digestion",
        "Enhancing body awareness",
        "Correcting posture"
      ],
      "unit": "somatic therapy",
      "clinical_application": "Encouraging clients to notice internal shifts during massage supports mind-body reconnection.",
      "correct": 2
    },
    {
      "id": "Somatic 004",
      "long_explanation": "Dr. Levine’s method focuses on the biological basis of trauma and emphasizes slow, titrated release of stored autonomic energy to restore regulation.",
      "tags": [
        "founder_SE",
        "trauma_integration",
        "modality_origin"
      ],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "source_reference": "Levine, Waking the Tiger",

      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.136Z",
      "short_explanation": "Peter Levine developed Somatic Experiencing.",

      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [

        "Somatic Experiencing",
        "Bioenergetics",
        "EMDR"
      ],
      "question": "Peter Levine is most associated with which somatic therapy approach?",

        "somatic therapy"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Knowledge of SE allows therapists to recognize trauma signs (e.g., freeze/shutdown) and adjust techniques accordingly.",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",

      "difficulty": "medium",

    },
    {
      "id": "Somatic 005",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "correct": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Therapists may adapt this by encouraging clients to sense what arises when an area is touched.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.136Z",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "In Hakomi therapy, clients are guided into mindful states to observe internal responses, uncover implicit beliefs, and facilitate emotional healing.",
      "answers": [
        "Diagnose mental disorders",
        "Enhance cardiovascular fitness",
        "Access unconscious core material",
        "Practice mantra-based meditation"
      ],
      "unit": "somatic therapy",
      "question": "In Hakomi therapy, mindfulness is used primarily to:",
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Hakomi uses mindfulness to access unconscious material.",
      "tags": [

        "mindfulness",

      ],

    {

        "attempts": 0
      },
      "clinical_application": "Massage activates the parasympathetic branch by engaging safe, rhythmic touch and slow pressure.",
      "correct": 1,
      "unit": "somatic therapy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [

        "Enteric"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "distractors": [],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "short_explanation": "The parasympathetic nervous system is a key focus in somatic work.",
      "keywords": [
        "parasympathetic",
        "nervous system",
        "vagus nerve",
        "relaxation"
      ],
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_parasympathetic",
        "autonomic_regulation",
        "nervous_system_focus"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Somatic therapies often aim to calm sympathetic arousal and support parasympathetic activation—helping the body move from fight-or-flight to rest-and-digest.",
      "question": "Which nervous system branch is often targeted in somatic regulation practices?",
      "source_reference": "Polyvagal Theory in Therapy"
    },
    {
      "id": "Somatic 007",
      "short_explanation": "Somatic therapy helps restore nervous system balance.",
      "question": "A common goal of somatic therapy is to:",

      "keywords": [
        "nervous system",
        "regulation",
        "trauma release"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists can support this by slowing down and matching the client’s breathing and tension shifts.",
      "source_reference": "Body Keeps the Score – Bessel van der Kolk",

        "somatic_goal",
        "healing_trauma"

      "answers": [
        "Increase intellectual insight",
        "Promote cathartic reliving",
        "Restore nervous system balance",
        "Repress traumatic memories"
      ],
      "difficulty": "medium",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "long_explanation": "By accessing and resolving trauma stored in body memory, somatic therapy helps regulate the autonomic nervous system and restore equilibrium.",
      "unit": "somatic therapy",

    },
    {
      "id": "FrictionMassageMCQ 001",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body – Massage Edition",
      "topic": "massage_techniques",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "correct": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Used post-injury or surgery to prevent chronic restriction.",
      "answers": [
        "Break down adhesions and scar tissue",
        "Increase lymphatic drainage",
        "Lengthen muscle fibers",
        "Promote relaxation"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "variant": 1,
      "distractors": [],
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "What is the primary goal of friction massage? [Variant 1]",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/techniques/friction",
      "keywords": [
        "adhesions",
        "scar",
        "cross-fiber",
        "collagen"
      ],
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "long_explanation": "Friction massage involves targeted pressure across muscle fibers or tendons to reduce scar tissue, restore mobility, and realign collagen fibers.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "tags": [
        "technique_friction",
        "adhesion_breakdown",
        "scar_tissue"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "synonyms": [],
      "short_explanation": "Friction disrupts adhesions in soft tissue.",
      "subcategory": "Friction Massage"
    },
    {
      "id": "FrictionMassageMCQ 002",
      "short_explanation": "Cross-fiber friction is applied at right angles to fibers.",
      "variant": 2,
      "long_explanation": "This approach helps separate stuck tissues, improve alignment, and restore elasticity by disrupting abnormal collagen formation.",
      "correct": 0,
      "subcategory": "Friction Massage",
      "question": "Which direction is typically used in cross-fiber friction massage? [Variant 2]",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "keywords": [
        "cross-fiber",
        "perpendicular",
        "muscle tendon"
      ],
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "answers": [
        "Perpendicular to muscle or tendon fibers",
        "Parallel to lymph flow",
        "Circular over fascia",
        "Upward toward heart"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "source_reference": "Orthopedic Massage Therapy Handbook",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/techniques/friction",
      "clinical_application": "Effective for tendinitis or repetitive strain injuries.",
      "distractors": [],
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "topic": "massage_techniques",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "synonyms": [],

        "cross_fiber",

        "soft_tissue_alignment"
      ]
    },
    {

      "tags": [
        "contraindication",

      "difficulty_rating": 3,

      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "subcategory": "Friction Massage",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "variant": 3,
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "correct": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Always assess stage of injury before applying friction.",
      "question": "Friction massage is contraindicated in which of the following conditions? [Variant 3]",
      "distractors": [],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "synonyms": [],
      "keywords": [
        "acute",
        "redness",
        "swelling",
        "avoid"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "In cases of redness, swelling, or heat, friction can exacerbate symptoms, damage capillaries, or delay healing.",
      "answers": [
        "Acute inflammation",
        "Chronic tension",
        "Myofascial trigger points",
        "Old scar tissue"
      ],
      "topic": "massage_techniques",
      "source_reference": "Pathology for Massage Therapists – Fritz",
      "short_explanation": "Friction can worsen acute conditions."
    },
    {
      "id": "FrictionMassageMCQ 004",
      "variant": 4,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "What type of massage stroke is friction considered? [Variant 4]",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "answers": [
        "Connective tissue technique",
        "Effleurage",
        "Swedish technique",
        "Tapotement"
      ],
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "source_reference": "Massage Techniques and Clinical Applications",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "long_explanation": "Unlike gliding or kneading strokes, friction penetrates superficial layers to influence deeper structures and stimulate healing responses.",
      "synonyms": [],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "topic": "massage_techniques",

      "tags": [

        "connective_tissue",
        "deep_technique"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Friction targets fascia, tendons, and ligaments.",
      "subcategory": "Friction Massage",
      "clinical_application": "Often used in sports massage or trigger point therapy.",
      "distractors": [],
      "correct": 0,
      "keywords": [
        "deep friction",

        "tendon",
        "scar release"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "FrictionMassageMCQ 005",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/techniques/friction",
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "Friction stimulates blood flow, oxygen delivery, and fibroblast activity — which aids in remodeling damaged tissue.",

        "Enhances lymph absorption",

    {
      "id": "FrictionMassageMCQ 006",

    {
      "id": "FrictionMassageMCQ 007",
      "clinical_application": "Commonly used for tennis elbow, Achilles tendinitis, or rotator cuff adhesions.",

      "stats": {

      },
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/techniques/friction",

      "answers": [

        "Skin layers",

      "tags": [
        "tendon_therapy",
        "attachment_sites",
        "sports_injuries"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "long_explanation": "Repetitive use or trauma may cause micro-tears and adhesions in tendon sheaths or ligament edges. Friction helps remodel these tissues.",

    },

    {

      "long_explanation": "Clients may feel dull ache, warmth, or tissue sensitivity, but should not experience sharp pain or bruising.",

        "Bruising sensation"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "short_explanation": "Some therapeutic irritation is expected.",
      "question": "What sensation is normal during effective friction massage? [Variant 9]",
      "tags": [
        "treatment_sensation",
        "client_education",
        "therapeutic_response"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Always educate clients on expected sensations and monitor response.",
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "synonyms": [],
      "source_reference": "Massage Therapy Principles and Practice",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "moderate"
    },
    {
      "id": "FrictionMassageMCQ 010",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "subcategory": "Friction Massage",
      "keywords": [
        "subacute",
        "remodel",
        "scar tissue",
        "healing window"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Used between sessions of rest and strengthening exercises in rehab.",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "short_explanation": "Friction promotes proper tissue repair in later healing phases.",
      "correct": 0,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "distractors": [],
      "source_reference": "Rehabilitation Protocols for Soft Tissue Injuries",
      "tags": [
        "healing_stages",
        "subacute_phase",
        "tissue_remodeling"
      ],
      "question": "When is friction massage most appropriately used? [Variant 10]",
      "topic": "massage_techniques",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "category": "Massage Techniques",
      "answers": [
        "During the subacute or remodeling stage of healing",
        "Immediately after injury",
        "During acute swelling",
        "After vigorous exercise"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "It should be avoided during acute inflammation but is beneficial when tissues are rebuilding and require alignment.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "variant": 10,
      "synonyms": [],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/techniques/friction"
    },

    {
      "id": "Gua Sha 001",
      "answers": [
        "Muscle building",
        "Skin exfoliation",
        "Promoting circulation and relieving stagnation",
        "Removing moles"
      ],
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.137Z",
      "correct": 2,
      "variant": 1,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "synonyms": [],
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/gua_sha",
      "short_explanation": "Gua Sha promotes circulation and relieves stagnation in tissues.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "This traditional scraping technique encourages blood flow, relieves tension, and helps flush metabolic waste from the tissue.",
      "source_reference": "Traditional Chinese Medicine – Qi Flow Manual",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "question": "What is the primary purpose of Gua Sha?",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:33:48.921846Z",
      "keywords": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "modality_gua_sha",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists use it to treat myofascial pain, stagnation, or inflammation in subacute phases.",
      "tags": [
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "modality_gua_sha",
        "organ_skin"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "Gua Sha 002",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "synonyms": [],
      "variant": 2,
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/gua_sha",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "clinical_application": "Therapists integrate Gua Sha tools into deep tissue or detox sessions.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "A jade or horn scraping tool is the traditional choice for Gua Sha.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.137Z",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_gua_sha"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "tags": [
        "modality_gua_sha"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Smooth-edged tools made of jade, horn, or ceramic are used to apply friction along the skin surface.",
      "source_reference": "Gua Sha: A Traditional Technique for Modern Practice",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "correct": 1,
      "question": "What tool is commonly used in a Gua Sha treatment?",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:33:48.921846Z",
      "answers": [
        "Wooden roller",
        "Jade or horn scraping tool",
        "Metal needle",
        "Cotton pad"
      ],
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      }
    },

      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/gua_sha",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "correct": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Common in massage for reducing muscle adhesions, tension, and energetic stagnation.",
      "long_explanation": "The scraping technique uses downward or linear strokes along muscles and meridians, often producing visible redness.",
      "short_explanation": "Gua Sha involves repeated scraping of lubricated skin.",
      "distractors": [],
      "synonyms": [],
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities"
    },
    {
      "id": "Gua Sha 004",

      "long_explanation": "Sha indicates the release of heat or stagnation from the tissues and is considered a therapeutic sign.",

      "answers": [
        "Bruises",

        "Erythema",
        "Sha"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Sha refers to the red or purple marks produced by treatment.",
      "distractors": [],

      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists educate clients that sha is normal and typically fades in 1–3 days.",

    },
    {

      "variant": 10,

    {
      "id": "MeridianMCQ 001",
      "answers": [
        "Pathways through which energy (Qi) flows",
        "Tendons connecting joints",
        "Circulatory veins",
        "Nerves carrying heat"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Meridian Theory",

        "meridians",

      "variant": 1,
      "short_explanation": "Meridians guide the flow of life energy (Qi) throughout the body.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "synonyms": [],
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "topic": "meridians",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/meridians",
      "source_reference": "The Web That Has No Weaver – Ted Kaptchuk",
      "correct": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "long_explanation": "Meridians are invisible channels connecting organs and tissues, facilitating the flow of Qi (vital energy). Blockages in these pathways are believed to lead to illness or imbalance.",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "question": "In Traditional Chinese Medicine, what are meridians? [Variant 1]",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "clinical_application": "Massage therapists use meridian-based acupressure or stretching to restore energetic flow.",
      "category": "Energy Modalities",
      "last_answered_at": null
    },
    {
      "id": "MeridianMCQ 002",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "stats": {

        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "keywords": [

      ],
      "question": "How many primary meridians are there in Traditional Chinese Medicine? [Variant 2]",
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "long_explanation": "The 12 main meridians correspond to organs like the Lung, Heart, Liver, and Kidneys, forming a continuous energetic circuit in the body.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "tags": [
        "meridian_count",
        "TCM_basics",
        "organ_systems"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "12",
        "10",
        "5",
        "8"
      ],
      "topic": "meridians",
      "subcategory": "Meridian Theory",
      "distractors": [],
      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "short_explanation": "There are 12 primary meridians, each linked to an organ system.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/meridians",
      "synonyms": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "clinical_application": "Therapists may integrate meridian theory to select massage points or rhythm patterns.",
      "variant": 2,
      "category": "Energy Modalities",
      "correct": 0,
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "Essential Chinese Medicine for Bodyworkers"
    },

      },
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",

      "relevance_score": 5,

      "mblex_category": "Massage and Bodywork Modalities",
      "question": "What emotion is traditionally associated with the Liver meridian? [Variant 7]",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "short_explanation": "Liver Qi imbalance often manifests as frustration or irritability.",
      "distractors": [],
      "clinical_application": "Deep abdominal or side body work can assist Liver Qi circulation.",
      "long_explanation": "The Liver meridian stores blood and governs the smooth flow of Qi. Emotionally, it reflects anger and unexpressed frustration when stagnant.",

      "correct": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Spinal massage and craniosacral holds help stimulate Du flow.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "distractors": [],
      "subcategory": "Meridian Theory",
      "long_explanation": "Known as the 'Sea of Yang,' the Du (Governing Vessel) regulates yang energy and is a key meridian in balancing the nervous system and posture.",
      "question": "Which meridian runs along the center of the back and is also called the Governing Vessel? [Variant 8]",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "updated_at": "2025-05-26T15:13:41.941886Z",
      "topic": "meridians",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "tags": [

        "spine_energy",
        "yang_regulation"
      ],
      "source_reference": "Meridian Therapy and Manual Techniques",
      "synonyms": [],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "MeridianMCQ 009",
      "long_explanation": "Originating in Japan, Shiatsu applies rhythmic pressure along meridians to harmonize energy flow and support homeostasis.",
      "tags": [
        "shiatsu",
        "modality_meridian",
        "energy_pressure"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Therapists trained in Shiatsu use pulse, breath, and meridian palpation.",
      "correct": 0,
      "source_reference": "Zen Shiatsu – Masunaga",
      "topic": "meridians",
      "subcategory": "Meridian Theory",
      "variant": 9,
      "answers": [
        "Shiatsu",
        "Swedish",
        "Deep Tissue",
        "Reflexology"
      ],
      "synonyms": [],
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "correct": 0,

        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Shiatsu follows meridian lines using thumb and palm pressure.",

        "meridians",
        "zen bodywork"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",

      "id": "MeridianMCQ 010",

      "source_reference": "Acupressure Techniques – Practitioner’s Guide",
      "question": "What technique is used to unblock Qi in meridians during massage? [Variant 10]",
      "short_explanation": "Pressure on specific points helps free Qi.",

      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "long_explanation": "Gentle sustained touch on meridian points (acupressure) can regulate energetic flow, reduce stagnation, and relieve pain.",

    },
    {
      "id": "Techniques 001",
      "synonyms": [
        "glide",
        "stroke",
        "flowing stroke"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Effleurage is typically used to begin and end a massage session. It increases circulation, warms the tissues, and helps apply lotion or oil evenly.",

      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.481Z",
      "distractors": [],

      "id": "Techniques 002",
      "tags": [

      "synonyms": [
        "kneading",
        "compression"
      ],
      "topic": "modalities",
      "variant": 2,
      "short_explanation": "Petrissage involves kneading and lifting tissue to promote circulation.",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "question": "What is the primary purpose of petrissage in massage therapy? [Variant 2]",
      "subcategory": "Swedish Techniques",
      "correct": 2,
      "mblex_category": "Benefits and Physiological Effects of Techniques",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.481Z",
      "long_explanation": "This technique helps break up muscle adhesions, improve lymphatic flow, and reduce soreness. It is typically applied with the palms, thumbs, or fingers.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_deep_tissue",
        "technique_effleurage",
        "technique_petrissage",
        "anatomy_joint",
        "anatomy_fascia",

        "condition_soreness",
        "joint_joint"
      ],
      "category": "Techniques",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "Reduce scar tissue",
        "Loosen joint capsules",
        "Lift and knead soft tissue",
        "Stretch fascia"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Used during deep tissue work or after warming the tissues with effleurage.",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,

    {
      "id": "Techniques 004",
      "distractors": [],
      "synonyms": [
        "deep friction",
        "scar work"
      ],
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "long_explanation": "By applying deep, focused pressure in small movements, friction affects deeper tissues and is used for chronic pain, scar work, or injury rehabilitation.",
      "short_explanation": "Friction breaks down adhesions and promotes collagen remodeling.",
      "subcategory": "Friction Techniques",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Often used in conjunction with stretching or myofascial release during rehab.",

      "answers": [

      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/modalities_compared",

      "clinical_application": "Effective for chronic pain syndromes, postural imbalances, or scar tissue release.",

      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.139Z",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "long_explanation": "It targets the connective tissue network, aiming to reduce restrictions and restore mobility. Techniques are applied without oil and held until release is felt.",
      "short_explanation": "Myofascial release uses slow, sustained pressure to elongate fascia.",

      "long_explanation": "Effleurage is typically used to begin and end a massage session. It increases circulation, warms the tissues, and helps apply lotion or oil evenly.",

      ],
      "long_explanation": "This technique helps break up muscle adhesions, improve lymphatic flow, and reduce soreness. It is typically applied with the palms, thumbs, or fingers.",

      "answers": [
        "Reduce scar tissue",

      "difficulty": "easy",
      "clinical_application": "Used during deep tissue work or after warming the tissues with effleurage.",

      "clinical_application": "Helps wake up tissues before athletic activity or toward the end of a session.",
      "question": "Tapotement is typically used for what effect during a massage? [Variant 13]",
      "subcategory": "Percussive Techniques",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.139Z",
      "long_explanation": "This technique involves brisk, rhythmic tapping or hacking motions that activate the nervous system. It's often used in sports massage or energizing treatments.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/modalities_compared",
      "correct": 1,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "source_reference": "FSMTB Technique Glossary; AMTA Handbook",
      "mblex_category": "Benefits and Physiological Effects of Techniques",
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "Relaxing the nervous system",
        "Stimulating the tissues",
        "Draining lymph",
        "Stretching the fascia"
      ],
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "short_explanation": "Tapotement stimulates nerves and tissues through rhythmic percussion.",
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "variant": 13,

      ],
      "question": "Which of the following describes a typical myofascial release technique? [Variant 20]",
      "subcategory": "Myofascial Techniques",
      "answers": [
        "Fast, superficial gliding strokes",
        "Vibratory shaking and rocking",
        "Sustained pressure with tissue stretching",
        "Percussive tapotement on trigger points"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "It targets the connective tissue network, aiming to reduce restrictions and restore mobility. Techniques are applied without oil and held until release is felt.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/modalities_compared",
      "relevance_score": 4,
      "source_reference": "John Barnes Myofascial Release Manual; FSMTB Guide",
      "correct": 2,
      "category": "Techniques",
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "difficulty_rating": 3,
      "mblex_category": "Benefits and Physiological Effects of Techniques",
      "distractors": [],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.139Z",
      "short_explanation": "Myofascial release uses slow, sustained pressure to elongate fascia.",

    {
      "id": "Techniques 021",
      "long_explanation": "Effleurage is typically used to begin and end a massage session. It increases circulation, warms the tissues, and helps apply lotion or oil evenly.",

      "clinical_application": "Effleurage is ideal for initial client assessment and for calming the nervous system.",

      ],
      "short_explanation": "Effleurage involves long, gliding strokes that warm tissues.",

    {
      "id": "Techniques 022",
      "mblex_category": "Benefits and Physiological Effects of Techniques",
      "clinical_application": "Used during deep tissue work or after warming the tissues with effleurage.",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "variant": 22,

      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.481Z",
      "distractors": [],
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "long_explanation": "This technique helps break up muscle adhesions, improve lymphatic flow, and reduce soreness. It is typically applied with the palms, thumbs, or fingers.",
      "question": "What is the primary purpose of petrissage in massage therapy? [Variant 22]",
      "short_explanation": "Petrissage involves kneading and lifting tissue to promote circulation.",

      "question": "Tapotement is typically used for what effect during a massage? [Variant 23]",

      "synonyms": [
        "percussion",
        "stimulating stroke"
      ],
      "relevance_score": 4,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "incorrect": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "This technique involves brisk, rhythmic tapping or hacking motions that activate the nervous system. It's often used in sports massage or energizing treatments.",

        "Stimulating the tissues",

      "question": "Friction techniques are primarily used to: [Variant 24]",
      "subcategory": "Friction Techniques",
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "long_explanation": "By applying deep, focused pressure in small movements, friction affects deeper tissues and is used for chronic pain, scar work, or injury rehabilitation.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.139Z",
      "variant": 24,
      "synonyms": [
        "deep friction",
        "scar work"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.481Z",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Soothe the parasympathetic nervous system",
        "Break up adhesions and scar tissue",
        "Stimulate superficial blood flow",
        "Improve lymphatic drainage"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Often used in conjunction with stretching or myofascial release during rehab.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/modalities_compared",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0

      "clinical_application": "Effleurage is ideal for initial client assessment and for calming the nervous system.",

      "difficulty": "easy",
      "long_explanation": "Effleurage is typically used to begin and end a massage session. It increases circulation, warms the tissues, and helps apply lotion or oil evenly.",

      "short_explanation": "Petrissage involves kneading and lifting tissue to promote circulation.",

      "difficulty": "easy",

      },
      "long_explanation": "This technique helps break up muscle adhesions, improve lymphatic flow, and reduce soreness. It is typically applied with the palms, thumbs, or fingers.",

      "long_explanation": "This technique involves brisk, rhythmic tapping or hacking motions that activate the nervous system. It's often used in sports massage or energizing treatments.",

      "clinical_application": "Often used in conjunction with stretching or myofascial release during rehab.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/modalities_compared",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "question": "Friction techniques are primarily used to: [Variant 34]",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "short_explanation": "Friction breaks down adhesions and promotes collagen remodeling.",
      "synonyms": [
        "deep friction",
        "scar work"
      ],
      "subcategory": "Friction Techniques",
      "variant": 34,
      "relevance_score": 5,
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "long_explanation": "By applying deep, focused pressure in small movements, friction affects deeper tissues and is used for chronic pain, scar work, or injury rehabilitation.",
      "mblex_category": "Benefits and Physiological Effects of Techniques",
      "tags": [
        "modality_lymphatic_drainage",

        "condition_injuries",
        "concept_soothing"
      ],
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.481Z",
      "answers": [
        "Soothe the parasympathetic nervous system",
        "Break up adhesions and scar tissue",
        "Stimulate superficial blood flow",
        "Improve lymphatic drainage"
      ]
    },

      "relevance_score": 4,

      "source_reference": "Trail Guide to the Body; FSMTB Massage Techniques",
      "correct": 1,
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "category": "Techniques",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "short_explanation": "Effleurage involves long, gliding strokes that warm tissues.",
      "answers": [
        "Petrissage",
        "Effleurage",
        "Tapotement",
        "Friction"
      ],
      "long_explanation": "Effleurage is typically used to begin and end a massage session. It increases circulation, warms the tissues, and helps apply lotion or oil evenly.",
      "variant": 41,
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.139Z",
      "question": "Which Swedish massage stroke is best described as a gliding motion over the skin? [Variant 41]",
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "clinical_application": "Effleurage is ideal for initial client assessment and for calming the nervous system.",
      "synonyms": [
        "glide",
        "stroke",
        "flowing stroke"
      ],

        "incorrect": 0,

    {
      "id": "Techniques 042",
      "synonyms": [

        "compression"
      ],
      "distractors": [],

      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "answers": [
        "Reduce scar tissue",
        "Loosen joint capsules",
        "Lift and knead soft tissue",
        "Stretch fascia"
      ],
      "mblex_category": "Benefits and Physiological Effects of Techniques",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty_rating": 2,
      "question_type": "multiple choice",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.139Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/modalities_compared",
      "long_explanation": "This technique helps break up muscle adhesions, improve lymphatic flow, and reduce soreness. It is typically applied with the palms, thumbs, or fingers.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "category": "Techniques",
      "difficulty": "easy",
      "relevance_score": 3,
      "correct": 2,
      "variant": 42,
      "clinical_application": "Used during deep tissue work or after warming the tissues with effleurage.",

      "short_explanation": "Petrissage involves kneading and lifting tissue to promote circulation."

    {
      "id": "Techniques 043",

    {
      "id": "Techniques 044",

      "mblex_category": "Benefits and Physiological Effects of Techniques",
      "distractors": [],

      "clinical_application": "Often used in conjunction with stretching or myofascial release during rehab.",

      "short_explanation": "Friction breaks down adhesions and promotes collagen remodeling.",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,

      "question": "Friction techniques are primarily used to: [Variant 44]",
      "source_reference": "Cyriax Technique Guide; FSMTB Outline",
      "synonyms": [
        "deep friction",
        "scar work"
      ],
      "relevance_score": 4,
      "stats": {
        "attempts": 0,
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "long_explanation": "By applying deep, focused pressure in small movements, friction affects deeper tissues and is used for chronic pain, scar work, or injury rehabilitation.",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_lymphatic_drainage",

        "condition_injuries",
        "concept_soothing"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Soothe the parasympathetic nervous system",
        "Break up adhesions and scar tissue",
        "Stimulate superficial blood flow",
        "Improve lymphatic drainage"
      ]
    },

      "topic": "modalities",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "mblex_category": "Benefits and Physiological Effects of Techniques",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.139Z",

      "last_answered_at": null,

      "correct": 2,
      "answers": [

      "synonyms": [

      "long_explanation": "It targets the connective tissue network, aiming to reduce restrictions and restore mobility. Techniques are applied without oil and held until release is felt.",

    {
      "id": "Myofascial 001",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.482Z",
      "tags": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_healing",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "modality_myofascial_release",
        "concept_targeted",
        "concept_restoration"
      ],
      "short_explanation": "Myofascial release targets fascial restrictions to improve tissue mobility.",
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.140Z",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/myofascial/myofascial",
      "correct": 2,
      "topic": "modalities",
      "clinical_application": "Commonly used for chronic pain, limited range of motion, or postural imbalances.",
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "attempts": 0,
        "correct": 0
      },
      "last_answered_at": null,
      "category": "Modalities",
      "long_explanation": "The goal of myofascial massage is to release restrictions in fascia — the connective tissue that surrounds muscles — to improve movement, reduce pain, and enhance circulation.",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "keywords": [
        "modality_massage",
        "anatomy_fascia",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "anatomy_muscles",
        "anatomy_connective_tissue",
        "condition_pain",
        "purpose_healing",
        "ethics_boundary",
        "modality_myofascial_release",
        "concept_targeted",
        "concept_restoration"
      ],
      "distractors": [],
      "answers": [
        "Increase muscle size",
        "Stimulate reflex points",
        "Release fascial restrictions and restore mobility",
        "Enhance cardiovascular endurance"
      ],
      "question": "What is the primary goal of myofascial release therapy?",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0
    },
    {
      "id": "Myofascial 002",
      "long_explanation": "Myofascial release relies on holding pressure into a restriction for 90 seconds or more to allow fascial tissues to soften and release. Sudden or deep force is typically avoided.",
      "unit": "../../../src/data/questions/modalities/myofascial/myofascial",
      "short_explanation": "Myofascial techniques involve gentle, sustained pressure.",
      "clinical_application": "Effective for fibromyalgia, post-surgical recovery, and tension headaches.",
      "tags": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_fibromyalgia",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_healing",
        "modality_myofascial_release",
        "concept_recovery",
        "concept_tension",
        "population_post_surgical"
      ],
      "created_at": "2025-05-24T14:34:08.140Z",
      "difficulty": "moderate",
      "updated_at": "2025-05-24T14:54:13.482Z",
      "topic": "modalities",
      "category": "Modalities",
      "question": "What type of pressure is typically used in myofascial release?",
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "answers": [
        "Rhythmic and percussive",
        "Deep and abrupt",
        "Sustained and gentle",
        "Feather-light touch"
      ],
      "keywords": [
        "technique_pressure",
        "anatomy_tissues",
        "condition_fibromyalgia",
        "purpose_stress",
        "purpose_healing",

    {
      "id": "Myofascial 003",
      "distractors": [],
      "stats": {
        "incorrect": 0,
        "correct": 0,
        "attempts": 0
      },
      "difficulty_adjustment": 0,
      "keywords": [
