      "long_explanation": "Normal hard end feel occurs when bone contacts bone, such as in full elbow extension where the olecranon process contacts the fossa.",
      "img": "hard_endfeel_elbow.png",
      "topic": "assessment",
      "source_reference": "Salvo, Joint ROM Standards",
      "short_explanation": "A hard stop between bones defines this end feel.",
      "question": "What is a normal hard end feel?",
      "clinical_application": "Helps confirm normal joint ROM and rule out joint fusion or spurs.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "correct": 1,
      "tags": [
        "hard_endfeel",
        "bone_contact"
      ],
      "unit": "manual assessment",
      "category": "End Feel Types"
    },
    {
      "id": "End Feel 005",
      "answers": [
        "Firm resistance in wrist extension",
        "Springy resistance at hip extension",
        "Soft resistance in shoulder flexion",
        "Hard stop in elbow extension"
      ],
      "question_rank": 5,
      "clinical_application": "Alerts therapist to refer out or reassess mobility limits.",
      "unit": "manual assessment",
      "tags": [
        "abnormal_endfeel",
        "pathology_suspect"
      ],
      "topic": "assessment",
      "category": "End Feel Types",
      "short_explanation": "Abnormal when springy occurs where it shouldn't.",
      "source_reference": "Orthopedic Manual Assessment Guide",
      "keywords": [
        "springy resistance",
        "abnormal ROM"
      ],
      "correct": 1,
      "img": "abnormal_endfeel_springy.png",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "long_explanation": "A springy end feel in hip extension may indicate joint pathology like cartilage tear or internal derangement, which is not expected in that plane of motion.",
      "question": "Which of the following would be considered an abnormal end feel?"
    },
    {
      "id": "End Feel 006",
      "question_rank": 6,
      "long_explanation": "Characterized by the client halting motion due to pain before a physical barrier is felt, often indicating acute inflammation, abscess, or fear of movement.",
      "answers": [
        "Hard",
        "Firm",
        "Empty",
        "Soft"
      ],
      "correct": 2,
      "source_reference": "FSMTB, Salvo, Clinical Reasoning Text",
      "tags": [
        "empty_endfeel",
        "pain_guarding",
        "red_flag"
      ],
      "topic": "assessment",
      "keywords": [
        "no resistance",
        "pain limited",
        "empty"
      ],
      "clinical_application": "Seen in acute bursitis, fractures, or post-injury anxiety.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "unit": "manual assessment",
      "short_explanation": "Empty end feels are limited by pain, not resistance.",
      "question": "Which end feel occurs when there is pain with no mechanical resistance?",
      "img": "empty_endfeel_pain.png",
      "category": "End Feel Types"
    },
    {
      "id": "End Feel 007",
      "source_reference": "Trail Guide, Joint Dysfunction Reference",
      "long_explanation": "Abnormal springy end feel results from loose body obstruction (e.g., torn cartilage), commonly felt in the knee with meniscal tears.",
      "short_explanation": "Internal joint damage may cause a springy block.",
      "keywords": [
        "springy block",
        "joint derangement"
      ],
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "topic": "assessment",
      "img": "springy_endfeel_meniscus.png",
      "question_rank": 7,
      "correct": 2,
      "clinical_application": "Suggests referral to orthopedics or imaging.",
      "question": "What might a springy abnormal end feel indicate?",
      "unit": "manual assessment",
      "tags": [
        "joint_derangement",
        "meniscus_injury"
      ],
      "answers": [
        "Muscle strain",
        "Joint fusion",
        "Internal derangement such as a torn meniscus",
        "Tendonitis"
      ],
      "category": "End Feel Types"
    },
    {
      "id": "End Feel 008",
      "question_rank": 8,
      "long_explanation": "Boggy end feel is an abnormal soft quality, caused by fluid buildup in the joint capsule, typical in cases of synovitis or trauma.",

      "answers": [

        "Tendon strain"

      "unit": "manual assessment",

      "img": "boggy_endfeel_inflamed_joint.png",
      "correct": 0,
      "clinical_application": "Seen in acute joint swelling or synovial inflammation.",
      "keywords": [

      "answers": [

        "Firm",

      "question": "Which end feel is typically associated with a capsular stretch?",
      "unit": "manual assessment",
      "source_reference": "FSMTB, Salvo, Range of Motion Standards",
      "long_explanation": "Firm end feel is typical when the joint capsule or ligament limits motion, felt in normal ROM like shoulder external rotation."

      "question": "What is the primary purpose of grounding techniques in massage therapy?",
      "question_rank": 1,
      "short_explanation": "Grounding helps clients feel safe and centered.",
      "long_explanation": "Grounding techniques anchor a client's awareness to the present moment, promoting relaxation, trust, and nervous system regulation during massage.",

    },

      "question": "What environmental feature enhances grounding in the massage room?",

      "short_explanation": "Effleurage is a smooth, gliding technique used to warm up the tissue and spread lubricant.",
      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "long_explanation": "Effleurage consists of long, flowing strokes typically performed with the palms, fingers, or forearms. It's often the first and last technique in a session to promote relaxation, increase circulation, and prepare the muscles for deeper work."
    },

      "id": "Terminology 002",

        "kneading",

      "short_explanation": "Petrissage involves lifting, rolling, and kneading the muscles to improve deeper circulation.",
      "long_explanation": "This technique manipulates underlying muscles using a rhythmic compression. It loosens adhesions, improves muscle tone, and helps mobilize deeper tissues.",
      "question_rank": 2,

        "modality_swedish",
        "deep_tissue"

      "difficulty": "intermediate",
      "topic": "terminology",
      "source_reference": "FSMTB Massage Therapy Outline, Salvo, Trail Guide to the Body",

        "Sustained compression",

      "short_explanation": "Tapotement is a rhythmic tapping technique used to stimulate nerves and circulation.",

      "correct": 1,
      "question_rank": 5,
      "clinical_application": "Excellent in sports massage and chair massage to address tension without oils. Encourages blood flow and neuromuscular relaxation.",
      "source_reference": "FSMTB Massage Therapy Outline, Salvo, Trail Guide to the Body",

      "long_explanation": "Shearing techniques involve dragging or shifting layers of fascia or muscle to mobilize restrictions and increase tissue glide.",

      ],

      "long_explanation": "It encompasses a broad range of manual techniques like gliding, kneading, and compression. Massage therapy aims to promote health, relaxation, and injury recovery.",
      "clinical_application": "Massage is used to reduce stress, manage pain, rehabilitate injuries, and enhance general wellness in clients of all ages.",

      "question": "What does the term 'massage' generally refer to?",

      "short_explanation": "Massage is hands-on therapy to affect muscles and other soft tissue.",
